# TITLE XXVII.

## CONVEYANCES.

### 1862.
*February 25.*

**1** AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to cause writings to be made in the English language."

1 Stanton's R. S. 286.
How deeds and powers of attorney in foreign languages may be admitted to record.

**SEC. I.** That the act, approved March 10, 1856, entitled "an act to cause writings to be made in the English language," be, and is hereby, so amended as that deeds and powers of attorney, written in any other language than the English, shall be admitted to record as deeds and powers of attorney are now admitted to record: *Provided*, That the person offering the same for record shall produce a correct English translation thereof; and the translation shall be filed with the deed or power of attorney, and, together with the said deed or power of attorney, be recorded in the deed book or power of attorney book.

**SEC. II.** This act shall apply to deeds and powers of attorney heretofore executed, and shall take effect from its passage.

**SEC. II.** This act shall be in force from its passage

### I. *Of wife's choses in action, personalty and slaves.*

1. *What law governs right of husband to wife's movables.*—The right of the husband to the movables, including slaves, owned by the wife at the time of the marriage, is determined by the law of the place where they were married, although they may afterward remove with the property to another domicile. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352.

as they (the council) should designate: *Held*—that this did not authorize a tax on *choses in action*. *City of Covington v. Powell*, 2 Met. 226.

5. *To create lien for taxes*—*what requisite*.—To enable a municipal corporation to enforce a lien for taxes on the property of a citizen, there must be a strict compliance with the prerequisites of the statute conferring the lien. *City of Louisville v. Bank of Kentucky*, 3 Met. 148.

6. *Billiards, how licensed*.—By section three of the act of February 9, 1858, the municipal authorities of cities and towns have the exclusive right to license billiard tables within their limits, to prescribe the rates to be paid, and the number of tables to be set up. And if the grantee be licensed to keep more than one table, still he is only bound under the proviso of the above section to pay to the clerk of the county court $100. *Metz v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 14.

# TITLE CIX.

## ROADS AND PASSWAYS.

### 1860.
*March 2.*

*[Road running partly in two counties. Jurisdiction.]*

**1** AN ACT regarding the jurisdiction of county courts to open roads in certain cases.

Where new road begins and ends in same county—county or circuit of such county to have jurisdiction to establish, although proposed route may run through part of another county.

**SEC. I.** That whenever the route of a proposed new road commences in one county, and runs partly through another county, and finally terminates in that county in which it commenced, then, and in all such cases, the county court, or circuit court, of that county in which said road begins and terminates, shall have the right to appoint viewers to view the entire route in both counties; and shall have jurisdiction to open and establish the same, as now provided by law in cases where the road is wholly within the limits of one county.

**SEC. II.** This act to be in force from its passage.

**Trials.**
SEC. VII. When the issues of fact in cases are made up for trial, the clerk shall put the cases on the docket of the next trial term, in proper order (cases in which several trials are allowed by the Civil Code, and judgments against several defendants may, if ready for trial as to some, and not ready against other, proceed on both the trial and the appearance docket). And at said trial terms issues of fact shall be tried, verdicts rendered, motions for new trial, or in arrest of judgment, made and disposed of, and final judgments on the pleadings may also be allowed, and the pleadings may be amended to conform them to the proof, or otherwise, upon just terms, in the discretion of the court. All questions of law undisposed of, for want of time, or under advisement, shall be continued to the next appearance term. The appeals from justices' and other inferior courts shall be docketed for the last days of the docket; and the first days of the trial docket shall be set aside for county cases, according to the present law concerning the Jefferson circuit court. Postponements of trials shall be to the next trial term, or, if the business of the court allow it, to a later day of the same term.

**Appeals.**
**Trial of appeals.**
SEC. VIII. Appeals from justices' and other inferior courts shall go to the trial terms in the first instance, without regard to the intervening appearance terms, and be otherwise regulated by the laws regulating appeals to circuit courts; and when a defendant appeals from a judgment for money or personal property, he may demand judgment upon the two returns of not found, if the plaintiff does not appear at the second term.

**Mandamus and writs of prohibition.**
SEC. IX. Actions on mandamus and writs of prohibition may be docketed for, or continued to either class of terms; and all matters as to which the contrary is not above provided, which are within the power of said court, may be done at either term; and the rendering of any judgment, or allowing of any step, or making an order at a term of the wrong class, shall never render a judgment void, nor shall it be error unless it be excepted to at the proper time, and unless it moreover be of substantial prejudice to the party complaining.

**Terms, etc.**
SEC. X. The terms of either class shall count as terms within the meaning of all provisions of the Civil Code requiring anything to be done before the end of a term, or within a certain number of terms; except that motions to correct the clerical

SEC. III. No divorce shall be granted under this act, except in cases where the lunacy or unsoundness of mind was the result of intemperance (or of an hereditary taint of insanity, which was concealed from the other party at the time of marriage), nor until the questions of fact arising under this act have been tried by a jury, as in cases of trials of issues of fact in equity cases: *Provided*, That in the event the jury returns an affirmative verdict, the divorce shall not be granted until and unless the chancellor shall enter upon the record that he has heard all the testimony, and that in his judgment the verdict is sustained by the evidence.

SEC. IV. Where the alleged lunatic, or person of unsound mind, is in an asylum established by the laws of this state, the case may be tried in the circuit or chancery court of the county where the asylum is located.

SEC. V. This act shall be in force from its passage.

1 AN ACT in relation to the duties of county court clerks.

Duty of county court clerks to make and furnish poll-books.

SEC. I. That it shall be the duty of the several county court clerks of this commonwealth to make out and furnish to each clerk of the election, in their respective counties, a poll, at each election, for which they shall be allowed a reasonable compensation, to be allowed by the court of claims, and paid out of the county levy.

SEC. II. This act shall be in force from its passage.

All instruments that have been so acknowledged or proven before any clerk, and produced to, and recorded by, his successors, are hereby declared legally recorded, and such instruments and official copies therefrom, shall, to all intents and purposes, be taken and held precisely as if they had been lodged for record with the clerk before whom the acknowledgment or proof was made.

NOTE.—The section of the Revised Statutes amended by the above act will, as amended, read as follows:

"Where the office of any county court clerk has been, or may hereafter be vacated, leaving therein any instruments of writing unrecorded, which, from an official indorsement thereon, shall appear to have been acknowledged or proved ready for record, or that have been acknowledged or proved before another officer, and certified according to law, and lodged in his office for record, *or shall be produced to his successor for record*, the successor of such clerk shall record the same, making his certificate conform to the facts."

*[Death of parties after judicial sale. Revivor unnecessary before conveyance.]*

AN ACT in relation to conveyances by commissioners.

SEC. I. That where, by the judgment of any of the courts of this commonwealth having jurisdiction thereof, a conveyance of real estate is ordered to be made by a commissioner or commissioners, and before the conveyance is perfected any of the parties die, it shall not be necessary to revive the action, but the conveyance, in pursuance of the judgment, shall be effectual to pass the title, notwithstanding the death of any of the parties.

SEC. II. That all conveyances heretofore made, in which the defect, supplied by the first section of this act exists, be, and they are hereby, made valid and effectual in law, for all pur-

8
LXXVI.] HUSBAND AND WIFE. 263

2. *When her choses in action subject to his debts.*—A chose in action accruing to the wife during coverture, which the husband can reduce into immediate possession, can be subjected to his debts in a proceeding against them as non-residents. *Tobin v. Dixon*, 2 Met. 422.

3. *Husband's right to deceased wife's slaves.*—By the Revised Statutes, and the previous law of 1846, "further to protect the rights of married women," the husband who survives the wife, is only entitled to a life estate in her slaves. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352; *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.

4. *Where slaves descended before law of 1846.*—Where the marriage took place in 1830, the husband, upon the death of the wife in 1857, was entitled to slaves by survivorship or as administrator, which had descended to her in 1829. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

5. *Right to deceased wife's choses in action.*—Husband surviving his wife is entitled to her choses in action, which right, on his decease, will pass to his executor. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

6. *Same.*—The wife having a vested right to a legacy not due at her decease, her husband is equitably entitled thereto, and may recover it when due. *Duncan v. Prentice*, 4 Met. 216.

7. *Sale of wife's slaves—how effected.*—The title to the wife's slaves can only be passed in the manner in which her lands may be sold and conveyed; and until so disposed of, her right to them is complete, subject only to the marital rights of her husband. *Tharp v. Tharp*, 3 Met. 372.

8. *Choses barred by husband's warranty.*—Where the right of the wife has been converted into a demand for money, to which the husband would be entitled when recovered, such demand will be barred by his covenant of warranty. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

9. *Husband's debt can not be set off against wife's rent.*—By the Revised Statutes it is declared that the rent and hire of the wife's real estate and slaves shall not be liable for any debt of the husband, nor shall his contingent right of curtesy or his right to the use or rent of his wife's estate be subjected to the payment of his debts during her life: *Held*—in an action by husband and wife to recover rent, for her property, that the tenant could not set off demands due him from the husband against the rent. *Green v. Carson*, 4 Met. 77.

## II. Necessaries. Selling and incumbering wife's ordinary estate.

10. *Necessaries, what are.*—The word necessaries as used in section one, article two, chapter forty-seven, Revised Statutes, should be liberally construed, and is held to embrace such things as the family, including the husband, considering its social position and the estate of the wife, ought to have and enjoy. *Pell v. Cole*, 2 Met. 252.

11. *Same.*—The wife owned eighty acres of land worth $1,500, with a house on it worth $1,275. This was all she had, and her husband was insolvent: *Held*—that a note given by her and her husband in payment of an addition put up to her house, was not for necessaries within the meaning of the law, and was not a lien on her property. *Ib.*

## 1860.
*March 3.*

*[Persons owning stone quarries near Ohio river may have road thereto.]*

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act for the benefit of persons holding lands lying back of other lands, in the vicinity of any of the navigable streams of this state," approved December 6, 1851.

SEC. I. That the provisions of the act, entitled "an act for the benefit of persons holding lands lying back of other lands, in the vicinity of any of the navigable streams of this state," approved December 6, 1851, be so amended as to embrace any person or persons owning or occupying a stone quarry within three miles of the Ohio river, or any navigable stream in this state.
2 Stanton's R. S. 296.

SEC. II. This act shall take effect from its passage

## 1861.
*December 10.*

*[Fines from delinquents to be paid to surveyor of highways. How expended.]*

AN ACT to amend the twenty-third section of chapter eighty-four, of Revised Statutes, concerning roads and passways.

SEC. I. That the fines that may be collected hereafter from delinquents in working on roads in this commonwealth, shall be paid to the surveyor of roads in the precinct in which the funds are collected, to be expended in the improvement of roads under his charge; and each surveyor of roads shall account to his successor in office for the funds so received by him, and pay over any unexpended balance remaining in his hands; and each surveyor shall report annually to the court of claims of his county the amount of fines received by him, if any, and the disposition thereof; and upon failure to do so, he shall be subject to a fine not exceeding ten dollars, to be recovered upon presentment of a grand jury.
How fines for failure to work on roads to be applied and accounted for.
Penalty.

SEC. II. The fine shall be paid by the person entitled to the services of slaves at the time they are required to work on a road, anything in said section to the contrary notwithstanding.

**SEC. XI.** In all actions brought originally in the Jefferson court of common pleas, or removed into it by appeal, in which the plaintiff claims damages for any trespass, libel, slander, illegal attachment or distress, breach of marriage promise, or on a bond given to obtain a provisional remedy in an action, and does, on such claim, recover no more than five dollars, the said court may, in its discretion, refuse to award the plaintiff the whole or any part of the costs.

**SEC. XII.** Said court shall have full power to make all proper rules to facilitate its business, not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of this state; and its judgments and final orders shall be subject to appeals, in like manner as those of circuit courts, and not otherwise.

**SEC. XIII.** In the summoning and selection of juries, and in the trial by jury, and the payment of jurors, in said court, the same shall be governed by the laws now in force, and which are applicable to circuit courts.

**SEC. XIV.** All laws or parts of laws within the purview of this act, and conflicting therewith, are repealed, from and after the taking effect of the parts of this act conflicting with them; and the provisions of this act shall take effect at the times indicated in the various sections.

AN ACT to amend section 8, article 7, chapter 32, title "Elections," of the Revised Statutes.

## 1866.
*January 10.*

AN ACT to amend sub-division 6 of section 670 of the Civil Code of Practice, title "Evidence.

**SEC. I.** That sub-division 6 of section 670 of the Civil Code of Practice be, and the same is hereby, so amended as to allow the plaintiff's or owner's wife, in an action to recover the value of lost baggage against common carriers, inn-keepers, or wrong-doers, to testify as to the contents and value thereof, in the same manner and to the same extent that the husband is now allowed to testify by law.

Actions for lost baggage; plaintiff's wife made a competent witness.

**SEC. II.** This act shall take effect from its passage.

CONVEYANCES.

I. BY MARRIED WOMEN.
II. WHAT INSTRUMENTS RECORDABLE.
III. REGISTRATION—DEPOSIT FOR RECORD.
IV. CONSTRUCTION AND EFFECT.
V. VOLUNTARY CONVEYANCE.

I. *By married women.*

1. *Her power to convey under the statutes construed.*—Neither the law
of 1846 for the protection of married women, nor the substantial re-enact-
ment of its provisions as contained in the Revised Statutes, affect the law
then and now in force, regulating conveyances by husband and wife. *Smith*
*v. Wilson*, 2 Met. 235.

2. *The same.*—The power of alienation vested in married women, by
chapter twenty-four, section twenty, of the Revised Statutes, is only re-
stricted by section seventeen, article four, of the title Husband and Wife,
which prohibits a married woman from aliening or incumbering her *sep-
arate estate* under certain circumstances. *Ib.*

II. *What instruments recordable.*

3. *Bonds and evidences of equitable rights not recordable.*—The laws
now and formerly in force, regulating the registration of conveyances, re-
late to conveyances of the *legal* title only, and contain no provisions for the
registration of bonds and other written evidences of mere equitable titles.
*Conn v. Simms*, 3 Met. 391.

4. *Choses in action—claims for debt.*—The statute which requires the
recording of mortgages, etc., of real and personal estate, does not embrace
choses in action and claims for debt. *Newby v. Hill*, 2 Met. 530.

III. *Deposit for record. Registration.*

5. *Payment of tax essential to a legal deposit for record.*—Though a
deed be acknowledged and deposited in the clerk's office for record, it is not
constructive notice to purchaser and creditors until the tax is paid thereon.
*Phillips v. Clark*, 4 Met. 348.

6. *Tax not paid—deed not notice though deposited.*—Such a deed will
not defeat a mechanic's lien created upon the premises after it was lodged
for record, and before the tax was paid. *Ib.*

Digitized by Google
264 HUSBAND AND WIFE. [TITLE

12. *Mechanic's lien.*—The husband can not create a mechanic's lien upon the estate of his wife. *Ib.*

13. *Mortgage to secure husband's debt.*—A mortgage executed by husband and wife in 1853, on wife's lands to secure a debt owed by him, was valid and binding on her under the law then in force. *Smith v. Wilson*, 2 Me. 235; *Johnston v. Ferguson*, 2 Met. 503.

14. *Wife's power of alienation.*—The comprehensive power of alienation conferred on married women by the twentieth section of the chapter of the Revised Statutes on conveyances, is only restricted by the seventeenth section, article four, title HUSBAND AND WIFE, prohibiting married women under certain circumstances from aliening or incumbering her separate estate. *Smith v. Wilson*, 2 Met. 235.

15. *Lumber furnished to repair her property.*—Where lumber was furnished to repair fences and buildings and erect out houses on the wife's land on which she resided with her husband and family, the repairs and out houses being necessary to the comfortable and proper enjoyment of the property: *Held*—that a note executed by husband and wife for such lumber, constituted a lien upon her general estate, including land and slaves. *Marshall v. Miller*, 3 Met. 333.

16. *Intention in executing note.*—That the intention of the wife in executing a note for necessaries was to charge her separate, and not her general estate, of which intention the payee was apprised when he took the note, will not prevent her general estate from being held to its payment. *Ib.*

17. *Wife's power to contract.*—In 1843 the wife had no power to make contracts respecting any of her property, except real estate. *Ashby v. Woolfork*, 3 Met. 540.

### III. *Separate estate*—what is, and how created.

18. *What language creates separate estate.*—To create a separate estate in the wife, there must be a manifest intention evinced by the language creating the estate, that the wife shall have the exclusive property in the thing conveyed. Technical terms are not indispensable. *Johnston v. Ferguson*, 2 Met. 503; *Gains v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

19. *Same.*—This language, "To have and to hold the said tract or parcel of land with its appurtenances, unto the said party of the second part (the wife), her heirs and assigns, to *their* only proper use, benefit, and behoof forever," does not create a separate estate, but a fee simple. *Ib.*

20. *Trustees not necessary.*—The intervention of a trustee is not indispensably necessary to the creation of a separate estate, though a circumstance tending strongly to evince an intention to invest the wife with the exclusive use of the property conveyed. *Ib.*

21. *Trust estate not necessarily separate.*—The mere creation of a trust estate for the benefit of the wife does not of itself create a separate estate; words of exclusion must be used. *Johnson v. Ferguson*, 2 Met. 503; *Toombs v. Stone*, *Ib.* 520.

22. *May be created by parol.*—Where the title of property will pass without writing, a separate estate may be created by parol; provided it is clearly

**Commiss'r's fee.**
**SEC. II.** That for all reports of services rendered by the commissioner under orders of reference, his fee shall be stated at the foot of the report, and when not objected to by the parties, shall be allowed by the court; and when objected to by either party, the court shall hear and determine the objections, and shall allow a reasonable compensation.

**Term of office of commiss'ner and receiver.**
**SEC. III.** That the present commissioner and receiver of said court shall continue in office during the term for which the present chancellor thereof has been elected, subject to removal for cause, and shall perform the duties now required of him by the law and the rules of said court; he shall deposit the funds of said court in any chartered bank doing a regular banking business in the city of Louisville, or in any national bank located in said city; and said commissioner and receiver may require of said depository bond or covenant, payable to him in the penalty of $100,000, conditioned to pay all moneys deposited by him upon and under any check that he may draw in obedience to the order of said court; and said commissioner and receiver, or any party interested, shall have the right of action on said bond, which shall be returned to and filed with the clerk of said court.

**Commiss'ner and receiver to report bank of deposit.**
**SEC. IV.** That the commissioner and receiver shall state what bank is the depository of the court upon the record of said court; and if at any time he shall change the same, the court shall make the necessary order on the former depository for the transfer of the fund on hand in said bank.

**Commiss'ner and receiver — how appointed.**
**SEC. V.** That upon the election and qualification of each succeeding judge of said court, he shall appoint a commissioner and receiver thereof for his said term, who shall be first recommended to him in writing, and signed by a majority of the resident practicing attorneys of said court, and none other shall be deemed qualified for said office; said commissioner and receiver shall, before he enters upon the duties of his said office, take the oaths now prescribed by law, and shall give bond with two or more good securities, in the penalty and with the condition as now prescribed by law and the rules of said court, and shall hold said office until his successor is appointed and qualified; and shall be liable to impeachment and dismissal for misfeasance or malfeasance in office, or for using for individual purposes the funds in said court, to be tried and convicted in the manner as clerks of courts are tried.

2,500 copies to be purchased.
rected to purchase of the publishers, for the use of this commonwealth, two thousand five hundred copies of the supplement to the Revised Statutes of Kentucky, prepared by Harvey Myers, embracing the general laws enacted by the Legislature of Kentucky between the 4th day of December, 1859, and the 4th day of June, 1865, at the price of four dollars and twenty-five cents per copy: *Provided*, Said copies shall embrace the general laws passed at the present session of the legislature, properly arranged in an appendix to said books with an index.

What to embrace
How delivered and paid for.
SEC. II. That as said books are delivered to said secretary at his office in Frankfort, he shall examine them; and if they are well bound and in good order, he shall certify to the auditor of public accounts the number delivered, and the said auditor is hereby required to issue his warrant upon the treasurer for the price of the same.

How distributed.
SEC. III. That it shall be the duty of the secretary of state, when the acts of the present general assembly shall be distributed, to cause to be delivered to each of the officers of this commonwealth, now entitled by law to receive copies of the acts of the legislature, one copy of said work, to be by them held as public property, and delivered to their successors in office, in like manner as they are required to hold and deliver the said acts of the general assembly.

Who entitled to copies.

7. *Unrecorded assignment—notice.*—An unrecorded assignment in behalf of creditors who have notice thereof before the acquisition of the legal title, is not avoided by the eleventh section of chapter twenty-four of the Revised Statutes. *Ward v. Crotty*, 4 Met. 59.

IV. *Construction and effect.*

8. *Prima facie validity of deed determined by laws of Kentucky.*—In the absence of an averment that a deed was made in another state, and proof of what the law of that state is, the validity of the deed must be tested by the law of Kentucky. *Hurdt v. Courtenay*, 4 Met. 139.

9. *Void conveyance—grantee not bound to complete transaction.*—A sale and conveyance of real estate by one executor was void, because the co-executor did not join therein: *Held*—that the purchaser was not bound by the transaction, and could not be compelled to accept the deed of the person to whom the land and its proceeds were devised. *Wells v. Lewis*, 4 Met. 269.

10. *What land passes by.*—A deed passes the title to all the land within the boundaries designated therein, although they may contain more than the quantity mentioned in the deed. *Jennings v. Monks*, 4 Met. 103.

11. *Title to whole boundary passes though greater than intended.*—A conveyance by a commissioner of land sold under judgment of court, described the premises by bounds, and as containing one hundred and seventy-four acres. It turned out that the tract sold and described in the deed contained two hundred and fourteen acres: *Held*—that the deed passed the title to the whole tract. *Ib.*

12. *The same—purchaser without notice.*—In the foregoing case, the purchaser of the land subsequently sold it to a third party who had no notice of the mistake as to the quantity and derived no benefit from it, and who paid for all the land: *Held*—that he could not be required to surrender the surplus or to pay for it. *Ib.*

13. *The word purchaser—construction of.*—The word purchaser has a well-defined technical meaning, and embraces every holder of the legal title to real or personal property acquired by deed. A mortgagee both before and after forfeiture, is a purchaser. *Halbert v. McCulloch*, 3 Met. 456.

V. *Voluntary conveyances.*

14. *Good against purchaser with notice.*—A voluntary conveyance is good against a subsequent purchaser with actual notice of its existence. *Earle v. Couch*, 3 Met. 450.

15. *Not always void as to subsequent creditors.*—Though a conveyance be voluntary, it is not on that account *alone* void as to subsequent creditors. *Hurdt v. Courtenay*, 4 Met. 140.

16. *Statute in favor of creditors to be liberally construed.*—The statutes to protect creditors against fraudulent conveyances, should be liberally con-

23. *Declarations of husband*.—The declarations of a husband that a slave belonged to his wife, which had been given to her previous to the act of 1846, do not even tend to prove that the wife has a separate estate in the slaves. *Ib.*

24. *Parol gift*—*intention of donor*.—Donor testifies that a slave was given to his married daughter as her own property, and that he intended the slave to be for her exclusive use and benefit. This did not create a separate estate. The *intention* of the donor can not create a separate estate, unless that intention be clearly and unequivocally *expressed* at the time of the gift. *Ib.*

25. *Language held to create separate estate*.—A deed provides that a trustee shall hold the property for the use and benefit and support of the wife and her children, and shall let her and her children have, use, and enjoy every part and parcel of the property, which is to be controled by her for her comfort and support, and for the support and education of her children: *Held*—to be a separate estate. *Toombs v. Stone*, 2 Met. 520.

26. *Trust estate and separate estate*.—The provisions of the Revised Statutes, concerning estates held in trust, have no application to separate estates which are something more than mere trust estates, and which can only belong to a married woman, though they may be created previous to her marriage. *Ib.*

27. *Separate estate not liable for necessaries*.—The provision of the Revised Statutes which renders the wife's real estate and slaves liable for necessaries, agreed to be paid for by writing signed by her and her husband has no application to her separate estate. *Ib.*

28. *Property conveyed on separation is separate estate*.—Where property is conveyed by a husband to a trustee for the benefit of his wife, and with a view to a separation between them, it is clear that a separate use was intended, and the trustees hold the property for her separate use. *Gaines v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

29. *What necessary to create separate estate*.—A stranger's conveyance of property, or covenant to pay money to a married woman or to a trustee for her, must, in order to create a separate estate, contain words indicating that intention. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 85.

30. *What necessary for husband to create separate estate*.—Where a wife joins with her husband in a sale of her property, in order to give him the proceeds, in consideration of which he gives her his notes, it is not necessary that the notes should specify that they are for her separate use in order to create a separate estate in her. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 85; *Gains v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

32. *Same*.—Since the Revised Satutes took effect, a married woman has no power to charge or incumber or dispose of her separate estate for any purpose whatever, in any other mode than that prescribed by the statute, namely, by order of a court of equity. *Stacker v. Whitlock*, 3 Met. 244; *Marshall v. Miller*, 3 Met. 333.

33. *Separate estate not to be incumbered*.—A married woman, the owner of land, not her separate estate, joined in a conveyance thereof with her husband, the conveyance specifying that the proceeds should be invested in other property for her separate use. Slaves were purchased with the proceeds and conveyed to a trustee for her separate use. The plaintiffs sold goods to her on the faith and credit of this separate estate: *Held*—that it could not be subjected to the payment of their debt. *Hanly v. Downing*, 4 Met. 96.

34. *What separate estate can not be incumbered*.—The provisions of chapter forty-seven, article four, section seventeen, prohibiting the sale or conveyance of separate estate, except by order of a court of equity, applies only where the estate has been devised or conveyed to her separate use. It does not apply to her general estate which, by an antenuptial agreement with the husband, she is to hold to her separate use. *Bryan v. Bohanan*, MSS. opin., Dec., 1856; *Hanly v. Downing*, 4 Met. 96.

35. *Deed authorizing woman to convey separate estate*.—Where a deed creating a separate estate expressed that the woman might sell, devise, and control the property so conveyed: *Held*—nevertheless, that the deed was controlled by the statute prohibiting the sale, by married women, of their separate estate, and that she had no power to dispose of it. (*Stuart v. Wilder*, 17 B. Mon. 57); *Hanly v. Downing*, 4 Met. 96.

36. *Trustee of separate estate may incumber it*.—The statute which prohibits a married woman from creating any charge or incumbrance on her separate estate, does not affect the rights of their trustees with reference to such estates. *Lewis v. Harris*, 4 Met. 356.

37. *Lien on separate estate for purchase money*.—Property was conveyed to a trustee for the separate use of a married woman, and for a part of the purchase money the trustee, the woman, and her surety executed a note to the vendor: *Held*—that the trustee did not intend to bind himself individually, but to create a charge upon the property (personal) conveyed, and that it was competent for him to do so. *Ib*.

1.  *Right of way by necessity.*—To enable a purchaser to claim a right of way through the lands of his vendor, he must show that such a right is indispensably necessary to the enjoyment of the land which has been conveyed. *Hall v. McLeod*, 2 Met. 98.
2.  *Permissive use of passway gives no right.*—Where a man has a passway through his own land for his own use, the mere permissive use of it by other persons even for a half century, confers no right on them to its enjoyment. *Ib.*
3.  *When grant presumed.*—To create a presumption of a grant of the right of way, the use must have been enjoyed under circumstances which indicate either that it was established for the benefit of the claimant, or that he has claimed its use as a right, and that it has not been regarded by the parties as a mere privilege revokable at the pleasure of the owner of the soil. *Ib.*
4.  *Prescription—right of way by.*—By the common law the right to an incorporeal hereditament may be acquired by *prescription* which is founded upon an uninterrupted enjoyment time out of mind, or for such a length of time that the memory of man runneth not to the contrary. *Ib.*
5.  *Twenty years' enjoyment.*—The enjoyment of an incorporeal hereditament twenty years furnishes only a *prima facie* presumption of right, which may be rebutted by showing that the enjoyment was a mere *favor*, and not accompanied with any claim or admission of right. *Ib.*
    *Specific execution.*—A general promise by one to establish a passway over his land where no specific contract was made by him with any one, can not be specifically enforced. *Ib.*
6.  *Agreements for right of way—statute of frauds.*—A right of way is an interest in real estate, and a verbal contract that it shall be granted can not be enforced. *Ib.*
    *Use under verbal promise.*—Where a right of way was opened for the benefit of a party in pursuance of a verbal promise to that effect by the owner of the soil, its use for twenty years would confer an absolute right to the easement. *Ib.*
7.  *Dedication.*—A private passway can not be created by dedication, nor can the establishment of a private passway be construed to be a dedication to public use. *Ib.*

39. *Contracts upon separation, for support of wife.*—A contract by a husband, made in contemplation of the continuance of a previous separation from his wife, or of an immediate and continued separation, where disagreements have taken place between them, to support her or to convey property to a trustee for her benefit, is valid. *Gains v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

40. *Release of dower as a consideration.*—The release by the wife of her potential right of dower is a sufficient consideration to sustain a settlement upon her by her husband even against his creditors. *Ward v. Crotty*, 4 Met. 59.

41. *Agreement to release dower.*—An agreement by a husband to transfer to his wife a note for a part of the purchase money, for her separate use, in consideration of her release of her potential right of dower in land sold by him, is binding in equity, and may be specifically enforced. *Ib.*

42. *Agreement to release dower as a consideration.*—In consideration that the wife would release her potential right of dower in a tract of land which the husband desired to sell, he agreed to transfer to her one of the notes received in payment for the land: *Held*—that the agreement was binding, and could be enforced in equity. *Ib.*

43. *Same.*—In the case *supra* the husband delivered the note to the wife indorsed, "I assign the within note to C. W. (his wife), for satisfactory consideration." No trustee was named, nor was the assignment recorded: *Held*—that as against subsequent creditors, she is entitled to the note. As against prior creditors she is entitled to the value of her potential right of dower in the land. *Ib.*

44. *Antenuptial agreements.*—Executory agreements between husband and wife, made before their marriage, and to be performed during marriage, have frequently been enforced in equity, though void at law. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 84.

45. *Conveyance from husband to wife.*—Conveyances by husband to wife, without a trustee, are valid in equity, although at law the general rule is that both executed and executory contracts between them, without the intervention of a trustee, are void. *Ib.*

46. *When contract between husband and wife enforced.*—As a general rule whenever a contract would be good at law when made by the husband with trustees for the wife, that contract will be sustained in equity when made by a husband with his wife without the intervention of trustees, if it does not affect the rights of third persons. *Ib.*

47. *The same.*—The husband induced the wife to unite with him in selling her lands and slaves, he to use the proceeds in paying his debts, but upon an express promise that he would make her full compensation out of his own property. He also executed to her at the time his promissory notes for the amount received for the property: *Held*—that the agreement was valid in a court of equity and enforcible by the wife against the husband or his estate. *Ib.*

12. *Report quashed—new appointment of viewers.*—Where the report of viewers is quashed, they can not make a second report without a new appointment. *Ib.*

13. *How route must be defined.*—The viewers of a road must fix its exact place of commencement and termination by some visible object. It is not sufficient that the beginning may be found by reversing the courses and distances run by the surveyor. A route to begin near the corner of P.'s orchard, where the turnpike makes a crook or bend, is insufficient. *Ib.*

14. *The same.*—The viewers of a road must describe the route laid out by them by metes and bounds, and courses and distances, and show the amount of land to be taken for the road. *Ib., and Craig v. North, Ib. 187.*

15. *The same.*—Such expressions as the following in defining the route of a road, to-wit: a "northwardly course," "following the general course of said creek northwardly," "crossing said creek *about* one hundred yards from C.'s line *near* the corner of N.'s land," are not sufficiently definite. *Craig v. North, 4 Met. 187.*

16. *Appeals in road cases.*—Any person has a right to resist an application for a gate across a public road, and upon doing so on the record, may prosecute an appeal from the order allowing its erection. *Bond v. Mullins, 3 Met. 282.*

17. *Order appointing viewers for gates, sufficiency of.*—The order appointing viewers on an application for the privilege of erecting gates across a road, must designate the precise places where it is proposed to erect them. "One at T. K.'s woodland pasture, and the other between said M.'s and Mrs. H.'s," is too indefinite and will render the order fatally defective. *Ib.*

268 HUSBAND AND WIFE. [TITLE

equitable, must be postponed until the legal demands of creditors were satisfied. *Ib.*

49. *What an agreement between man and wife need not specify.*—Where the wife joins with the husband in the sale of her land and slaves in order to give him the proceeds in consideration of which he gives her his promissory note for a sum of money, it is not necessary that the note should specify that it is for her separate use in order to give her an equitable claim upon his estate for the value of the property so conveyed. *Ib.*

50. *Wife's earnings.*—A husband is legally entitled to his wife's earnings, but his agreement to give them to her has been held valid in equity. *Ib.*

## VI. *Wife's equity to a settlement.*

51. *Her claim will prevail against husband's creditors.*—Where the husband's creditors seek to subject the wife's choses in action to his debt, wife may interpose, and upon showing a proper state of case, will be entitled to a settlement as against the husband and his creditors. *Tobin v. Dixon, 2 Met. 422.*

52. *As against purchaser from her, if sale void.*—A wife, in 1843, united with her husband in a deed conveying land and slaves descended to her. She received a portion of the consideration: *Held*—that as to the slaves the deed was void, a married woman having no power at that time to sell her personal property, and that in a suit by the purchaser to get possession of the property she might assert her equity to a settlement out of the slaves which the court would sustain, requiring her to refund so much of the purchase money as was received for the slaves. *Ashby v. Woolfork, 3 Met. 560.*

53. *No equity after husband reduces estate to possession.*—Where a husband reduces his wife's cash to possession his title is complete; and although he should afterward purchase property and cause it to be conveyed to a trustee for her benefit, because of his having received her money, still the property would be subject to his debts, and she could not interpose and assert a right to a settlement. *Hurdt v. Courtenay, 4 Met. 140.*

## VII. *Actions by wife against husband.*

54. *When wife may sue husband.*—The provision of the Code (section forty-nine) that where an action concerns the separate property of the wife, or where the action is between herself and her husband she may sue alone, relates merely to the form of proceeding and confers no new right of action. *Matson v. Matson, 4 Met. 262.*

55. *Effect of this provision of the Code.*—The only effect of the foregoing provision of the Code is to dispense with the necessity of a next friend in actions concerning her separate estate, or where she seeks to assert an equitable right against the husband. *Ib.*

56. *Wife can not sue, when.*—The wife can not sue the husband to recover possession of slaves devised to her as separate property free from the control of her husband, and which he refuses to deliver to her, no other ground of relief being relied upon. *Ib.*

**THE REVISED STATUTES OF OHIO.**

**THE REVISED STATUTES OF THE STATE OF OHIO**, of a General Nature, in force August, 1, 1860. Collated by Hon. J. R. Swan. With Notes of the Decisions of the Supreme Court, and References to Prior Laws, by L. J. Critchfield.

2 Vols. Royal 8vo. $12.00

This work possesses the following merits:

I. The subjects are arranged in chapters alphabetically, with a synopsis at the head of each chapter of its contents.

II. Laws and sections which have been repealed are omitted, and their repeal noted.

III. When a new section has been enacted to take the place of a repealed section, the new section has been introduced into the place of the repealed section; printed in smaller type than the rest of the text, a marginal note placed by the side of it, stating that it is an amendatory section, and giving the time when it passed, and when it took effect. Former editions of the Statutes have contained no such arrangement.

IV. The Decisions of the Courts contained in the twenty-nine volumes of the Ohio Reports have been carefully collated and introduced as notes, accompanying the text, to illustrate its construction and subject matter.

V. It also contains, the Constitution of the United States, Abstract of Naturalization Laws, Old and New Constitutions of Ohio, with copious Indexes.

No care or expense has been spared to make the work perfect and reliable in all respects.

It has now the Legislative sanction, having been approved by nearly the unanimous vote of both Houses, and was ordered to be distributed to the following State and County Officers:

Governor, Attorney General, Supreme Judges, Secretary, Comptroller, Treasurer and Auditor of State, and to the Probate Courts, Courts of Common Pleas, Superior and Police Courts, Auditor and Clerks of the various Courts in each County, to the Members of the Senate and House of Representatives of this State, and the Governors of the several States of the Union.

This book, containing, as it does, all of the Statutes now in force, and the authoritative construction of them and of the New Constitution, will be found to be especially useful in the performance of their duties to all County Officers, Justices of the Peace, Township Trustees, Clerks of Townships, and City Officers.

Inasmuch as very many changes have been made in the Statutes since the publication of the last edition, by repeal, alterations and additions, and many important decisions have been given by the Supreme Court, on controverted points, all Attorneys at Law, Bankers, Merchants, and Business Men generally, will find this an invaluable work.

1 Vol. Royal 8vo. $6 00.

The Publishers would invite the attention of the Profession to their third and carefully revised and improved edition of this excellent and highly approved work on Pleading and Practice, under the New Code of Ohio, and that recently adopted in Kansas.

The work in its former editions received the approbation of the Profession, and has occupied a high stand as a careful analysis of the principles involved in the Code, and as a basis of uniformity and regularity in the forms and practice of Pleading throughout the State. The forms here supplied will be found convenient and concise, and adequate to nearly every case likely to arise under the requirements of the Code. They have already been much used in practice, and found admirably adapted to the purposes for which they are designed, conducing especially to uniformity, brevity and perspicuity—qualities in forms and proceedings at law which can not be too highly commended.

The Publishers would particularly present this work in the improved form, to the notice of the Profession in the States of *Indiana* and *Iowa*. The close resemblance of the Codes of these States to the one of which this work is a digest and practical analysis, renders it directly applicable to their practice. It will be found admirably suited to the practitioners of those States, and well calculated to aid them in arriving at that simplicity and uniformity in the Civil Practice, so desirable in every state and court in the country

AN ACT to regulate certain corporations in Kentucky.*

To make annual reports.
SEC. I. That every corporation in this state shall report annually on the first of December to the auditor the amount of stock they are permitted to take; the amount taken; the amount of stock paid in; the amount of their liabilities; the amount of their assets; the amount of their income, and a full expose of their condition and management. This shall be done by the president and cashier, or the officers controlling and directing such corporation, by whatever name they may be designated. For failure to make such report at the time designated in this act, the president and cashier, or the officers designated in this act, shall be fined five hundred dollars each. This may be recovered in the Franklin circuit court, or any of the circuit courts of this state having jurisdiction, on motion, after ten days' notice. The commonwealth's attorney shall have one third of any fines made for prosecuting under this act when he causes to be paid the fines made into the treasury. The fines made under this act shall go to the school fund.

**SWAN'S PLEADINGS AND PRECEDENTS.**
COMMENTARIES ON PLEADINGS UNDER THE OHIO CODE, with Precedents of Petitions, Answers, Demurrers and Replies. By Hon. Joseph R. Swan.
1 Vol. 8vo. $6.00.

The author has been employed during the vacation of courts, for the past two years, in the preparation of this work. The entire two years, however, could have been profitably employed upon a work which consumed many days in determining the tenor of some of its single paragraphs. But no time or labor could result either in a complete work, or in educing, from the provisions of the Code, rules of pleading which could reconcile the various and conflicting views and opinions of the bar and bench of New York and Ohio. The author has endeavored to construe the Code in the spirit which originated its enactments; a mode heretofore not generally adopted. Instead of annotating the sections on pleading by giving the mass of decisions of the circuit judges of New York, or courts elsewhere, he has ventured to treat of the construction, language, certainty and particularity of Code Pleading, and to illustrate it by reference to the rules of pleading at common law, and pointing out the difference between those rules and the system of the Code. It also treats of the law relating to parties to action; the joinder and election of causes of action, the requisites of petitions, answers and demurrers; and contains forms of petitions, answers and demurrers.

In the *Western Law Monthly* for January, 1861, will be found a careful review of this work, from a correspondent, and remarks by the editors, to which we refer. We extract the following:

> "It should receive respectful and candid consideration on account of the author, so well known as a writer on our own laws; and it requires a thorough examination of the principles announced, to determine their true value. The book, in our opinion, is a bold one. The introduction, short as it is, announces that the purpose of the book is to enforce and illustrate the difference between the common law system of pleading and the Code system—'a difference radical and irreconcilable; frequently overlooked, sometimes perversely disregarded.' And the author, believing that the vindication of the Code from the artificial and technical rules of the common law pleading, must devolve upon the rising young men of the profession who have mastered both systems, dedicates his work 'to the young gentlemen of the bar.' * *
>
> "The spirit of these rules and of the book, seems to be opposed to all technicality in pleading; and an earnest denial of the power or propriety of any court to build up, under the Code, any system of artificial or technical rules of construction. 'The Code,' he says (page 170), 'originated in a determination to put an end to such a system; the very nature of its rules of pleading, unless perverted, will render even the courts powerless to enforce any prescribed forms, or to demand from pleaders technical language.'
>
> "We can not cite further the various things worthy of note in this volume, but we have found the book so congenial to our taste and sympathies, in regard to Code pleading, as to wish to call the attention of others to what we conceived to be the key-note of this book. We are happy to know that the present judges of the Supreme Court are said to have very much the same opinions in regard to the Code; and under their administration in the highest court, and a cordial co-operation of the other courts and the bar, we do not see why we may not have the true spirit of Code pleading and practice, as we claim this book shows it forth, everywhere adopted."

[The foregoing review of the recent treatise of Judge SWAN, upon the subject of Pleading, under the Code of Ohio, is from the pen of a correspondent who has thoroughly examined the work, and is well qualified to speak of its merits and character; and to present, as he has faithfully done, some of the views of its author; and the principles adopted by him as applied to that statute. * * The work is certainly worthy of the careful reading of every practitioner in our country. The learned author's presentation of the principles of the Code, will be found highly instructive; particularly to that class of the profession to whom the book is dedicated.]—*Eds. Western Law Monthly.*

*From Hon. James L. Bates.*

I have examined "SWAN'S Pleading and Precedents, under the Ohio Code," with some care. It will be a valuable aid to every attorney. In the course of the work, the distinction between common law pleading and pleading under the Code, is clearly pointed out, and the peculiarities of the latter are carefully stated.

The Precedents furnish good specimens of pleading in each class of cases. After a careful examination of the two parts, an attorney will have little difficulty in preparing his pleadings in any given case. The general introduction of the work will tend to produce uniformity in pleading, and essentially aid the perfecting our new system.

-For an act requiring tenants in common and coparceners to contribute ratably to the expense of litigation for their joint benefit, see CONTRIBUTION.

1. *Informer not liable for cost.*—In a proceeding to strike the name of an attorney from the roll of attorneys, the person who prefers the charges, is not a necessary or proper party, and no judgment can be given against him for the costs. *Turner v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 619.
2. *Cost on appeals.*—On the trial of an appeal from the judgment of a justice of the peace, the appellant is entitled to a judgment for his cost, if the judgment on the appeal is more favorable to him than the one appealed from. *Keller v. Bate*, 3 Met. 130.
3. *Contract of indemnity—what cost may be recovered on.*—In an action on a contract of indemnity the obligee may recover such legal costs as have been recovered against him, but not his attorney's fee incurred in defending the first action, unless he made the defense at the request of the obligor. *Gains v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

1. *Deduction from judge's salary—neglect of duty.*—The thirteenth section of article eighth, of the constitution, authorizes the legislature to make deductions from a judge's salary for neglect of official duty only, and the legislature has no power to authorize deductions on any other ground. *Gar-rard v. Nutall, 2 Met. 166.*
2. *Treasurer can not determine when judge has neglected duty.*—The treasurer has no power to determine when a circuit judge has been guilty of such neglect of official duty as to authorize a deduction from his salary. *Ib.*
3. *Treasurer no right to refuse payment.*—The treasurer has no right to refuse payment of the auditor's warrant in favor of a circuit judge for a portion of his salary, although the same amount has been paid a judge *pro tem.* for holding his court during the period for which the warrant is drawn. *Ib.*

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend section eight hundred and thirty-two, Civil Code of Practice.

**SEC. I.** That an act, entitled "an act to amend section eight hundred and thirty-two, of chapter three, and article one, Code of Practice," approved February 16, 1858, be amended as follows, to-wit: Any constable who shall hereafter return any summons or other process returnable before a justice of the peace, in any other district than that in which the defendant resides at the date of such return, shall be fined for each offense the sum of twenty dollars, to be recovered by indictment in the city court of Louisville. All judgments which may be hereafter rendered by a justice of the peace against defendants who do not reside in the district of such justice of the peace when judgment may be rendered, or did not reside in such district when process was served, shall be null and void. If, however, the justices in the district of the defendant's residence are both interested in the subject of the suit, or refuse to act, it shall be the duty of the constable to return the summons in an adjoining district, with such facts indorsed thereon; and thereupon, and not otherwise, judgment may be rendered as if such defendant resided in said district: *Provided, however*, That this act shall only apply to the city of Louisville.

**SEC. II.** This act shall take effect from its passage.

**SENEY'S OHIO CODE.**

**THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, AND THE CODE OF PROCEDURE BEFORE JUSTICES OF THE PEACE, for the State of Ohio:** With Notes of the Decisions of the Courts of Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, and New York. By Hon. Geo. E. Seney, Judge of the Tenth Judicial District.

1 Vol. 8vo. $6.00.

This work contains the Code of Civil Procedure, and the Code of Procedure before Justices of the Peace, with all subsequent Amendments and Supplementary Acts, in force April, 1860; also, several Statutes of general application under the present system of Pleading and Practice. Brief notes of the published decisions of the courts of Ohio are appended to those sections of either Code, and to the Statutes referred to, to which a judicial construction has been given. Also, notes of the decisions of all the courts of Kentucky, Indiana and New York, upon all provisions of the Codes of either of those States, which are identical, or nearly so, with those of the Ohio Code.

These notes are compiled from the Ohio State Reports, Ohio Reports, Handy's Cincinnati Superior Court Reports, Cin. Law Gazette, Cleveland Law Monthly, Western Law Journal, Reports of the Court of Appeals of Kentucky, Supreme Court of Indiana, and the numerous Reports in New York.

In the arrangement of the work, where a section of either Code has been repealed and an amended section enacted to supply its place, the amended section is substituted for the repealed section, with a note stating the time when it was passed, and the volume of Laws in which it was published; also, giving the section repealed.

As no edition of the Code has been published since 1853, this volume, containing all the subsequent amendments and acts supplementary to the Code, with nearly three thousand notes of judicial decisions, construing its provisions, and determining many interesting and important questions of practice, will be found useful and convenient not only to the Bench and the Bar, but to Justices of the Peace.

*From Western Law Monthly.*

Whatever contributes to the dissemination of a correct knowledge of the Code of Civil Procedure of this State, and of the practice under it, is of great value to the whole people. We therefore hail with sincere satisfaction the work of Judge SENey. It bears the impress of the mature and cultivated legal mind and sound judgment characteristic of the author. The plan of the work does not admit of any discussion on his part. The object of the author has been to furnish a perfect copy of the present Code, with all the light that judicial decisions have thrown upon it or the practice under it; entirely divested of all speculations or opinions of his own. In this, he seems to have accomplished all that the present state of authorities admits; giving, at one view, all that is authoritative upon the subject, and saving to the practitioner and the court a vast amount of labor in research, that might in the end have proved less efficacious. The book should be deemed—as practically it is—indispensable to every lawyer, and every student of the Code. And it ought to be in the hands of every Justice of the Peace. It is supplied with a full table of contents, and a copious and minute index. It is printed and bound in the very best style of workmanship and material.

*From Hon. R. P. Ranney.*

I have carefully examined the work of Judge SENey upon the Ohio Code, and am much pleased with it. It is good for all it professes, and reflects credit upon both the author and publishers. The chief value of such a work must consist in the facility with which the practitioner can, in the hurry of business, refer to any disputed or doubtful question, and feel sure that he has seen all that can influence its decision. This is accomplished in this work by a very careful and judicious collection and arrangement of the various statutory provisions, and a reference to all the judicial decisions of several States in which the construction of similar statutes has been involved. The work is every thing that could be desired in this respect, and I think will be found a valuable aid to the bar of the State.

*From Hon. Milton Sutliff.*

From the cursory perusal of SENey's Ohio Code, which I have been able to give, I regard it as being all that its title page professes. It seems to be a work characterized by good judgment in its design, and much industry and care in its execution. The aid and facility thereby afforded for a correct understanding of the practice under the Code, I think, will insure the work a favorable reception by the legal profession.

*From Hon. James Murray, Attorney General of Ohio.*

After a very careful examination of the "Code of Civil Procedure of the State of Ohio, with Notes, etc., by GEORGE E. SENey," I have no hesitation in saying that I regard it as a most valuable aid to the legal profession. It is a work without which no lawyer's library is complete, and which should be in the hands not only of every lawyer in active practice, but also of every student of the law, as well as every Justice of the Peace in the State.

1 Vol. 8vo. $2.00.

The design of this work is to furnish Executors and Administrators with a plain, familiar and accessible guide in the discharge of their duties. It is the result of the observation and experience of the author in an official position which gave him ample opportunity to learn the real wants of those for whose benefit it has been prepared. It makes no pretensions to the title of a LAWYER'S BOOK, nor to great learning and research. What is claimed for it is, that it is more practical in its scope, enters more minutely into the details of the ordinary duties of Executors and Administrators, and is more explicit in its instructions with regard to the manner in which those duties should be performed, than any other work on the same subject published in this State. It does not propose to enable those who consult its pages to dispense with the advice of efficient attorneys; but, on the contrary, invariably insists that counsel should be taken upon all the more difficult questions.

In the citation of authorities, the author has confined himself exclusively to the decisions of our own courts, and has preferred, in most instances, to give the law as it has been expounded by the courts, in the form of foot-notes, rather than to embody it in the text.

The Forms for the use of Probate Judges, Executors, Administrators, Widows, Heirs, Legatees, Creditors, etc., are believed to be more numerous, and adapted to more purposes, than in any similar work ever published.

*From the Western Law Monthly.*

This is a book that has long been needed by persons having the estates of deceased persons to settle. It is the result of several years experience of the learned author as a Probate Judge, which experience has enabled him to give, in the plainest and most familiar style, all necessary instructions as to the manner in which the duties of Executors, Administrators, and Probate Judges, should be performed. We are shown in other works what needs to be done in the settlement of an estate. Mr. RAFF not only gives us this, but also gives full directions how each step should be taken in the settlement of the property of a deceased person. It also contains nearly one hundred and fifty different Forms for the use of the above named officers.

The opening chapter, relative to Wills, is a well written and correct digest of the law upon that subject; it contains also some wholesome advice to the drawers of this instrument. Although the author disclaims having written the work for lawyers, it should be in the hands of every attorney in the State. The author is a good lawyer, and the work will relieve the attorney from much labor. It must meet with ready sale.

*From Hon. Wm. H. Burke, Probate Judge of Stark Co., O.*

I have thoroughly examined "RAFF's Guide," and am constantly in the habit of appealing to it in the discharge of the duties pertaining to this office. I have also at all times recommended it to Executors and Administrators, and it should be in the hands of every man discharging those trusts. The book gives evidence that the author has had that practical experience so requisite in framing a work of this kind, and wanting which, authors are apt to mystify, rather than assist, those who appeal to it for aid.

*From Hon. G. W. Belden.*

I have examined the second edition of your book entitled "A Guide to Executors and Administrators." I think it a work of great usefulness and importance to the public generally. To those engaged in the discharge of the duties of Executors and Administrators, it can not fail to be a practical and able guide. The Forms alone are very important, and are worth more than the price of the book.

*From Hon. Calvary Morris.*

I have thoroughly examined "Raff's Guide," and for two years have adopted it as my "Guide" in regard to the duties pertaining to this office, and have already ordered from you some twenty-four copies, and disposed of them to executors and administrators, and to lawyers and justices of the peace, all except three or four copies; and by their circulation I already find great relief from very many vexing applications for counsel and direction. To executors and administrators the work is almost indispensable.

I shall make every effort to give it a wide circulation in our county.

**SWAN'S NEW TREATISE FOR JUSTICES IN OHIO.**

**A TREATISE ON THE LAW RELATING TO THE POWERS AND DUTIES OF JUSTICES OF THE PEACE AND CONSTABLES**, in the State of Ohio (and in Kansas), with Practical Forms, etc. By Hon. Joseph R. Swan. Eighth edition, corrected and enlarged. Revised to conform to the Statutes, etc., in force May, 1862.

**1 Vol. Royal 8vo. $6.00.**

The object of this treatise is to enable the Magistrate and the Constable, in the exercise of their respective offices, to understand and apply the common practical rules of Law, as known and recognized in the higher courts of justice. It is not to be regarded as embracing all possible cases that may occur in the ever-changing affairs of human life, but only as a compilation of general rules, accompanied with practical forms, such as may be useful to the Magistrate in the performance of his duties, and to the Constable in executing the ordinary process of the law.

The first edition was published in 1836. In 1855 the changes in the law were so radical that it became necessary to re-write most of the work. The sixth edition was then issued, embracing the law and forms of the lately-passed Justices' Act, together with the provisions of the Code, so far as they applied to proceedings before justices of the peace. The Law relating to Agents, Mechanics' and Furnishers' Lien, Limited Partnerships, Bills of Lading, Mortgage of Goods, and actions against Watercraft, etc., with the necessary forms were then added, and other titles enlarged; especially those which relate to Evidence and Mercantile Law.

The numerous changes in the law since that time, have rendered another careful revision of the work absolutely necessary. In this, the eighth edition, the alterations will be found both numerous and important; being revised so as to conform to the statutes passed prior to the adjournment of General Assembly, May, 1862. The references to the statutes are, to the recent edition, compiled by Swan & Critchfield. Such of the reported decisions of the Supreme Court, contained in the twenty volumes of the Ohio Reports, and in the eleven volumes of the Ohio State Reports, as modify the text of the late edition, are also noted, and the text revised.

Though intended specially for the guidance of Magistrates and Constables, in the discharge of their various duties, yet, when occasion requires it, the members of the Bar will find a consultation of its pages not altogether unuseful; and the jurisdiction of the Magistrate is so extensive, and embraces so many transactions of life, that business men will find a frequent reference to it much for their interest, as well as convenience in the conduct of their affairs.

**STANTON'S TREATISE FOR JUSTICES IN KENTUCKY.**

**A TREATISE ON THE LAW RELATIVE TO THE POWERS AND DUTIES OF JUSTICES OF THE PEACE AND CONSTABLES, in the State of Kentucky, with Forms, etc.**
Second edition, thoroughly revised, to conform to the Statutes now in force.
By Hon. Richard H. Stanton.

**1 Vol. 8vo. $4 00**

This new work, which has been prepared with unusual diligence and care by the author, is intended to be a complete and perfect guide to Justices of the Peace, and the other officers named above, in the discharge of their respective duties under the laws of the State. Its arrangement is such that, while it presents to the officer a full view of the law which he may be called upon to enforce, whether civil or criminal, it affords him such complete and accurate instructions as to the practical manner of discharging his duties in each particular case, that it will be almost impossible for the least informed among them to go astray.

Every necessary form which a Justice of the Peace, or other officer may require in the course of his official duties, will be found in its appropriate place in the work; and the instructions are so full, perspicuous and clear, that it will be found an invaluable official companion to those for whom the treatise is intended, and a highly useful work to the legal profession, and all others having business with those officers. The author has spared no labor to make it just such a work as is now most needed by the class of officers whose duties are so fully and clearly explained, and his well known accuracy and success will commend it to the hearty patronage of all persons in Kentucky concerned in the administration of the law.

SEC. I. That if the county court shall willfully or negligently fail to appoint a commissioner, as required by section six, article three, chapter twenty-six, Revised Statutes, title "County Levy," the persons holding said court shall be subject to a fine of not less than two hundred dollars, nor more than five hundred dollars, to be recovered by indictment of a grand jury; and any commissioner appointed under said section, who shall accept said appointment, and who shall willfully or negligently fail to perform his duty, shall be fined not less than two hundred dollars, nor more than five hundred dollars, to be recovered by indictment of a grand jury.

SEC. II. It shall be the duty of said commissioner to notify the sheriff or collector of the county levy to appear before him, and settle his accounts concerning the county levy.

SEC. III. If said sheriff or collector shall willfully or negligently fail, for ten days, after being notified as aforesaid, to settle said accounts, he shall be fined twenty per cent. on the amount of the county levies for that year, with interest from the first day of October of the year he should have settled, to be recovered by motion, after ten days' notice, in the county court; said motion shall be made by the county attorney, in the name of and for the use of the county.

SEC. IV. The commissioner shall file a report of his settlement with the county judge, at least five days before the court of claims; and said judge shall present said report to said court of claims for examination and approval.

SEC. V. In all cases, in which the settlements have not heretofore been made with the sheriff or county collector of the county, as required by law, the court shall, in the year 1864, appoint a commissioner to make said settlements, as provided in this act; and any sheriff, collector, or commissioner, willfully or negligently failing to perform their duty under this section, shall

**BICKNELL'S INDIANA CIVIL PRACTICE.**

**THE PRACTICE OF THE SUPREME AND CIRCUIT COURTS OF THE STATE OF INDIANA**
**IN CIVIL CASES.** By George A. Bicknell, L.L. D., Judge of the Second
Judicial Circuit and Professor of Law in the State University.

**1 Vol., 8vo. $6.00.**

After ten years' use, aided by rules of court, by the discussions of counsel, by the learning of the
Supreme Court, and by legislative emendation, the Code of Practice introduced by the Revised Stat-
utes of 1852, has attained sufficient certainty and consistency to be a fit subject for systematic con-
sideration.

In this Book of Practice, the author takes a view of this Jurisdiction of the courts in civil causes,
and of the Judges, Clerks, Sheriffs and Attorneys, so far as their duties in civil causes are concerned;
he afterward states the proceedings in the management of civil causes in general from beginning to
end, including the practice in causes appealed to the Supreme Court, and in causes appealed to the
Circuit Court, from all inferior tribunals; and finally, the practice in particular cases, and in special
and summary proceedings, is stated; and it has been the design of the author throughout to show,
not only what the law is and what ought to be done, but also how to do it and when to do it.

**THE PRACTICE OF THE SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE OF INDIANA, IN CRIM-**
**INAL CASES.** By George A. Bicknell, L.L. D. *In press.*

**1 Vol., 8vo.**

The Practice of the Superior Courts of Indiana in Criminal Cases has never been thoroughly ex-
hibited. There is no book exclusively devoted to it.

The author has been urged repeatedly, since the publication of his "Civil Practice," to set forth
the Practice in Criminal Cases, and in this book he has undertaken to do it.

The nature of crime and punishment and criminal responsibility, the extent and essentials of
criminal jurisdiction, and the manner in which prosecutions are commenced, by indictment and by
information, are particularly set forth; all the proceedings, general and special, in Criminal Cases,
are fully stated, including the practice in appeals to the Supreme Court, and the principal felonies
known to the law of Indiana, are separately considered, and forms of indictment are given for each,
and other necessary forms are given in the course of the work.

128 COUNTER-CLAIM AND SETOFF. [TITLE

How plaintiff may present his cause.
New parties
by amended petition or in his reply to such offset or counter-claim, set forth and present said cause of action, as in a cross petition, and introduce any new parties that may be necessary to an adjudication of the same; and the same shall be prosecuted in such manner as is provided by the act to which this is an amendment, in relation to cross petitions by a defendant growing out of matters set up in the petition of a plaintiff.

SEC. II. This act shall apply to proceedings, in conformity therewith, which have been instituted in actions already pending, and it shall take effect from and after its passage.

---

I. *What demands may be used as a setoff.*

1. *Unliquidated damages.*—A demand for unliquidated damages, though it arises upon a contract, is not the proper subject of a setoff under the Code. *Shropshire v. Conrad*, 2 Met. 143; *Taylor v. Stowell*, 4 Met. 175.

2. *Same in cases of insolvency and non-residence.*—But the existence of any state of fact calculated to defeat or impair the defendant's remedy, as the insolvency or non-residence of the plaintiff or his assignor, is ground for a court of equity to interpose and enforce the setoff even in cases of unliquidated demands. *Taylor v. Stowell*, 4 Met. 175; *Geoghegan v. Ditto*, 2 Met. 433.

3. *Unliquidated demand on insolvent or non-resident plaintiff.*—A demand for unliquidated damages for the breach of a warranty of the quality of a commodity for which the note sued on was given, may be relied on as an equitable setoff against the note when the vendor is insolvent or non-resident, even where the note has passed to the hands of an assignee. *Ib.*

4. *Against assignee; notice of assignment.*—The principle is well settled that a setoff, to be available against an assignee, either at law or in equity, must have existed before notice of the assignment. *Walker v. McKay*, 2 Met. 294.

5. *Demand not due; no setoff.*—A debt or demand can not be used as a setoff until it becomes due, and unless it be due before notice of the assignment, it is not available against the assignee. *Ib.*

6. *Surety; insolvency of principal.*—The insolvency of the principal at the time he assigns a note held by him upon the surety, does not entitle the latter to an equity against the note in the hands of the assignee, he not having paid the debt for which he was surety until after he had notice of the assignment. *Ib.*

7. *Sheriff's sale void; trespass; purchase-money.*—Where the purchaser of land at a void sheriff's sale, is sued for trespass by the defendant in the execution who is insolvent, he may set up the money paid by him in satisfaction of the execution as a setoff against any judgment to be recovered against him for the trespass. *Geoghegan v. Ditto*, 2 Met. 433.

**A SUMMARY OF THE LAW OF PARTNERSHIP.** For the use of Business Men.
By Stanley Matthews, one of the Judges of the Superior Court of Cincinnati.

This outline of the Law of Partnership has been sketched for the use of the business community. It aims to give nothing more than a general view of the principles on which it is based, and the rules for their application. It is not designed to supersede the necessity of professional advice. On the contrary, its chief value, it is thought, will consist in pointing out the occasions when such assistance is needed, and in enhancing the appreciation of it, when rendered.

"We have examined Judge Matthews' 'Summary of the Law of Partnership,' and are satisfied it is a carefully compiled and thorough treatise on the subject. The principles are clearly, yet concisely stated, in language almost faultless, and the whole spirit of the work assures us its author has conscientiously discharged his duty. It is an unpretending volume in size. but it is no less valuable to business men, as well as the practicing lawyer.

"B. STORER,
