**CURWEN ON ABSTRACTS OF TITLE.**

**A MANUAL OF ABSTRACTS OF TITLE TO REAL PROPERTY;** containing Practical Directions for the Examination of Titles to Real Property; Searching Records of Deeds, Wills, Judgments, Decrees, Mortgages, Liens; and the Doctrine of Equity with respect to Vendors' Liens, Separate Estates of Married Women, Notice of Trusts, etc. Illustrated by references to the Statutes of Pennsylvania, Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa and Kansas. By M. E. Curwen, of the Cincinnati Bar.

**1 Vol., 12mo. Cloth, $1.50. Law Sheep, $1.75.**

This work, believed to be the only one in the United States upon the subject, is designed to give plain, practical directions for the examination of records relating to the title to lands in the States of Pennsylvania, Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa and Kansas. The statutory requirements of these States in reference to contracts concerning lands, deeds, wills, leases, mortgages, liens, judicial sales, and the like, are fully stated, and the authorities referred to. The subject of oil lands and leases is considered, and the usual form of oil leases given.

Without going into the abstruse learning on the law of real property, the work states, sharply and definitely, the points of law to which the attention of men of property, real estate dealers, auctioneers and searchers of titles should be directed; the requisites of a binding contract of sale of land; the rules for computing the area of land; the requisites of deeds, leases, wills, and judicial titles; the liens which attach to lands; the provisions of the stamp act, and the usual forms of deeds and mortgages.

The officer conveying the prisoners to the jail designated, and such guards as the judge or court may direct him to take, not exceeding the number of guards allowed in taking convicts to the penitentiary, shall receive the same compensation and mileage that they are now allowed by law for taking convicts to the penitentiary, to be allowed by the circuit court directing the transfer, and paid out of the treasury of the state, unless there was no jail in the county, or it was rendered insecure by reason of the failure of the county court to keep it in the requisite condition, then it shall be paid out of the county levy of the county; and the court in making the allowance, shall state in the order out of which fund it shall be paid. The order of court or judge directing the transfer shall be conclusive evidence that the transfer was proper, and to the right jail, and shall be a justification to the jailer for holding any such prisoner, in any action against him for false imprisonment. The officers of the county from which the removal was had, or a circuit judge, shall have jurisdiction to hear applications for bail, and to grant the same, if the case is bailable, in the manner now prescribed by law. If there is no circuit judge in the county when the order of transfer is made as directed in the first section of this act, the county judge shall make the same. He shall deliver his order to the clerk of the circuit court, which shall be presented to the circuit court at its next term, subject to its supervising control.

**CARUTHERS' HISTORY OF A LAW SUIT.**

**HISTORY OF A LAW SUIT**; or, A Treatise on the Practice in Suits and Proceedings of every description, from the beginning to the end in Courts of Law, by Abraham Caruthers, late Circuit Judge of Tennessee, and Law Professor in Cumberland University. Second Edition.

**1 Vol. 8vo.**

Hon. R. J. Meigs, in 1860, soon after the publication of the first edition of the "History of a Lawsuit," wrote the following:

> "I have examined the new law book by the Hon. Abe Caruthers, one of the Professors of Law in Cumberland University, pretty thoroughly, and formed a very favorable opinion of its value, especially to the practitioner. The author's intimate and accurate knowledge of the principles of practice. would be sufficient to assure me of the importance of the book to the Bar of the State, and I am sure no lawyer can read a page of it without wishing to possess a copy as a circuit companion, always ready to remove every doubt that may occur in circumstances often where reflection is impossible."

NASHVILLE, *February 8, 1866*.

> "The undersigned Judges of the Supreme Court of Tennessee, take pleasure in stating that they have examined and frequently referred to and used the new work entitled "The History of a Lawsuit." by the Hon. Abe Caruthers, late professor in the Law School of Cumberland University, at Lebanon, Tennessee, and for many years one of the Circuit Judges of the State, and have no hesitation in pronouncing it a most valuable accession to the works on practice. We consider it almost invaluable to the Bench and Bar, as well as to Clerks, Sheriffs and Justices of the Peace. The author has been most happy in presenting lucidly and concisely, both the written and unwritten law on points of practice in the various suits and proceedings in courts of law, as well as forms required by Attorneys, Clerks, Sheriffs and Magistrates.
>
> The criminal practice, with forms, together with the law in relation to the execution, probate and contest of wills, the laying off of dower, partition of land, etc., are also embraced in this work.
>
> The high character of the author for accuracy and profundity of legal learning, perspicuity of style, and clearness of conception is fully sustained in this work. We recommend it to the favorable consideration of the Legal Profession, and the public generally."

J. O. SHACKELFORD.
ALVIN HAWKINS.
SAM. MILLIGAN.

It will be the aim of this work to meet a want urgently felt both by the Legal and Medical Profession. Trials for Surgical Malpractice are quite frequent throughout the country, and both Counsel and Surgeons are often put to great trouble in their research after cases and reports of trials which bear upon the question at issue. Prof. Blackman will endeavor to present the Surgical branch of the subject, in such a manner, that the members of the Bar may be able, with less trouble than heretofore experienced, to become acquainted with the modern surgical teachings of our highest authorities which have reference to these trials. To render still further aid, numerous illustrative engravings will be given with the text, which will be of great value in the study of fractures and injuries in the vicinity of the joints, the results of which have given rise to so many trials. Other anatomical and surgical illustrations will be given so as to make the work far more complete and valuable than any yet published.

The discussion of the limits of legal rights and responsibilities growing out of such cases, in the light of judicial decisions, will be furnished by Judge Matthews.

CXI.] SALES OF THE ESTATE OF INFANTS, ETC. 431

3. *What interests may be sold.*—Under the statute *supra* the court may order the sale of any interest of an infant in real estate, whatever its character and whether derived by descent, devise or contract. A contingent remainder or an executory devise may be sold under the statute. *Ib.*

*The same.*—A will declared that in case of the death without issue of any one of a class to whom land was devised, his portion should revert to the remainder of the class and their heirs: *Held*—that the contingent interest of infant devisees in the land may be sold under the statute *supra*. *Ib.*

## II. Preliminary report. Appointment and oath of commissioners.

4. *When commissioners to be appointed.*—The court has no power to appoint commissioners to value the real estate of infants until the petition for its sale has been filed by the statutory guardian. *Mattingly v. Reed, 3 Met. 524.*

5. *Commissioners must be sworn.*—The record must show that the commissioners appointed to report the nett value of infant's estate, etc., were sworn; otherwise the sale will be void. *Watts v. Pond, 4 Met. 61.*

6. *What the order of appointment must show.*—The order appointing commissioners authorized them simply "to value the infant's real estate." It is doubted whether a report made in conformity to the law under such an order and confirmed by the court, would cure the defect in the order. *Woodcock v. Bowman, 4 Met. 40.*

## III. Preliminary report—sufficiency of.

7. *Must state that all the infant's property is enumerated.*—Where, in a proceeding to sell the real estate of an infant, the report of the commissioners fails to state that all the estate owned by the infant is enumerated in it, it is not sufficient to authorize the sale, and the sale will be void. *Wells v. Cowherd, 2 Met. 514; Mattingly v. Reed, 3 Met. 524.*

8. *Must state that the interest of the infant requires the sale.*—A report "that if the property above mentioned was sold and proceeds properly invested it would redound to the interest of said heirs," gives the court no jurisdiction to adjudge a sale. *Wells v. Cowherd, 2 Met. 514.*

9. *The same.*—A report that a sale would "redound" to the interest of the infant confers no jurisdiction to order a sale. The guardian must allege his belief that a sale would redound to the benefit of the infant, but the report must show that his interest requires the sale before the court has jurisdiction. *Mattingly v. Reed, 3 Met. 524; Watts v. Pond, 4 Met. 61.*

10. *Must show nett value of estate.*—The report of the commissioners must also show the nett value of the infant's real and personal estate and the annual profit thereof. *Woodcock v. Bowman, 4 Met. 40.*

11. *A report held insufficient.*—The report, after stating the value of the land proposed to be sold, makes this statement: "That there is no other estate in this country belonging to said heirs known to them:" *Held*—that the report was insufficient and conferred no jurisdiction to adjudge a sale. *Bell v. Clark, 2 Met. 573.*

SEC. I. *Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky*: That the quartermaster-general be, and he is hereby, required to audit all claims against the state arising out of the military service of state troops for forage or commissary stores furnished them by any citizens thereof. # 1861.
*May 24.*

AN ACT to suspend the circuit and other courts in this commonwealth, and for other purposes.*

**SEC. I.** That all laws requiring circuit courts, equity and criminal courts, quarterly courts, justices' courts, and all police, town, and city courts, except for the trial of criminal and penal causes in this commonwealth, be, and they are hereby, repealed until the first day of January, 1862: *Provided*, That after the expiration of said time, said courts shall be held and governed by all the laws now in force: *And provided further*, That all civil process of every kind, returnable to said terms, shall be continued until the next regular terms of said courts after the first day of January, 1862; and nothing herein contained shall be construed so as to interfere in any manner whatever with the trial of criminal and penal causes in said courts at their regular terms, as though this act had not taken effect: *And provided further*, That the circuit court of Boyle county be held at its regular August term for the trial of all contested cases remaining on the docket of said court at the February term, 1861. That the several judges of the equity and circuit courts shall hold the terms of their several courts at the times now fixed by law for the trial of criminal and penal prosecutions, and for the purpose of the assignment of dower, for the trial of cases of divorce, for the probate of wills, for making the partition of land, ordering the distribution of estates ratably among the creditors, and for the trial of actions of tort, actions of forcible entry and detainer, and forcible detainer, and cases where the title to land or other property is in dispute, or where the boundary of land is involved, or a party is seeking to establish or complete, by judgment or order of court, title to lands, and all causes between principal and agent, between trustees and *cestui que trust*, and for all other cases of law or equity where a decree or judgment for money is not to be rendered, and for the taking of all proper steps for the preparation of cases in said courts.

Laws requiring circuit courts, eq. and crim. courts, quarterly courts, justices' courts, police, town and city courts, repealed until 1st January, 1862.

SEC. II. He may exchange with any state in this Union copies of the school reports and documents of this state, for copies of the school reports and documents of such other state.

SEC. III. Copies of records and papers in his office, certified by him, shall, in all cases, be evidence equally with the originals.

SEC. IV. He shall keep his office in the city of Frankfort, or in some city or town within a day's travel therefrom.

SEC. V. The school commissioners of the several counties of this commonwealth shall, so soon as the school fund coming to their respective counties shall have been received by them, and the proper demand made, pay out and disburse the same to the persons entitled thereto, and within ninety days settle their accounts with the county court, and forward a copy of such settlement, certified by the clerk of said court, to the superintendent of public instruction, and said copies of settlement with the county court shall embrace all sums received since the date of their last settlement, by said commissioners for the benefit of common schools, and also contain a full statement of all sums paid out by them, for what, to whom, and when paid; and should any commissioner fail to pay out and disburse the common school moneys received by him, and to make settlement and report the same within ninety days, as above directed, shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, on indictment in the circuit court, be fined in a sum not less than one hundred, nor more than five hundred dollars, as well as remain liable on his official bond.

SEC. VI. Any school commissioner who shall knowingly and willfully report to the superintendent of public instruction a number of common schools as having been taught in his county greater than the number of such schools actually taught therein according to law, or a number of children entitled to tuition in his county greater than the actual number of such children therein, or otherwise knowingly and willfully misstate any fact or facts which he now is, or may hereafter be required by law to report to said superintendent, shall be deemed guilty of a high misdemeanor, and shall, on conviction thereof, on indictment in the circuit court, be fined in a sum not less than two hundred, nor more than five hundred dollars, or imprisoned in the county jail not longer than six months, or both so fined and imprisoned, in the discretion of the jury.

and all executions hereafter issued in this commonwealth by the officers authorized to issue the same, shall be made returnable on the first day of March, 1862; and no officer, by authority of any execution issued under this act, shall sell any personal property of any kind until he has caused said property to be appraised, as land is now required to be appraised, unless such property shall at such sale bring at least two thirds of its appraised value; and all sales of such property, which shall be made under any such executions for less than two thirds of its appraised value, shall be null and void, and invest the purchaser with no title to the same: but said execution shall still hold and retain a lien on said property: *Provided*, That any lien which may have been acquired or created by the issuing of an execution, shall not be released or impaired by the return of said execution, but shall continue in full force on any subsequent execution that may be issued; and after the said first of March, 1862, executions shall be issued and regulated according to the laws now in force, and sales of property after that time shall also be regulated by the laws in force previous to the passage of this act: *And provided further*, That this act shall not release the liability or responsibility of any assignor or assignors to his or their assignee or assignees on any assignment or transfer on any note, bond, or contract, or any surety on any note or contract on which suit has not been instituted, or on which judgment has been rendered: *Provided*, This act shall not be so construed as to prohibit any person whatever from bringing suits in any of the courts aforesaid, and obtaining any provisional remedy under existing laws, nor shall the law, as it now stands, authorizing the trial of attachments and other provisional remedies, be in any wise obstructed by the provisions of this act; and judges and justices of the peace of said courts shall have power, under existing laws, to make any necessary order for the sale or preservation of attached property, or to enter any order authorized by the present laws for the distribution of any money whatever among the parties entitled to receive the same, in any case pending in any of said courts.

1. *Grand jurors only to be summoned by sheriff or coroner.*—The sheriff, or where there is no sheriff, the coroner, only is entitled to summons bystanders to sit upon a grand jury. If bystanders be summoned by another person, though appointed by the court, an indictment found by such jury may be quashed on motion. *Commonwealth v. Graddy, 4 Met. 223.*

2. *The same.*—The provision of the Criminal Code authorizing the court to designate some other person to summons jurors, relates only to petit juries. *Ib.*

3. *Who may summons when selected by commissioners.*—Where jury commissioners have selected grand jurors as directed by the Revised Statutes, the court incline to the opinion that the summoning them by a person other than the sheriff or coroner, would not be a "substantial error." *Ib.*

# TITLE LXXXI.
## LANDLORD AND TENANT.

1. *Extent of landlord's lien.*—If the landlord (prior to the act of February, 1858) claim the lien conferred by the fourteenth section of article two, Title Landlord and Tenant, he can only obtain it by having a distress warrant issued and levied on the property. Otherwise he will be entitled only to the provision made for him by the twentieth section, by which the officer levying an execution or attachment on the tenant's property is only required to pay the landlord out of the proceeds thereof, such rent as is due and in arrear, not exceeding one year's rent. *Williams v. Wood*, 2 Met. 41.

2. *Same under act of February 16, 1858.*—By the act of February 16, 1858, an exclusive lien is given to the landlord on all the personal property of the tenant found on the leased premises after possession taken by the tenant, and this lien exists and may be enforced by the landlord without suing out a warrant to enforce it. *Ib.*

3. *How distress warrant discharged.*—The law prescribes but one mode in which the levy of a distress warrant may be discharged, and that is by executing the bond prescribed by section seven hundred and twenty-one of the Civil Code. *Grubbs v. McCoy*, 2 Met. 486.

4. *Invalid bond.*—Tenant whose property had been levied under a distress warrant, executed a bond to the effect that if the property should be adjudged subject to the distress warrant, he would pay to the plaintiff the value thereof, and ten per cent. interest, and thereupon the property was returned to the tenant: **Held**—That the bond was unauthorized and could not be enforced by motion: that it did not discharge the levy, and that the officer might peaceably retake possession of the property and sell it, although he had returned the warrant. *Ib.*

5. *Tenant executing bond, admits tenancy.*—If the defendant in a distress warrant executes the bond authorized by section seven hundred and twenty-one, Civil Code, he thereby admits that he is either tenant, assignee, or sub-tenant, and on trial of the motion for judgment on the bond is estopped to deny that character, or to assert that he was but a surety of the lessee, or that the lease was procured by fraud or mistake. *Pegard v. Kellar*, 4 Met. 260.

6. *Relief in equity.*—Whether in the case *supra* the defendant might not be relieved in equity, is not decided. *Ib.*

7. *Attachment for rent must conform to the Code.*—The provisions of chapter fifty-six, article two, section six, Revised Statutes, providing that upon the return into the circuit court of an attachment issued by a justice

# PREFACE.

THIS volume embraces the GENERAL LAWS OF KENTUCKY, enacted by its Legislature since the publication of MR. STANTON'S edition of the REVISED STATUTES, in 1859. The propriety of bringing those laws together in one volume, under appropriate titles, will doubtless be admitted by all who are conversant with the subject. Whether this attempt to perform that task has been attended with a reasonable degree of success must be determined by those for whose use the volume is intended.

I have aimed at publishing in the volume all laws of a *general character* and of a *permanent nature*. In theory, it is easy to define the boundary between general, and local or private laws; and between laws of a permanent, and those of a temporary nature; but in applying the theory to the legislation of the last six years, it has often been difficult to decide whether a law was of a sufficiently general nature, or of a sufficiently permanent character, to justify its publication. Whenever there has been any great doubt on the subject, I have published the act, feeling that its publication would be attended with less inconvenience than might happen if it were omitted.

Several laws passed with reference to the civil war through which we have just passed were, either in express terms, or from the nature of their subject-matter, limited to the period of that conflict. Others, though passed with reference to it, would remain permanent laws until repealed by the legislature. They were mostly arranged for the press before the termination of hostilities, and when it could not be foreseen how long the conflict might continue. Some of them might have been properly omitted, after the

The possession to authorize the exclusive right to pre-empt, shall be by actual residence on the land by the claimant, or his or her tenant.

Preference to actual occupant.

SEC. V. Any person holding any such forfeited lands by deed of conveyance made *bona fide*, and who have regularly listed and paid taxes on the same, but who have not such title and possession as will, under existing laws, vest them with title by reason of the forfeiture of the land, shall have the exclusive right, at any time within twelve months after the passage of this act, to pre-empt the land so held.

Claimant by deed to have the right to pre-empt the land for 12 mths.

SEC. VI. That in all cases where there are taxes due the state, on lands forfeited prior to the first day of January, 1834, and such lands have been granted by the state subsequently to such forfeiture, such grant shall operate as a release of all the taxes due upon that so granted, up to the date of such grant. And all grants that may be hereafter made of any such lands, shall in like manner operate as a release of the taxes due at the date of such grant. The release of taxes, as herein provided, shall inure to the benefit of the grantees, their heirs or assigns: *Provided*, That nothing herein contained shall prevent the state from proceeding to sell for taxes any lands not heretofore granted, and that shall not be entered and surveyed within twelve months from the passage of this act; after that time, the state may proceed to sell for taxes, in conformity with existing laws, any forfeited lands not granted, or entered and surveyed as herein provided.

Grant to operate a release of taxes

Proviso.

SEC. VII. This act shall take effect from and after its passage: *Provided, however*, That the provisions of this act shall in nowise apply to or affect an act passed at the present session of the general assembly, for the benefit of the heirs of John Moylan, deceased.

PREFACE.

war had virtually ceased, and I at first determined to drop them;
but, upon reflection, they seemed to me to possess a historical and
political interest which rendered their publication proper, even
though several of them had expired, and others should be imme-
diately repealed by the legislature. I have accordingly retained
them. They occupy but a few pages of the book.

The fees and salaries of the state and county officers were gener-
ally raised by acts of the last legislature—the acts, in some in-
stances, being limited to two or three years duration, and in others
being without limitation. These acts were of immediate and prac-
tical importance to those for whose benefit they were passed, and,
while in force, must be constantly before the courts and officers of
the state and counties. To omit them, it seemed to me, would have
been to have left the volume defective, and impaired its usefulness
for a number of years to come. I have, therefore, published them
also.

I have followed, as nearly as the subject-matter would admit,
those titles laid down in the REVISED STATUTES, even where they
did not seem to be exactly applicable, because the people of the
state were familiar with those titles, and would be guided by them
in searching any volume of the statute laws of the state. But in
many instances other titles had to be interposed, and in others, sub-
jects which were arranged as subdivisions of some general title of
the REVISED STATUTES, I have treated as independent titles; a
volume of this character not seeming to admit of general titles,
with subdivisions. Thus the subject of DIVORCE and DOWER, which
are subdivisions of the general title of HUSBAND AND WIFE in the
REVISED STATUTES, I have treated as independent titles; and so in
other cases.

Many acts could, with propriety enough, be placed under two or
more titles; in other instances, portions of an act were appropriate
subjects for one title, while other portions fell properly under some
other title. In these cases I have placed the act entire under one
title, and inserted the other title (where it was not already in) at its
proper place, with a reference to the title where the act could be

PREFACE. v

found. To facilitate these references, I have, where there is more than one act under a title, numbered them in the margin opposite the title of the act, and the references are to those numbers.

In arranging the acts, I have generally followed the chronological order in which they were passed, but in several instances have departed from this order, where perspicuity and the subject-matter of the acts seemed to call for that departure.

At the head of each act I have placed the date of its becoming a law; and where the title of the act did not indicate the general scope of its contents, I have placed in brackets a short note indicating the character and object of the law.

I found a multitude of acts changing and modifying the time of holding the CIRCUIT, EQUITY, AND CRIMINAL courts of the state, and enlarging or diminishing the length of the terms. To have published them as they were would have presented a chaotic and disorderly mass of acts of little practical use to any body. I have, therefore, undertaken to extract from them a concise statement of the TIME of holding those courts, and the LENGTH OF THE TERMS, in each county of the state, referring to the acts by which I was guided, which, I trust, will be found correct, and more acceptable than a publication of the numerous acts from which the statement is abstracted.

I have published the General Laws applicable to Jefferson County only, at the end of the volume, arranging them under such subdivisions as seemed appropriate.

MR. STANTON, in his edition of the Statutes, brought down the notes of the decisions of the Court of Appeals, construing the statute laws of the state to the second volume of METCALFE'S REPORTS. ## TIME OF HOLDING THE CIRCUIT, EQUITY AND CRIMINAL COURTS.

### FIRST DISTRICT.

*Hickman*—On the fourth Mondays in February and August, each term continuing twelve days.

*Fulton*—On the second Mondays of March and September, each term continuing twelve days.

*Ballard*—On the fourth Mondays in March and September, each term continuing twelve days.

*McCracken*—On the second Mondays in April and October, each term continuing thirty days.

*Graves*—On the third Mondays in May and November, each term continuing eighteen days.

*Calloway*—On the second Mondays in June and December, each term continuing twelve days.

*Marshall*—On the fourth Mondays in June and December, each term continuing twelve days. Act, February 18, 1860.

### SECOND DISTRICT.

*Trigg*—On the third Monday in February and the last Monday in August, each term continuing eighteen days.

*Christian*—On the third Mondays of March and September, each term continuing twenty-four days.

*Todd*—On the third Mondays in April and October, each term continuing eighteen days.

*Muhlenburg*—On the second Mondays in May and November, the May term continuing eighteen, and the November term twelve days.

# 1864.

AN ACT to revise, amend, and reduce into one the common school laws of Kentucky.

## ARTICLE I.
### SCHOOL FUND.

**SEC. I.** That the school fund is the fund dedicated by the constitution and the laws of this commonwealth to sustaining a system of common schools therein.

**SEC. II.** The auditor shall keep the accounts in relation to this fund. He shall, once in each month, make a transfer to the credit of said fund of all receipts into the treasury for the benefit of the common schools of the state, up to the date of such transfer. He shall allow no expenditures on that account beyond the annual revenue of the fund; and see that no county draws more than its proper proportion.

**SEC. III.** Annually, as soon after the fifteenth day of February as practicable, he shall apportion the revenue of the fund among the several counties of the state, according to the number of free white children in each between the ages of six and twenty years, as shown by the returns of the assessors in his office, or the reports of the school commissioners to the superintendent of public instruction, whichever is the greater in each county. If in any year the returns be not in by the fifteenth day of February, he shall use the returns of the last preceding year. He shall, at the same time, apportion the share of each county among the several school districts thereof in which a common school shall have been actually taught in that year, according to law, in proportion to the number of free white children in each district between the ages of six and twenty years, as shown by the report of the school commissioner to the superintendent of public instruction for that year, and, if no such report be in, then as shown by the report of that officer for the last preceding year.

SEC. VII. The auditor shall apportion each year the revenue from the fund realized under this act for the benefit of said paupers among the several counties of the state according to the number of said paupers in each county, as shown by the reports of the several county courts.

SEC. VIII. Nothing in this act shall be construed as interfering with the rights of the county courts in levying county taxes.

SEC. IX. This act shall take effect from its passage.

440 SCHOOLS AND SEMINARIES. [TITLE

lishing or sustaining any school or seminary other than common schools, in each county.

**Revenue to be paid into treas'ry**
SEC. V. The revenue of the school fund, and the proceeds of all taxes levied in aid thereof, together with all fines and forfeitures inflicted or granted for the benefit of the common schools of the state, shall be paid into the treasury, but shall never be drawn out or appropriated otherwise than pursuant to this chapter in aid of common schools.

**Balances due the counties to be transferred and bond given therefor.**
SEC. VI. On or before the first day of July, 1864, the board of education shall transfer to the commissioners of the sinking fund the surplus due the several counties of the state, for the several years intervening between the date of the last transfer of surplus and the aforementioned first day of July, 1864, and to this sum shall be added the surplus heretofore transferred, and for which bonds were issued by the governor, bearing date, respectively, August the twenty-third, 1855, and February the twenty-third, 1857; these bonds canceled, and one new bond issued by the governor for the whole amount of surplus due the counties for common school purposes, with a detailed statement of the amount due each county. In canceling the bonds referred to, dated, respectively, August the twenty-third, 1855, and February the twenty-third, 1857, the interest which shall have accrued, and remained unpaid, shall be set apart and credited to the counties of the state, entitled to draw it, and at the next regular apportionment and distribution of the revenue of the school fund of the state to the several counties thereof, shall be added to the distributable share of the counties of the state, entitled to draw it, and paid out at the same time and in the same way in which said distributable share is paid out. After the cancellation of these bonds, there shall never be more than one bond in existence at one and the same time, for surplus due the counties; but, annually, thereafter, on the same day of the same month on which this first cancellation takes place, a new transfer of surplus shall be made by the board of education to the commissioners of the sinking fund, the existing bond canceled, and a new bond issued for the whole amount of balances due the counties, with a detailed statement as heretofore mentioned.

**Thereafter only one bond for surplus due counties**

**Conditions of bond.**
SEC. VII. This bond shall not be transferable, shall be redeemable at the pleasure of the legislature, and shall bear six per cent. interest.

GUARANTY. ### 1866. *January 24.* AN ACT to define the liability of assignors and indorsers of promissory notes. SEC. I. That whenever a promissory note is made by the obligor payable to himself or to his order, and is signed on the back thereof by the said obligor, and then delivered, such signature and delivery shall operate as a promise to pay the face of the note at maturity to the party to whom the same shall have been delivered, and such party may fill up the blank with words of promise and recover thereon in the same manner as if such party had been named as payee in the note; and such note shall be assignable as are other promissory notes. Promissory note payable to maker's order good. SEC. II. That whenever a person or persons shall sign his or their name in blank upon the back of a promissory note, without being the payee in such note or the assignee thereof, and deliver the same, each signature and delivery shall authorize the *bona fide* holder of the same to fill up the blank with words of guaranty to himself or order. Indorsement of note by stranger good as a guaranty. SEC. III. This act shall take effect from and after its passage. Digitized by Google

All persons guilty of treason against the commonwealth, pardoned.
WHEREAS, The power to pardon persons who have committed treason against this commonwealth is, by the constitution, vested solely in the general assembly thereof; therefore,
SEC. I. *Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky*: That all persons who have, at any time heretofore, committed the offense or crime of treason against said commonwealth, be, and they are hereby, pardoned and absolved from all the pains and penalties thereto attached.

Randall*, 4 Met. 292. 4. *Thirty days not reasonable.*—The act of March 15, 1862, by which, after thirty days, the limitations contained in chapter sixty-three of the Revised statutes should extend to all cases, irrespective of when the right of action accrued, is unconstitutional and void. *Ib*. ## II. What cases they embrace. 5. *Actions pending when statute passed.*—The act of February 4, 1858, entitled "an act to amend the second section of article sixty-three of Revised Statutes, entitled 'limitation of actions and suits,'" does not embrace causes of action on which suits were pending at the time the act was passed. *Hedger v. Rennaker*, 3 Met. 255. 6. *Bar not complete when action brought.*—By chapter sixty-three, Revised Statutes, actions on notes are barred after fifteen years, except where the right accrued prior to the adoption of the statute. An amendatory act of February 4, 1858, extended the provisions of that chapter to all cases whether the right of action accrued before or after the Revised Statutes took effect, from and after the first of August, 1859. A suit brought before the last named day on a note due more than fifteen years before the commencement of the action, is not embraced by the law. *Fenwick v. Phillips*, 3 Met. 87. 7. *What cases embraced by act of 1858.*—The act of 1858, *supra*, was only intended to apply to such actions as were brought after the first of August, 1859. *Ib*. ## III. When and against whom the statute runs. 8. *Between tenant for years and remainderman.*—Where a slave was given to a married woman until her son attained to the age of twenty-one years, and then to the son, and the son permitted his mother and her husband to hold the slave more than five years after he was entitled to it: *Held*—that his right of action was barred. *Divine v. Bullock*, 3 Met. 418. 9. *Remainderman not barred by adverse possession during particular estate.*—The statute never runs against a party until his right of action accrues. REVIVOR. ### 1866. *January 19.* **1** AN ACT to amend the four hundred and thirty-seventh section, fourth chapter, "Revivor of Judgments," of the Civil Code of Practice. Stanton's Civil Code, page 190. Defendant dying after judgment, plaintiff may revive, by rule, against personal representatives and heirs. No revivor had until six months after qualification of representative, nor for twelve months against heir. Rule must be served ten days. **SEC. I.** That the four hundred and thirty-seventh section, chapter four, "Revivor of Judgments," of the Civil Code of Practice, be so amended, that when any or all of the defendants in an action against whom their is judgment, die after the rendition of the judgment, and before levy of execution issued thereon, or before the enforcement of the judgment, the plaintiff may, by rule in the action, revive the judgment against the personal representatives of the deceased defendant, and against his heirs, devisees, or legatees, jointly or severally; but no such judgment shall be revived, or execution issued thereon, or the judgment enforced against the personal representative of deceased defendant, until six months after his qualification; nor against the heir, devisee, or legatee until twelve months after the death of any such defendant, and not until the rule or notice to show cause why the judgment should not be revived is executed ten days before the motion to revive is made, and the rule returned executed, by delivering a copy to the persons against whom the judgment is sought to be revived. How motion docketed and tried. **SEC. II.** That the motion for the rule herein shall be placed on the motion docket of the court, and be heard as other motions; and the court may sit any day of the term to hear it, or may continue it until the next term, and may cause execution to issue without delay. **SEC. III.** This act shall take effect from its passage. ### 1866. *February 2.* **2** AN ACT to amend section five hundred and sixty-three of the Civil Code of Practice. Stanton's Code, page 228. Sureties. 11. *Forbearance induced by sureties.*—That the payee in a note was induced by the sureties to indulge them (the principal being insolvent), and the indulgence was thus extended beyond seven years, is not a defeating or obstructing suit upon the note within the meaning of the act of 1838, and can not therefore deprive the sureties of the right to rely upon that act in bar of the action. *Coleman v. Walker, 3 Met. 65.* 12. *"Defeat and obstruct"*—how construed.—The words defeat and obstruct as used in the act *supra* signifies such conduct on the part of the sureties as amounts to a hindrance or prevention of suit in opposition to the will of the creditor, and which he can not overcome by reasonable diligence. *Ib.* 13. *Liability incurred before Revised Statutes.*—Where the surety of an administrator incurred a liability before the Revised Statutes took effect, the provisions of these statutes do not apply. *Hayden v. Hayden, 4 Met. 189.* 14. *Infancy of distributees.*—To enable a surety in an administrator's or guardian's bond to rely upon the limitation prescribed in the second section of the act of 1838, he must aver and prove that five years had elapsed *after the youngest* of the distributees, devisees or wards had attained majority before suit was brought. *Ib.* 15. *Party held to be principal and not surety.*—Upon the separation of husband and wife a third party entered into an obligation indemnifying the husband against any claim of the wife upon him for alimony or dower: *Held*—that the obligor was not the surety of the wife but a principal, and that he could not rely upon the seven year statute in favor of sureties. *Gaines v. Poor, 4 Met. 503.* 16. *The same.*—A dower slave was sold by a doweress, and the slave removed from the state. The price was laid out by the doweress in other slaves: *Held*—that the reversioner's cause of action to recover the slaves so purchased, did not accrue until the death of the doweress, the forfeiture of the life estate having been waived. *Williams v. McClanahan, 3 Met. 420.* 17. *Actions to recover usury.*—Where the borrower pays the usury to the lender, who on the same day re-loans the principal and usury to him, in accordance with an agreement to that effect, the statute against the recovery of the usury begins to run from the day of such payment. *Hays v. Goodwin, 4 Met. 80.* 18. *The same—usury as a setoff.*—But if the period of such limitation is not complete when the lender sues the borrower upon the note given in renewal, the usury may be pleaded as a setoff, although at the time of filing the answer claiming such setoff the period of limitation had expired. *Ib.* 19. *Statute as against setoff.*—To authorize a setoff there must be mutual subsisting demands, constituting causes of action at the commencement of the suit, and limitation ceases against the setoff at the commencement of the original suit. *Ib.* Digitized by Google CXII.] SCHOOLS AND SEMINARIES. 447 said revenue. 298 LIMITATION. [TITLE

20. *Slaves*—*five years' adverse holding*.—Slaves were sold to the defendant by deed duly recorded, and he held them five years under the deed, claiming them as his own, the plaintiff residing in the same neighborhood: *Held*—that plaintiff's claim was barred. *Vandever v. Vandever*, 3 Met. 137.

21. *Presumption of payment*.—A mere lapse of fifteen years and a few days without execution upon a judgment does not raise a presumption of its payment. *Chiles v. Monroe*, 4 Met. 72.

22. *The same as to a judgment*.—An execution issued on a judgment on which no execution had previously issued for fifteen years and a month: *Held*—on a motion to quash the execution, that the lapse of time did not raise the presumption of payment, and that at the time this proceeding was commenced, there was no statute of limitation in force applicable to the judgment on which the execution issued, the judgment having been rendered before the Revised Statutes took effect. *Ib*.

### V. Trusts.

23. *Trustee barred, beneficiary barred also*.—When the legal estate is vested in a trustee for the benefit of another, it may be barred by the statute of limitation, though the beneficiary be an infant. And whenever the right of action on the part of the trustee is barred by time, the equitable right of the beneficiary is also barred. *Coleman v. Walker*, 3 Met. 65.

24. *And although beneficiary under disability*.—That the beneficiary of a note was an infant and *feme covert*—the note having been executed to her guardian for loaned money belonging to her, can not prevent the makers of the note from relying on the statute. *Ib*.

### VI. Statute must be pleaded.

25. The statute, if relied on, must be pleaded by the defendant, unless the petition shows that the action is barred and that the plaintiff is not entitled to the benefit of any of the exceptions. *Chiles v. Drake*, 2 Met. 146.

Penalty for willfully misstating facts.

I.** That master commissioners in chancery shall have power to issue subpenas for witnesses, whose evidence may be required, touching any matter pending before such commissioner, directed to any constable, sheriff, or marshal. Any person failing to attend, as required by such summons, when the same shall have been duly executed, shall be liable to be attached. The master commissioner shall have power to issue an attachment against the person so failing to appear in obedience to the summons, fixing the day for the return of the attachment, and allowing the person against whom the attachment issues to give bond, with security, for his or her appearance on the day fixed for the return of the attachment, in a penalty not exceeding $25. Upon the appearance of the person attached the commissioner shall have power to impose a fine for contempt of the summons, in any sum not exceeding ten dollars, or may discharge the party without fine, if in his judgment it would be proper. Commissioners shall, in cases where they impose a fine, report the same, together with a statement of the cost, to the circuit or chancery court of his county; and the court shall render judgment in conformity with the judgment of the commissioners, unless good reasons be shown why judgment should not be rendered. In case any person against whom an attachment is issued gives bond for his or her appearance, and fails to attend, the fact shall be certified on the bond by the commissioner, and the bond returned to the circuit or equity court of his county; and the court Com'rs in chan'y to have power to issue subpenas for witn'ses, issue attachments, etc. Impose fine. IV.** The commissioners of two adjoining counties, when the division line intersects a neighborhood, whose convenience requires it, may lay off a district composed of parts of both counties. XXXVI.] COURTS—COUNTY. 151

3. *Power of legislature unlimited except by constitution.*—The legislature may enact any law not forbidden in express terms or by fair implication, by the national or state constitution. *Ib.*

4. *Act to suspend courts constitutional.*—The first section of the act entitled "an act to suspend the circuit and other courts of this commonwealth, and for other purposes," approved May 24, 1861, which forbid the courts from rendering judgments for debts for a limited period, does not impair the obligation of contracts, and is valid. *Ib.*, and *Barclay v. Glover*, 4 Met. 44.

5. *Amount involved.*—The circuit court has jurisdiction where the principal of the debt sued on, and the interest which has accrued on it, exceed in the aggregate fifty dollars. *Bakewell v. Howell*, 2 Met. 268.

6. *Same—several claims united.*—Where several debts (promissory notes) amounting in the aggregate to more than fifty dollars, are united in the same paragraph of plaintiff's petition, although each debt is below that sum, the circuit court has jurisdiction. *Ib.*

7. *Boundary of Kentucky—jurisdiction.*—That the boundary of Kentucky extends to low-water mark on the northwestern side of the Ohio river, is well settled (5 Wheaton, 375; 4 J. J. Mar. 158). And the circuit courts of the counties bordering on the Ohio river, have jurisdiction of offenses against the laws of Kentucky, committed on the river south of the line of low-water mark. *McFall v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 394.

See CONSTITUTIONAL LAW, notes 6, 7, 8.

# TITLE XXXVI.

## COURTS—(COUNTY).

---

1. *Power to subscribe stock—strictly construed.*—The charter of a turnpike company authorized a county court to subscribe stock in the company. The power must be strictly pursued, and the only contract which the county court can make is a subscription of stock in the company. *Winchester, etc., T. P. Co. v. Clarke County Court*, 3 Met. 140.

2. *Subscription on unauthorized condition, void.*—In the case *supra* the county court, before the organization of the turnpike company made an order reciting that "to continue a turnpike road to Mount Sterling, to be expended between Winchester and the Montgomery county line, they (the court) pledged the county for $5000:" *Hold*—that the order is not a subscription of stock in the company either absolute or conditional, present or future, and imposes no liability on the court or the people of the county. *Ib.*

AN ACT to amend chapter eighty-six of the Revised Statutes. **3**
2 Stanton, p. 303.
Court may order lands of joint owners sold, and proceeds divided.

SEC. I. That chapter eighty-six of the Revised Statutes be, and the same is hereby, amended by adding the following section: When land is held by coparceners, joint tenants, tenants in common, or otherwise, by several parties, a court of equity having jurisdiction thereof may, upon the petition of a part of such owners against the others, order a sale of such land and a division of the proceeds among those entitled, where it shall have been made to appear to the court by competent proof that a division of the land would materially impair its value: *Provided, however*, If any party in interest shall, before the final trial of the action, appear and demand that his or her share of the land sought to be sold be laid off by partition, the court shall, by proper proceedings, direct the share of such person to be laid off.
Party objecting may have portion set apart.

SEC. II. All persons interested must be made parties to the proceeding, either by actual or constructive summons, as provided in title four, chapter two, article two, and section ninety, of the Civil Code of Practice.

SEC. III. Where some of the parties interested are infants or persons of unsound mind, or married women, the provisions of section five hundred and forty-three of the Civil Code of Practice shall be complied with, before a sale is ordered.
Parties under disability.

SEC. IV. This act shall take effect from its passage; and all acts and parts of acts conflicting with this act are hereby repealed.

How change location.

To select teachers

May appoint collector.

Shall invite all indigent children to attend school.
SEC. IX. It shall be the duty of the trustees to invite and encourage all the indigent children in the district to attend the school, and to inform them and their parents that such is their right, for which the state pays, though they themselves may contribute nothing toward paying the expense of the school. The annual report of the trustees shall always show that this duty has been performed; and no arrangement shall be made for the benefit of some individuals of this description to the exclusion of any others.

Shall visit the school weekly.
SEC. X. One of the trustees shall visit the school at least once in each week; see that the regulations for its government are complied with, and that the teacher performs his duty. They shall perform this duty in rotation, or otherwise as may be agreed upon. Upon complaint of the teacher, they shall have power to expel a scholar.

Shall report to commiss'r under oath, items, to-wit
SEC. XI. Immediately after the termination of the school year, to-wit, immediately after the 31st of December, each year, the trustees shall report to the county commissioner, under the oath of any two of them, or of one of them, provided he shall bring a certificate from both or either of the others that he is authorized to act for them, as follows, to-wit:
1. The number of free white children between the ages of six and twenty, residing in their district.
2. The whole time that a common school has been kept in the district during the year, by a qualified teacher.
3. The highest, lowest, and average number of children in attendance during that time.

Defective sales of infant's real estate, how remedied.
Purchaser may file petition.
What facts to be averred.
Judgment.

SEC. I. That in all cases wherein a judgment or decree for the sale of an infant's real estate has been rendered upon the petition of the guardian of such infant, and a sale has been made under such judgment or decree, and it shall appear that there are such errors or defects in the proceedings in which the judgment or decree was obtained as will or may vitiate the same, it shall be lawful for any purchaser of the estate, under such judgment or decree, to file his petition in the court rendering the same, making the infant and all other persons interested parties, reciting the facts of such proceeding, judgment, or decree, and sale, and averring that the sale was fairly made, and at the reasonable value of the property, that the sale was confirmed and the price paid by the purchaser, and that the sale was at the time beneficial to the infant, which petition shall be sworn to by the purchaser.

SEC. II. Upon the filing of such petition, and the parties being regularly before the court, it shall proceed to hear proof; and, if satisfied of the truth of the allegations, it shall render a judgment or decree confirming the original judgment or decree and sale, which shall render the same as valid and binding in all respects as if the original proceedings had been in every particular according to the requirements of the law.

SEC. III. This act shall take effect from its passage, and continue in force three years thereafter.

Ballard*, 2 Met. 177. 2. *Degree of negligence to make responsible.*—Where a railroad company is not bound to fence its road, it is not liable for stock straying on its track and killed by its trains, unless the injury was caused by the wanton and reckless negligence of those controlling the train. *Ib*. 3. *Fitness of employees may be inquired of on trial.*—The qualification of an engineer to discharge the duties of that place, and who was in charge of the engine at the time of the injury, is a proper subject of inquiry upon the trial of an action for damages resulting from the injury; and instructions limiting the inquiry of the jury as to whether the injury was the result of carelessness or negligence alone, are improper. *Bowler v. Lewis*, 3 Met. 384. 5. *Gross negligence and fraud—distinction.*—Gross negligence and fraud are not convertible terms. Gross negligence may conduce to prove fraud and want of good faith, but does not amount to actual or intentional fraud. *Ib*. 6. *Property destroyed while in possession of wrongdoer.*—Where a man tortuously remains in possession of real estate after his estate therein is terminated, and a building thereon in the possession of his tenant, is destroyed by fire, he is not liable therefor to the rightful owner, unless the fire was the result of the negligence or carelessness of himself or tenant. *Morrow v. Mason*, 2 Met. 114. See *Munford v. TEACHERS.

Examiners of teachers.
* **SEC. I.** The commissioners may appoint one or more competent persons as examiners of teachers, whose duty it shall be carefully to examine all applicants as to their qualifications to teach the elements of a plain English education. A certificate of qualification from an examiner shall constitute a qualified teacher for the county; or the examination may be made and the certificate granted by a commissioner. 1. *Amount in controversy.*—The Civil Code confers jurisdiction upon justices of the peace in actions for the recovery of money and personal property where the matter in controversy does not exceed $50. In determining the extent of the matter in controversy, the interest is always computed. *Fidler v. Hall, 2 Met. 461.*
2. *Same—amount more than $50.*—Where the amount in controversy was over $50, the justice of the peace properly dismissed the warrant, and the plaintiff can not obtain a judgment by appealing from the justice to a court which has jurisdiction of a sum greater than $50. *Ib.*
3. *Same—amount demanded in warrant.*—In an action before a justice of the peace, the amount demanded in the warrant is the "matter in controversy." The law in this respect has not been changed by the Civil Code *Burbage v. Squires, 3 Met. 77.*
4. *Transcript from their docket.*—A transcript of the record of proceedings in a case before a justice of the peace, certified by him to be a full and complete transcript, is admissible as evidence, although the transcript is in the form of a statement by the justice of what occurred in the case. *Commonwealth v. Foster, 3 Met. 1.*
5. *Written statement of amount in controversy.*—In the written statement of facts upon which an action before a justice of the peace is founded,

6. *Examining court*.—One justice of the peace can not act as an examining court and commit to jail, or hold to bail a person charged with felony; another justice must be associated with him. *Revill v. Pettit*, 3 Met. 314.

7. *Acting beyond jurisdiction a trespasser*.—A justice of the peace who, without associating with him another justice, assumes to act as an examining court, and holds a person to bail or commits him upon a charge of felony, transcends his authority and is liable for the trespass. *Ib*.

8. *Presumption in favor of jurisdiction*.—A recognizance taken by a single justice showed that the defendant was charged with "larceny," but whether grand or petit larceny did not appear: *Held*—that the presumption was that it was petit larceny, and that the justice did not, therefore, exceed his authority in holding to bail. *Thap v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 411.

308 MECHANICS' LIEN LAW. [TITLE

object of this amendment is to give, and hereby is given, to the persons performing labor or furnishing materials for the purposes mentioned herein, a lien therefor, precisely as mechanics, laborers, and others, have a lien under the act to which this is an amendment, and also the right to enforce the same under the provisions of said act.

SEC. III. This act shall be in force from its passage.

1. *Upon married woman's estate.*—The husband can not create a mechanic's lien upon the estate of his wife. *Pell v. Cole*, 2 Met. 252.

2. *The same.*—An action was brought to enforce a mechanic's lien upon the property of the wife, for buildings erected thereon, and for which she and her husband had executed a note. It did not appear that she procured the erection of the building, or that it was essential to the comfort or shelter of the family: *Held*—that no lien existed. *Ib.*

3. *Lien favored by the law.*—Debts owing to mechanics, for the construction or repair of houses in the city of Louisville, are favored by the law, and a preference is given to them over other debts to the extent of the value of the property improved, which had not been incumbered. *Brown v. Story*, 4 Met. 316.

4. *Mechanic's lien an incumbrance.*—A mechanic's lien upon a house constructed by him in the city of Louisville for the amount due him therefor, is an incumbrance within the meaning of chapter thirty-six, article fifteen, Revised Statutes. *Ib.*

5. *Levy upon property subject to this lien.*—Where property subject to a mechanic's lien is levied on and sold under execution, the purchaser only acquires a lien on the property for the purchase money and interest after the rate of ten per cent. per annum from the date of the sale till paid. *Ib.*

6. *The same—removing the lien.*—That the property was not levied on or sold as incumbered property, and, that after the sale, the purchaser removed the incumbrance, will not give him an absolute title. *Ib.*

7. *Other creditors—rights of.*—Other creditors may, before the purchaser has, *by suit*, removed the incumbrance, bring suit to subject the incumbered property, as authorized by section two, article fifteen, chapter thirty-six, Revised Statutes. *Ib.*

8. *Same—incumbrance removed without suit.*—But where the purchaser under the execution removes the mechanic's lien without suit, and sells the property before the creditors sue, the sale will stand, and a personal judgment be awarded against the party for whatever he has received above his debt and interest. *Ib.*

### 1862.
*August 31.*

AN ACT creating the office of corresponding secretary of state.* 1

SEC. I. That the governor of this commonwealth, be and he is hereby authorized, for the time being, to appoint an additional secretary of state in the executive department, to be styled the "Corresponding Secretary," if in the opinion of the governor the labors of that department require it; and to be discontinued by the governor whenever the services of said office can be dispensed with.

SEC. II. Said officer shall perform such duties as may be required by the governor, and shall, during the continuance of the office, receive out of the treasury an annual salary of one thousand dollars, to be paid to him as the salaries of other public officers are paid.

SEC. III. This act to take effect from and after its passage.

**Exemptions—how proved.**
SEC. VI. Notice shall be published in each county, in such manner as the governor may direct, of the time and place when and where those claiming to be exempt from the performance of military service shall meet the commissioner, and make known the causes and reasons for his exemption, and furnish evidence of their truth. If the commissioner, on investigation, shall be satisfied that the applicant is exempt, he shall note the fact and the cause opposite the name of the applicant upon the roll.

**Those now in service to be deducted.**
The commissioner or commissioners for the county shall write upon the roll opposite the name of any person who, at the time the roll is made, is then in the service of the United States, as an officer or soldier, the words "now in service;" and opposite the name of any person who is then a volunteer for three years' service, and has been enrolled as such upon the rolls of the proper recruiting officer, but has not yet been mustered into the service of the United States as officer or soldier, the words "enrolled as volunteer;" and opposite the name of any person who has served nine months or more in the army of the United States as officer or soldier, and who has resigned or been honorably discharged, the words "has served nine months." In ascertaining the number of persons subject to draft in the county, all those opposite whose names any one of the aforesaid expressions is written, shall be deducted from the whole number on the roll of the county, as well as those exempted from the performance of military service, and the remainder shall constitute the persons subject to draft in the county.

**Commis'r may obtain service of physician, and administer oath.**
The commissioner shall have power to call to his aid a competent practicing physician, and to administer an oath to the party, or to any witness he may produce to testify in his behalf, and if, on any such investigation, the party or witness shall, after being sworn, knowingly and willfully state as a fact any matter or thing which may be relevant, and which is untrue and false, with a view to influence the judgment of the commissioner, and to procure the exemption of the party from the draft, he or she so offending shall be guilty of perjury, and on conviction thereof shall suffer the pains and penalties prescribed by law for that crime.

Turnpike stock to be taxed only at its real value.
SEC. I. That all laws heretofore passed in relation to revenue and taxation, and mode of taking in taxable property, shall be so construed as only to require the owners of shares in turnpike road companies, as those the commonwealth owns stock in, to give in for taxation the number of shares, nominal value and real value thereof, and to pay the same taxes on the real value as on other property assessed on the *ad valorem* principle.

No toll exacted from children attending school.

If there be left in such division a portion of less than one hundred and one, such portion shall be again divided by the quotient of the first division, and the quotient of this second division added to one hundred and one, so as to fix and determine the number of men which each company in the county shall contain, subject to draft. VAGRANTS. 765 VACCINATION. See INOCULATION. --- 58. VAGRANTS. 1866. *February 17.* AN ACT to amend the vagrant laws. SEC. I. That if any able-bodied person be found loitering or rambling about, not having the means to maintain himself by some visible property, or who does not betake himself to labor or some honest calling to obtain a livelihood, or who, not possessing such means, has quit his habitation, leaving a wife or child without suitable means of subsistence, or who is idle or dissolute in habits without visible means of support, he shall be taken and adjudged to be a vagrant and guilty of a high misdemeanor. SEC. II. That all such persons may be apprehended by the warrant of a quarterly court judge, city judge, police judge, or justice of the peace, issued upon his own knowledge, or upon information upon oath, and directed to any sheriff, marshal, constable, or other officer authorized by law to execute process, who shall return the same forthwith, together with the prisoner, to a judge of the quarterly court, city or police court, and summon such witnesses as either party may require. The judge to whom the return shall be made shall cause a jury to be impaneled to try the question of guilt or innocence of the prisoner; and if found guilty, to fix by their verdict the time he shall be held or bound to labor, not exceeding twelve months, if the convict be over twenty-one years of age. If a minor, under twenty-one years of age, the jury shall return in their verdict his age. SEC. III. That the convicts over twenty-one years of age shall, by order of the court, be sent to houses of correction in counties and cities having such houses, there to be held to labor; and in counties and cities having no such house, the county court shall appoint a superintendent, requiring him to give bond and security for the faithful discharge of his duties, who shall, upon the order Who are vagrants. [TITLE ## I. *Cutting and shooting.* 1. *Intent to kill.*—An intent to kill is not made an ingredient of the offense of stabbing described in section one, of article seventeen, Revised Statutes, and an omission of the words, "in sudden heat and passion," in an instruction on the trial of an indictment for that offense, is not prejudicial to the defendant. *Tyra v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 1. 2. *Degrees of offense charged in the indictment.*—A defendant indicted for stabbing with intent to kill, may be found guilty of the felony or of any lower degree of that offense. *Ib.* 1. ## II. *Assault and battery.* 3. *Committed on slave indictable.*—An assault and battery committed on a slave is an indictable offense. *Commonwealth v. Lee & Bledsoe*, 3 Met. 229. 4. *What justifies assault upon slave.*—Provocation which would not justify an assault by one white man upon another, would justify it when committed on a slave. *Ib.* ## III. *False Pretenses.* 5. *Sale by sample—fraud not false pretense.*—The defendant represented that he had a quantity of tobacco of the quality and description of a sample he then exhibited, and by these representations induced his creditor to buy so much of the tobacco as would pay the balance due on a note which he held on the defendant and surrender the note to the defendant. The tobacco was not of the quality or description of the sample, but was worthless, which the defendant knew. *Held*—that these facts did not amount to a false pretense within the meaning of the statute. *Commonwealth v. Haughey*, 3 Met. 223. 6. *Boundary between fraud and false pretense.*—It is difficult to lay down any rule as to the boundary between those frauds which amount to false pretenses and are indictable, and those which only amount to unfair dealing and imposition. *Ib.* 7. *Indictment must state what the pretenses were.*—An indictment which merely charges that the defendant obtained the goods or money by false pretenses, without specifying what the pretenses were, is insufficient. And the pretenses must be proved as laid. *Glacken v. If there be a number left when the whole number of the company is divided by the number of a class, the number so left shall be denominated supernumeraries, and these supernumeraries shall be taken out of the company by lot before the draft takes place. **Supernumeraries** Those who are supernumeraries, and those who shall constitute a class, shall be determined by lot in the following manner: There shall be as many tickets formed of white paper, of equal size, as there are members in the company. The name of a member of the company shall be written on one of the tickets, so that every member's name is written on a separate ticket. The tickets shall then be folded up in the same way as to conceal the name written thereon, and so as to make them after they are folded, as much alike as possible. That after the payment of the costs and fees of the prosecution, and a reasonable allowance to the superintendent for his services, to be made by the court, the net proceeds of the labor of the convict shall go to his family, if he has a family; if no family, then to the payment of his just debts pro rata; or if not required to pay his debts, it shall be paid into the county treasury as a fund to erect houses of correction, or to be applied by order of the county court in such other manner as it may consider best to protect the county against vagrancy. SEC. IV. That in case any convict shall escape from service, he shall be bound to serve double the time he had to serve at the time of his escape; and an attested copy of the judgment of conviction shall be authority to any sheriff, marshal, constable, or other like officer in this state, to arrest and return said convict to the keeper of the house of correction, or to the hirer (as the case may be), who shall be entitled to the same fees now allowed by law for arresting and conveying criminals, to be paid out of the proceeds of service or hire. SEC. V. That all persons under twenty-one years of age, convicted of vagrancy, shall be bound out as an apprentice to a master, until the age of twenty-one years, to learn some trade or business, upon the terms which may be agreed upon. The county court clerk shall prepare and keep the indenture, which, when signed, shall have the same effect as indentures of apprenticeship in other cases, and for which the clerk shall have the same fees. SEC. VI. That all persons having the custody and control of vagrants convicted under the provisions of this act shall have the same rights and powers, and be under the same responsibilities, as masters of servants and of apprentices during the time for which they have been hired or bound to them. III. AGENT OF THE STATE. 17 against the United States, whether the same is not in his hands for collection. SEC. V. Any violation by said agent of the provisions of the preceding sections shall be regarded as a misdemeanor, and punished by fine and imprisonment, or both, at the discretion of a jury. He may be indicted in the circuit court of the county where the claimant or soldier resides, or in the circuit court of the county where the agent resides. SEC. VI. Before entering on the discharge of the duties of his office, he shall take the oath required by the constitution of other officers of this state, and shall execute a covenant, with sufficient security, to be approved by the governor, conditioned that he will faithfully and diligently discharge the duties of his said office; make full, fair, and complete returns of all claims and funds which may come into his hands, or may be intrusted to him, and pay over to the claimants all moneys and effects which he may receive for such claimant; and that he will not, directly or indirectly, purchase, trade, or deal in any claim whatever against the United States—which covenant shall be filed and preserved in the office of the secretary of state, and upon which any aggrieved may maintain a suit for any breach thereof; and any recovery on the same shall not bar any other suit upon the same for any other and distinct cause of action. SEC. VII. He shall receive, as compensation for his services under this act, three hundred and thirty-three dollars and thirty-three and one third cents per month, payable out of the public treasury, as the salaries of other public officers are paid; and he shall be allowed one clerk, at a salary of one hundred and twenty-five dollars per month, payable as the salary of the agent is paid. SEC. VIII. 168 CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS. [TITLE

18. *Embezzlement by agent of corporation.*—The agent of a corporation who fraudulently converts money to his own use "which has been placed under his care and management as such officer," is, under chapter twenty-eight, article twelve, section one, Revised Statutes, guilty of felony, no matter for what purpose it was placed under his care. *Ib.*

49. *Offense defined—agent of private person.*—As to the agent of an individual or private banker the rule is different. Under the second section of the above article the offense defined and punished, consists in fraudulently converting to his own use, money, etc., "before delivery thereof at the place or to the person to whom the same was to be delivered." Under this section the money must have been intrusted to the agent *for delivery* at some place or to some person, in order to constitute the conversion thereof a felony. *Ib.*

20. *Clerk of private banker.*—The clerk of a private banker converted to his own use money which he charged to a depositor on the books of the bank: *Held*—That the offense was not a felony. *Ib.*

## VIII. Gaming.

21. *Gaming with negroes—two degrees of offense.*—An offense of two degrees is defined by the first and second sections of the act of 1858, against gaming with negroes. *Commonwealth v. Garland, 3 Met. 478.*

22. *Offense defined.*—To constitute the higher offense it is necessary that "money or other thing of value," shall have been bet, won, or lost, irrespective of the amount of money or value of the property, and it is not necessary to prove the value as laid. To convict of the lower offense it is only necessary to prove the gaming. *Ib.*

23. *Under indictment for higher, conviction may be for lower degree.*—Under an indictment charging the higher offense under the above statute, the defendant may be convicted of the lower offense. *Ib.*

## IX. Bribery.

24. *Meaning of the term "bribe."*—The term "bribe," as used in the election laws, has a legal signification, and is defined in section eleven, article twelve, of said law. *Commonwealth v. Stephenson, 3 Met. 226.*

25. *Sufficiency of indictment.*—That an election was held at the time and place charged in the indictment appears sufficiently from the averment that the person bribed, did, for said bribe, *vote* for the several persons named in the indictment, and for the several offices. *Ib.*

## X. Peddling without license.

*Indictment for, must state the particular acts.*—The statutes which define who are peddlers, also by necessary implication, specify what acts constitute peddling, and an indictment for peddling without a license must state the particular act or acts intended to be relied on, or it will be insufficient. *Commonwealth v. Dudley, 3 Met. 221.*

XIII. Witness—competency of.

41. *One of two jointly indicted, not competent for the other.*—One of several joint defendants in an indictment, is not a competent witness for his co-defendants, even where they are tried separately. This rule does not universally exclude the wife of one defendant as a witness for another defendant, where they are tried separately. *Cornelius v. Commonwealth, 3 Met. 481.*

42. *Wife of one jointly indicted with another, competent for the latter.*—Where two persons were jointly indicted for larceny and embezzlement, and were tried separately, the wife of one is a competent witness for the other. *Ib.*

XIV. Evidence.

(1.) CONFESSIONS—

43. *Improperly obtained—inadmissible.*—Confessions improperly obtained, as by a promise of pardon, are not admissible as evidence; but facts which have been brought to light in consequence of such confessions, are competent. *Jane v. Commonwealth, 2 Met. 30.*

44. *Must be voluntary—not obtained by threat or promise.*—The confession of a prisoner to be admissible evidence against him, must be voluntary—not procured by improper influence, nor by threats or promises. *Ib.*

45. *Confession ascertained to be certainly true—admissible, though improperly obtained.*—But where the statement of a prisoner though obtained improperly, is ascertained to be certainly true, the reason for excluding it does not apply. *Ib.*

- For an act allowing mileage to sheriffs of election for transmitting poll-books, see **ELECTIONS**, 5.
- Sheriff's office vacant, court may appoint elisor. See **ELISOR**.
- Relieving sheriff from thirty per cent. damage for failing to return execution in certain cases, see **EXECUTION**, 4.
- Sheriff to have copies of acts of the legislature, see **LAWS**, 3.
- How sheriff to proceed in collecting revenue, see **REVENUE AND TAXATION**, 5, 6, 7, 11.
- Proceedings against sheriff in default in paying over revenue, see **REVENUE AND TAXATION**, 15, 16.

---

### 1. *Eligibility to office: de facto sheriff.*

1. *Sheriff must be resident of county.*—No person is constitutionally eligible to the office of sheriff who is not a resident of the county in which he is elected, and has been a resident thereof for one year next before the election. *Patterson v. Miller*, 2 Met. 493.

2. *Election of unqualified person—effect.*—Although a person, ineligible to the office of sheriff, be nevertheless elected, obtain a certificate of his election from the examining board, execute the bond, and take the oath of office, he does not thereby become the lawful incumbent of the office. *Ib.*

3. *Acts of usurper—how considered.*—The acts of such ineligible person, are valid as to third parties, and can not be questioned collaterally; but as to the usurper himself they are invalid, and in an action against him for trespass for acts done in his supposed official capacity, it may be shown collaterally that he was ineligible. *Ib.*

4. *Certificate of election—not proof of eligibility.*—The certificate of the examining board, issued to a candidate that he has been elected to an office, is not even *prima facie* evidence of his eligibility to the office. *Ib.*
