172 CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS. [TITLE

## XV. Accessories.

57. *Slave may be indicted as accessory.*—A slave may be indicted and tried as an accessory before the fact to the crime of administering poison with intent to kill, and the accessory may be indicted with the principal. *Commonwealth v. Anthony*, 2 Met. 399.

58. *Punishment of slave accessory.*—In such a case the punishment of the accessory only extends to the infliction of stripes, not exceeding one hundred. *Ib.*

## XVI. Offenses by slaves.

59. *What statutes apply to crimes by slaves.*—None of the provisions of the chapter on crimes and punishments of the Revised Statutes, not in terms applicable to slaves, apply to their punishment, nor change any rule of law in relation to their punishment. *Commonwealth v. Anthony*, 2 Met. 399.

60. *Larceny by slave a misdemeanor.*—Larceny committed by a slave amounts to a misdemeanor only. *Munford v. Taylor*, 2 Met. 599.

61. *Slaves as accessories to crime.*—A slave may be indicted and tried as an accessory before the fact to the crime of administering poison with intent to kill, committed by another slave, and both may be united in the same indictment. *Commonwealth v. Anthony*, 2 Met. 399.

62. *Punishment of accessory.*—The punishment of such accessory only extends to the infliction of stripes, not exceeding one hundred, as prescribed in chapter ninety-three, Revised Statutes, article seven, section six. *Ib.*

## XVII. Conviction and sentence—construction and effect of.

63. *Two terms of imprisonment—both run at same time.*—Before the adoption of the Code there was no law in force in this state authorizing the courts to adjudge that a term of imprisonment should commence at a future period, or after the expiration of a previous term of confinement. *James v. Ward*, 2 Met. 271.

64. *The same—five years on each indictment means five years only.*—Where, by a verdict on two indictments, a prisoner was found guilty of separate felonies and his punishment assessed by the jury at five years' imprisonment on each, upon which judgment was rendered that "it is considered by the court that the prisoner be confined in the jail and penitentiary house of this commonwealth to hard labor *for the space of five years upon each indictment*:" *Held*—that the legal effect of the judgment was that the prisoner was to be confined five years only; and that both terms of confinement began and terminated at the same time. *Ib.*

65. *Degrees of offense.*—A person indicted for an offense of different degrees, may be convicted of any degree not higher than that charged in the indictment; and of any offense included in the offense charged. *Commonwealth v. Garland*, 3 Met. 478.

472 SHERIFFS. [TITLE

after paying those which had been previously allowed. But when a claim is allowed at the time the levy is imposed, it is not necessary to aver in the notice that the sheriff has collected enough of the county levy to pay it and the other claims allowed. *Thompson v. Healy*, 4 Met. 257.

15. *When sheriff prima facie liable.*—When the county levy has been imposed, and a list of the persons chargeable therewith and of the county debtors and creditors, has been delivered to the sheriff, he is, after the first day of the next October, *prima facie* liable to those whose claims were ordered to be paid at the time the levy was imposed. *Ib.*

16. *Proof on trial of motion.*—On the trial of a motion by county creditor against the sheriff and his sureties, the record containing the list of claims may be read in evidence, and it may be proved by the clerk that he delivered a copy thereof to the sheriff without producing the copy. *Ib.*

17. *What notice must aver.*—The plaintiff must aver in his notice, and prove every fact necessary to show the sheriff liable. He must aver that the list of the persons liable to the payment of the levy, the sum to be paid by each, and a list of the sums due, and from whom due to the county, have been delivered by the clerk to the sheriff. *Ib.*

18. *Demand on deputy.*—In such cases a demand upon the sheriff is necessary, and a demand upon his deputy is not sufficient. *Ib.*

19. *Where no demand can be made.*—If a demand can not be made upon the sheriff, the creditor can recover from his sureties the debt without damages in an ordinary action, upon showing that the sheriff did, or might have collected a sufficient sum to pay the county creditors. *Argu.* *Ib.*

XXXIX.] CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS. 173

**XVIII. Appeals. Bills of exceptions. Immaterial errors.**

66. *What errors will reverse.*—A judgment will not be reversed because of an error in rejecting evidence merely pertinent or relevant, or technically admissible. It must be *important* to the party seeking the reversal, in view of the whole case. *Champ v. The Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 17.

67. *Exceptions*—*when to be taken.*—To authorize a reversal of a judgment convicting of felony because of an error in admitting important testimony, the record must show that exceptions were taken thereto at the time. *Clem v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 10.

68. *What bill of exceptions must show.*—The court of appeals can not disturb a judgment of conviction for errors in instructing and refusing to instruct the jury, unless it appears from the record that it furnishes every instruction given to the jury. *Ib.*

69. *The same.*—Where the bill of exceptions states that "the court gave the following instructions,"—going on to enumerate them—"to each and all of which the defendant objected, but the court overruled the objection and the defendant at the time excepted and still excepts:" *Held*—That the bill does not in terms, or by fair deduction, show that it contains *all* the instructions. *Ib.*

70. *Exceptions*—*when to be taken.*—Unless the bill of exceptions show that the defendant objected to instructions moved by the commonwealth and excepted to the ruling of the court overruling his objection at the time, he will be considered as having waived any error committed by the court to his prejudice. *Burns v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 13.

**XIX. Escape. Surrender of prisoner into custody.**

71. *Surrender of prisoner by bail*—*what amounts to.*—A defendant appeared upon the day named in the recognizance and pleaded to the indictment, was put upon trial and by order of court was placed in the custody of the sheriff during the trial. After the jury had retired he escaped from the sheriff and failed to appear for judgment upon the verdict found: *Held*—That the sureties in his recognizance were not liable. *Commonwealth v. Coleman*, 2 Met. 382.

72. *The same.*—But the mere appearance of the defendant in discharge of his recognizance, until the court orders him into custody, does not discharge the sureties. *Ib.* 382.

See **APPEALS**, notes 10, 11, 12, 13. **CONSTABLE**, notes 2, 3. **CONSTITUTIONAL LAW**, note 25. **GAMING**, notes. **EXAMINING COURT**, notes 1, 2, 3, 4.

AN ACT to amend chapter twenty-eight of the Revised Statutes, article nine, title "Forgery and Counterfeiting."

Punishment for forging, etc., U.S. Treas'y notes or postal currency.

**SEC. I.** If any person shall forge, counterfeit, or simulate any United States treasury note, or any United States fractional or postal currency; or shall erase or alter the same; or shall tender in payment, utter, vend, exchange, barter or demand to have exchanged or bartered for money or other thing of value, any such erased, altered, counterfeited, or simulated treasury note, fractional or postal currency, or have the same in his possession, or under his control, with intent so to pass or use the same, knowing the same to be so forged, counterfeited, erased, altered, or simulated, he shall be confined in the penitentiary, upon indictment and conviction, not less than two nor more than ten years.

IX.** The intention of this act is not, at present, to assume the payment of all the claims due to all the loyal citizens of Kentucky, embraced in the above provisions; but it is to legalize a mode of ascertaining the amount of such claims in the state, and to whom due, and to legalize a mode of proving the same without any cost to the state, so that any future legislature may be able to act advisedly should the state choose hereafter to consider the question of assuming to pay these claims, and look to the general government for reimbursement.

AN ACT to amend section six hundred and eleven, civil code of practice. **3**

SEC. I. That section six hundred and eleven of the civil code
of practice, be amended by adding to said section the following,

1861.
October 3.

*[Taken out of the state—how transmitted.]*

AN ACT to amend the law regulating the taking of depositions out of this state.

That where depositions have been taken out of this state to be used in this commonwealth, any such deposition or depositions may, after being sealed up by the officer taking the same, and directed to the clerk, or court, or justice, where or before whom the action is pending, in which the deposition is to be used, be delivered to the party taking the same, his agent or attorney; and when deposited by such party, his agent or attorney, with such clerk, court, or justice, shall be sufficient to authorize the reading of such deposition or depositions upon the trial, if, in other respects, they have been legally taken: *Provided*, That the person or persons conveying and delivering the depositions shall take an oath that the same have not been opened by him, nor by any other person, to his knowledge or belief.

AN ACT providing for the investment of certain funds belonging to the state, with a view to increasing the resources of the sinking fund. Preamble.

WHEREAS, a large sum in coin will shortly be realized to the commonwealth, from the stock which it owns in the Southern Bank of Kentucky, the profits on which stock formed a large part of the resources of the sinking fund, and to prevent the diminishing of that fund— Commissioners of sinking fund may sell coin belonging to state.

SEC. I. That the commissioners of the sinking fund may, as soon as the same, or any part thereof, shall be realized, proceed to sell said coin, on the best terms to be had in the money market, for United States currency, and then to pay the banks of this state any portion of the military loan due said banks by the state, and to invest the balance in Kentucky state bonds, the bonds of the United States, known as "5-20 bonds," or other government securities; and that the interest in these bonds, when paid, shall become a part of the sinking fund of this commonwealth.

SEC. II. This act shall take effect from its passage.

**County court clerk to make annual return of militia.**
SEC. IV. That it shall be the duty of the clerk of every county court to transmit to the adjutant general of the state, prior to the first day of September, in every year, an annual return, stating the number of militia of such county that have been enrolled; also, a list of such as are members of the active militia.

SEC. V. That, for the services required by this act of the assessor and county court clerk, there shall be allowed to each of those officers the sum of one cent for each name returned by the assessor and enrolled by the clerk.

**S** AN ACT to provide funds for paying troops raised for defense of the state.

SEC. I. That for the purpose of providing means for raising, equipping, paying, subsisting, and any other necessary expenses of the troops which the present general assembly by act approved January —, 1864, authorized the governor to raise for the defense of the state, it shall and may be lawful for the governor to borrow, for and on behalf of the state from any bank, banking association, corporation, or from private individuals, any sum of money, not exceeding in the aggregate five millions of dollars, which shall be appropriated to the objects above mentioned and no other.

Bonds 10-30 years
SEC. II. That the loans authorized by this act shall not be for a longer period than thirty years, nor less than ten years—the state reserving to itself the right, at its option, at any time after ten years from the date of any such loan, to redeem the bonds which may be issued therefor, at their par value.

Interest payable semi-annually.
SEC. III. The governor, for the money which he may borrow under the provisions of this act, shall issue and deliver to the lender the bond or bonds of the state, payable to him or bearer, bearing interest at the rate of six per centum per annum, payable semi-annually, on the first Mondays in January and July, at the public treasury of the state, or at such other place as the governor may contract. For the semi-annual interest, proper coupons shall be attached to the bonds so issued, signed by the secretary of state; and the principal and interest of said bonds shall be a charge upon the sinking fund of the state.

Bonds to be registered in office of secretary of state
SEC. IV. An accurate register of the sums of money borrowed under the provisions of this act, the date of the loan, the name of the lender, and the amount loaned by him, together with the date, number, and amount of each bond which may be issued for said loans, and when due, shall be kept in the office of the secretary of state—duplicates of which shall be filed and faithfully preserved in the offices of the treasurer and auditor of public accounts.

APPEAL.
[TITLE

I. APPEALS TO INFERIOR COURTS.

II. APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS.

I. *To Inferior Courts.*

1. *Where court appealed from had no jurisdiction*—If the quarterly court has no jurisdiction of the case, no recovery can be had upon an appeal to the circuit court although the circuit court would have had jurisdiction of the case had the action been brought in it in the first instance. In such a case the circuit court should make such orders as will render the proceedings in the quarterly court inoperative. *Wigginton v. Moss*, 2 Met. 38.

2. *Same*—*what order should be made on the appeal*.—Upon an appeal from the judgment of a justice of the peace, if it appear that the justice had no jurisdiction, the court should dismiss the suit. *Fleming v. Limebaugh*, 2 Met. 265.

3. *Amount in controversy*.—The amount in controversy was over $50; and the magistrate dismissed the warrant; the plaintiff appealed to the quarterly court, which likewise dismissed him, and he appealed to the circuit court which gave him judgment. The action of the justice and quarterly court was correct, and the circuit court should have dismissed the case for want of jurisdiction. *Fidler v. Hall*, 2 Met. 461.

4. *Upon trial of appeal, cause of action can not be changed*.—Upon the trial of an appeal from a justice of the peace, the cause of action must be the same as that set out in the warrant. The same action is to be tried anew; but no new action can be tried, neither can the demand be increased by pleading or otherwise beyond the jurisdiction of the justice. *Burbage v. Squires*, 3 Met. 77.

5. *Appeal from writ of ad quod damnum*.—Sections 30 and 31 of chapter 103 *Revised Statutes*, allowing either party to traverse an inquest on a writ of *ad quod damnum*, and an appeal from the decision on the traverse, are not repealed by the civil code. *Robb v. Turnpike Co.*, 3 Met. 117.

6. *Cost on appeal*.—On the trial of an appeal from the judgment of a justice, if the judgment is more favorable to the appellant than the one appealed from, he is entitled to his cost. *Kellar v. Bate*, 3 Met. 130.

7. *Appeals in will cases*.—On an appeal from the order of a county court admitting an instrument to record as a will, it is unnecessary to file a petition. A transcript of the proceedings of the county court, with the names of the appellants and appellees must be filed with the clerk of the circuit court, and process served on the appellees. *Jones v. Jones*, 3 Met. 266.

8. *In road cases*.—An appeal lies to the circuit court from an order of the county court allowing a gate to be erected across a public road. *Bond v. Mullins*, 3 Met. 282.

VII.] APPEAL. 31

## II. To the Court of Appeals.

9. *When supersedeas to issue.*—The clerk of the court of appeals has no authority to issue a supersedeas until a copy of the record has been filed in his office. *Hunt v. Berryman*, 2 Met. 239.

10. *An attorney may appeal from order striking his name from rolls.*—An appeal lies to the court of appeals from an order of a circuit court striking the name of an attorney at law from the rolls of such court upon charges of malpractice. *Turner v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 619.

11. *In misdemeanors, record must be brought up in sixty days.*—Neither the commonwealth nor the defendant can maintain an appeal from a judgment on a prosecution for a misdemeanor, unless the record is filed in the clerk's office of the court of appeals within sixty days after the rendition of the judgment. *Commonwealth v. McCready*, 2 Met. 376.

12. *Rule supra can not be changed by agreement of counsel.*—In the case *supra*, the fact that the counsel on both sides agreed to the entry of an order on the record allowing time until the next term of the court to prepare and file the bill of exceptions, does not change the rule. *Ib.*

13. *Immaterial errors.*—A party whose substantial rights have not been prejudiced by a judgment, can not maintain an appeal therefrom. *Combs v. Draining Co.*, 3 Met. 72.

14. *Order suspending jailer may be appealed from.*—A jailer, who has been permanently suspended in the exercise of his office by an order of the county court, may maintain an appeal from such order. *Low v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 237.

15. *Appeals from judgment of Jefferson county court.*—Where the matter in controversy is over $20, an appeal lies to the court of appeals from a judgment of the Jefferson county court. *Gross v. Jones*, 3 Met. 295.

16. *When court of appeals has no jurisdiction.*—Where the judgment appealed from is less than $50, and was not reduced below that sum by counter-claim or setoff, the court of appeals has no jurisdiction. *Revell v. Pettit*, 3 Met. 314.

17. *What is a final order.*—An order dissolving an injunction before the disposition of the case in which it was granted, is not final and can not be appealed from. The party should apply for its reinstatement as authorized by the code, sections 326, 327. *Rodman v. Forline*, 2 Met. 325.

18. *The same.*—Where an order overruling a motion for a new trial, does not finally dispose of the case, no appeal can be maintained from it. *Fernold v. Speer*, 3 Met. 459.

19. *The same.*—A judgment giving priority to one creditor over another out of an attached fund, but which does not distribute the fund nor give other relief to either party, is not final. *Hanson v. Bowman*, 4 Met. 108.

See CRIMES, PENALTIES, ETC., Notes 66, 67, 68, 69, 70.

1.  *Contradicting certificate of examiner.*—Where a deposition was taken before a justice of the peace in another state, who certified that the statements of the witnesses were reduced to writing by him, it was held that the opinions of an expert in handwriting, not acquainted with that of the magistrate or witnesses and who had never seen them write, are not competent to show that the statements of the witnesses were not in their own hand writing nor that of the magistrate. *Daniel v. Toney*, 2 Met. 523.

2.  *How certificate amended.*—An amendment or alteration in the certificate of the examiner is not allowable after the deposition is filed, without leave of the court entered on the record. *Hall v. Renfro*, 3 Met. 51.

3.  *Amendment without leave of court.*—Where exceptions were sustained to a deposition two years before the trial, the deposition and certificate amended without leave of court or knowledge of the opposite party, and then placed in the papers of the cause after the parties had announced themselves ready for trial, exceptions to the deposition after the jury were sworn were properly sustained. *Ib.*

4.  *Exceptions not acted on.*—Exceptions to depositions not acted on by the circuit court, can not be noticed by the court of appeals. *Corn v. Simms*, 3 Met. 391.

5.  *Error waived if not excepted to.*—Where the circuit court has erroneously decided upon exceptions, still the error is waived, unless excepted to at the time, and can not be considered by the court of appeals. *Ib.*

6.  *Party to ordinary action not required to give deposition.*—The Code

1. *What law governs.*—The estate of a person who died before the adoption of the Revised Statutes, passed by the law in force at his death. *White v. White*, 2 Met. 18

2. *Law of domicil as to movables.*—Movable property of a decedent is distributed as prescribed by the law of the country in which he was domiciled at the time of his death. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352.

3. *Death of infant without issue—who inherits.*—Where an infant died without issue and intestate in 1829 (his father being dead), his slaves passed to his mother, brothers and sisters. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

1860.
*March 3.*

*[Officers to create no debt on behalf of state.]*

**1** AN ACT prescribing the duties of the managers, trustees, etc., of the state institutions.

> No trustee, manager, or officer of any of the state institutions to have power to create any debt against the state.

SEC. I. That no officer, manager, or trustee of the lunatic asylums, or of the deaf and dumb or blind institutions, or any other state institution, shall have power or authority to create any indebtedness on behalf of the state for the benefit of said institution, either by improvement or building, or by the purchase of land or other thing beyond the amount of the appropriation made each year by the legislature.

## *SLAVES, RUNAWAYS AND FREE NEGROES.

1860.
*March 3.*

*[Emancipated slaves to leave the state. Free negro coming into state guilty of felony. Free negro going out of state not to return.]*

**1** AN ACT concerning free negroes, mulatoes, and emancipation.

> No slave can be emancipat'd until bond is given to rem've such slave beyond the limits of the state.

SEC. I. That hereafter no slave shall be deemed to be emancipated by the laws of this state, until the person emancipating such slave, or some person for him, as principal, with good and sufficient resident security, before the county court of the county of his residence, shall give a covenant to the commonwealth, covenanting that such person shall remove such slave (naming him or her, and giving the age, color, height, and weight) beyond the limits of this state within ninety days after the approval of such covenant by said county court; nor until such slave shall subscribe a written statement, to be indorsed on such covenant, that he or she does then forever abandon his or her residence in, and will, within the time named, remove from this state, which subscription shall be attested by the clerk of such court; upon the breach of such covenant, it shall be the duty of the attorney for the commonwealth of the district, and the attorney for such county, or either of them, to institute suit upon the same for the recovery of such damages as may be found to be due: *Provided*, That the amount of damages shall not be less than the value of such slave at the time of his or her emancipation, which damages shall be paid into the county treasury, after deducting therefrom twenty per cent. as a compensation for the attorney or attorneys by whom the suit is prosecuted, and also ten dollars to be paid to the person or persons who may have given information of the breach of the covenant.

> Nor until slave subscribe a written statement that he does forever abandon his or her residence in this state.

15. *Question of divorce and of property distinct*—need not be tried together.—In such case it is not necessary to postpone the trial of her suit for divorce until the issue between her and the claimant shall be ready for trial. *Ib.*

16. *No attachment or injunction necessary.*—As between husband and wife, it is unnecessary to have an order of attachment or injunction, or that the property shall be referred to in the petition for a divorce to entitle her to an order for its restoration. *Ib.*

17. *Purchaser of wife's property with notice.*—The statute provides that "sales and conveyances made by a husband to a purchaser with notice and with intent to defraud or hinder the wife, shall be void as against such wife." Actual notice to the purchaser that the wife has sued for a divorce and that she claims the property, is sufficient, without any order of attachment or injunction. *Ib*

### V. Costs and fees.

18. *Husband must pay wife's cost and counsel fees unless, etc.*—In a suit for a divorce the husband must pay the wife's costs, including her attorney's fee unless she is in fault and has ample estate to pay the same. *Mayer v. Mayer*, 3 Met. 298. *Ballard v. Caperton*, 2 Met. 412.

19. *But wife not entitled to judgment for cost where she fails.*—But a wife suing for a divorce and failing to obtain it is not entitled to judgment for her cost against her husband. The officers have a right to look to her husband for her cost. *Nickirk v. Nickirk*, 3 Met. 432.

3. *What is a jointure.*—Any conveyance or devise of real or personal estate intended by the grantor to be in lieu of dower must be deemed a conveyance or devise by way of jointure within the meaning of the statute. *Tevis v. McCreary, 3 Met. 151.*

4. *Question of intention.*—Whether a gift was made in satisfaction of dower is a question of intention to be gathered in general from the face of the instrument passing the title. It is not necessary that the instrument should state in terms that the gift is in lieu of dower. *Ib.*

5. *Wife deprived of jointure.*—If the wife be lawfully deprived of her jointure or any part of it, without any fault of her own, she may have indemnity by way of dower or damage out of her husband's estate, to the extent of the loss. *Ib.*

6. *Legacy as jointure entitled to priority.*—Where a legacy was given to the wife by way of jointure and the assets are insufficient to pay all the legacies, the legacy given as a jointure will be entitled to priority over the general legacies. *Ib.*

## II. Widow's right before dower assigned.

7. *Rent—from what time recoverable.*—A widow, whose husband died since the Revised Statutes took effect, is entitled to rent against a purchaser from her husband, from the time she commences her action to recover dower (Revised Statutes, 394). *Yancey v. Smith, 2 Met. 408.*

## III. Dower in incumbered property—assigning by executors.

8. *If wife mortgage dower, entitled to be reimbursed out of surplus.*—Though the wife join with her husband in a mortgage of his land, and though the husband should afterward release, convey, or devise the equity of redemption, the wife will be entitled at his decease to her dower in the land, subject to the mortgage; and if the land be sold under the mortgage, then to her claim as for dower in any surplus proceeds. *Harrow v. Johnson, 3 Met. 578.*

9. *Wife joining husband in mortgage—right to have personalty first exhausted.*—Where the wife joins with her husband in a mortgage of his land and personalty, she has, after the death of her husband, a right to have the mortgaged personalty first applied to the mortgaged debt, as against the mortgagee and general creditors. *Ib.*

10. *Executors may convey to wife in satisfaction of dower.*—Where the title of the testator's lands are vested in the executors with power to sell, they can assign a part thereof to the widow for dower, in consideration of her releasing the residue, which in the absence of collusion between them and the widow, will bind the heirs and creditors. *Ib.*

Negroes or mulattoes claiming freedom under the President's proclamation to be treated and dealt with as runaway slaves.
SEC. I. That it shall not be lawful for any negro or mulatto, claiming or pretending to be free, under or by virtue of the proclamation of the president of the United States, dated the 1st of January, 1863, declaring free slaves in certain states and parts of states, or any similar proclamation, by order of the government of the United States, or any officer or agent thereof, to migrate to or remain in this state.

SEC. II. Any negro or mulatto who shall violate the provisions of this act shall be arrested, dealt with, and disposed of as runaways, and the proceedings shall conform to the laws in existence, at the time they are had in relation to runaway slaves.

Purchaser's title declared good.
SEC. III. The purchaser of any such negro or mulatto sold under and by virtue of such proceedings shall, by virtue of such purchase, acquire and have the same right to, the same property in, and control over such negro or mulatto as masters have over their slaves under existing laws, subject to the provisions in relation to slaves sold as runaways, and shall in all respects be governed by the law in relation to master and slave.

When purchase money to be paid
SEC. IV. The purchase money for such negro or mulatto shall not be paid into the public treasury until the right of redemption shall have expired or been finally determined by adjudication, in case the same shall be put in litigation, as provided in relation to slaves sold as runaways, and the court shall have power to loan the same, in the meantime taking bond and good security for the same.

SEC. V. It shall be the duty of all peace officers to see that the provisions of this act are enforced.

SEC. VI. This act shall take effect from its passage.

x.] ATTACHMENT. 45

him, the plaintiff is entitled to an attachment against the property of all of them. *Ib.*

3. *Attachment laws to be equitably construed.*—The courts incline to an equitable construction of the attachment laws, so as to secure the rights of creditors and effectuate the intention of the legislature as expressed in the attachment laws. *Spalding v. Simms*, 4 Met. 285.

4. *Four months' absence from home—not out of the state.*—Where the debtor leaves his home with the intention of going out of the state, and does go out of the state, and is absent *from his home* four months pursuant to such intention, it must be regarded as absence from the state, and ground of attachment, although he may have been delayed a few days by some unlooked-for casualty in passing the territorial limits of the state, so that he had not been actually out of the state four months when the attachment issued. *Ib.*

## II. Sufficiency of the affidavit—Amendment thereof.

5. *That the affiant "believes" the facts, etc.*—The affidavit stated that "the affiant *believed* that defendant R. has left C. county, the county of his residence, to avoid the service of legal process. He believes that R. so conceals himself that a summons can not be served on him," which statement was sworn to as true, and the defendant responded to it without objection to its sufficiency, and the evidence showed that the statement was true: *Held*—that the judgment of the circuit court sustaining the attachment must be affirmed. *Ryan v. Bean*, 2 Met. 137.

6. *Affidavit by agent—what must be stated.*—Where an affidavit to obtain an attachment is made by an agent of the plaintiff, it must state that the plaintiff is then absent from the county in which the action is brought, and that the affiant is his agent. *Pool v. Webster*, 3 Met. 278.

7. *Amendment of affidavit.*—Where the affidavit upon which the attachment issued is defective, it can not be amended so as to render it valid after the attachment issues. *Ib.* [But see post, and *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met. 342.]

8. *Affidavit may be amended.*—The affidavit must state that the claim is just. To omit this statement is cause for reversal. But upon the return of the cause to the lower court, the plaintiff may amend the affidavit (*Civil Code, section 161*). *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met. 342.

9. *The same.*—There is no difference in principle between allowing an amendment of the petition on which an attachment issues, and the amendment of an affidavit on which an attachment issues. *Ib.*; and *Civil Code, section 161*.

10. *Defective affidavit—attachment not void.*—An attachment is not void which issues upon a defective affidavit. It is within the rule that the proceedings of a court which has jurisdiction of the person or subject, are not void however erroneous. *Ib.*

# 1860.

AN ACT to punish frauds in the election of school trustees. 4

**SEC. I.** That any person who may be chosen to preside over the election of school trustees in any school district in this commonwealth, who shall, knowingly and with intent to commit a fraud, receive and count any illegal vote, or issue a certificate of election to any person not entitled thereto, or shall refuse to issue such certificate to any one duly elected; and any person who shall, with fraudulent intent, deface, mutilate, or destroy the records of any such election, shall, upon indictment by the grand jury and conviction thereof, be fined the sum of fifty dollars for every such offense, or be confined for twenty days in the county jail in default of the payment of such fine. And such fines shall, when paid, be added to the school fund of the district in which such fraud was committed.

That the commandants of companies in each regiment, squadron, or battalion, shall constitute a regimental court of inquiry, over which the major, or the commandant present ranking as senior, shall preside, who shall meet at such times and places as the commanding officer of the regiment may direct; notices of which meeting shall be given at least two weeks previous to the convening of the court. At the court, when organized, all members of companies absent from any company or battalion drill, muster, or encampment prescribed by this act, occuring since the last meeting of the court, shall appear and present their excuses for such absences, and the court shall determine the validity of such excuses, and shall have jurisdiction to assess all fines under this act, except as otherwise hereinafter provided for; and every person who shall fail to appear as aforesaid, and demand a trial by jury, as hereinafter provided for, shall be deemed to have waived any other or further trial of his delinquencies. At said regimental, squadron, or battalion court of inquiry, the adjutant shall attend and keep a record of the proceedings of said court, and shall make out a list of the members fined at said court in each company, with the amount of the fine assessed on each annexed to their names, which list shall be signed by the officer presiding at said court, and attested by the adjutant, and by him preserved: *Provided*, That if, at said court of inquiry, any person shall appear, and before said court shall have acted upon his case, shall file a request in writing for a trial by jury, the case shall be certified to a justice of the peace for the proper county, who shall thereupon call a jury and proceed to trial, judgment and execution, as in other cases at law. Any person feeling aggrieved by the decision of said regimental court of inquiry, may have a new trial by order of the commandant of the proper regiment or battalion; which commander shall thereupon convene a court of appeals, to be composed of the field officers, adjutant, and senior captain of the regiment or battalion, a majority of whom shall be a quorum, before which such new trial shall be had. The court of appeals shall meet at such time and at such place, within the regimental bounds, as the commandant shall order.

AN ACT to amend the sixth section of an act passed the 19th December, 1861, entitled, an act to amend the law in relation to runaway slaves.

[TITLE ### III. *Levy*. 11. *Officer presumed to have done his duty*.—The officer stated in his return that he had levied the attachment on a town lot, "and posted a copy of the attachment on the premises, there being no tenant," but omitted to state that he left the copy in a conspicuous place on the premises. *Held*, that, nothing appearing to the contrary, the law presumed the officer to have performed his duty, and that the return was sufficient. *Lewis v. Quinker*, 2 Met. 284. ### IV. *Forthcoming bond and bond for the retention of attached property*. 12. *Bond discharges attachment, and admits the grounds on which it issued*.—The execution of the bond for the retention of attached property as authorized by sections two hundred and forty-two and two hundred and forty-three of the Code, operates to discharge the attachment, and the obligors in the bond must perform the judgment of the court. In an action on the bond they can neither contest the grounds for obtaining the attachment, nor the liability of the property to be taken under it. *Hazelrigg v. Donaldson*, 2 Met. 445. 13. *Forthcoming bond—effect of*.—Possession of property taken under a general attachment may be retained upon executing the bond provided by section two hundred and thirty-five, which, in effect, binds the obligors to have the property forthcoming to answer the judgment of the court; or that the obligors will pay the damage resulting from the default. *Bell v. The Wrecking Co.*, 3 Met. 558. 14. *Forthcoming bond and bond discharging attachment—distinction*.—The bond *supra* is a mere obligation for the forthcoming of the property; but if the defendant desires to obtain *a discharge of the attachment*, he may do so by giving bond "to the effect that he will *perform the judgment of the court*." (*Code, sections* 242, 243, 254.) This bond stands in lieu of the attachment, and the action proceeds as though no attachment had ever issued. *Ib*. 15. *Forthcoming bond does not discharge attachment*.—In attachments against steamboats, the bond authorized by section two hundred and sixty-eight is merely an obligation for the forthcoming of the property, and does not discharge the attachment, while the bond authorized by sections two hundred and seventy and two hundred and seventy-one discharges the attachment, and is an undertaking to perform the judgment of the court. *Ib*. 16. *Construction of informal bond*.—In an action brought to foreclose a chattel mortgage, a specific attachment issued and was levied on the property as authorized by section two hundred and seventy-three. 17. *Forthcoming bond*—*attachment may be contested*.—A party whose property was seized under an attachment against another, for the purpose of retaining his property, executed the forthcoming bond authorized by section two hundred and thirty-five of the Code: *Held*—that he was not thereby precluded from disputing the validity of the attachment, and asserting his own title as allowed by section two hundred and fifty-seven.—*Schwein v. Simms*, 2 Met. 209.

18. *Mistake*—*wrong bond executed*—*party bound*.—A party whose property was seized under an attachment against another, offered to execute the forthcoming bond as allowed by *Code*, section two hundred and thirty-five, which the sheriff refused to accept, or to surrender the property, unless the bond prescribed in sections two hundred and forty-two and two hundred and forty-three, was executed, which bond he was thus coerced to execute and did execute as surety of the defendant in the attachment, not understanding its legal effect at the time: *Held*—that notwithstanding the coercion and the ignorance of the nature and effect of the bond, he was bound unconditionally to perform the judgment of the court. *Hazelrigg v. Donaldson*, 2 Met. 445.

V. *Claim of attached property by stranger.*

19. *Irregularity of proceedings*—*effect*.—A. attached the property of B. A third party claimed the property, filed his petition to be made a defendant to the suit and was ordered by the court to be made a party, *but never was in fact made such*, though he was regarded by the plaintiff (who raises the question) and the court, as a party; and the action was tried as though he was such: *Held*—that the irregularity was no ground for reversal. *Schwein v. Simms*, 2 Met. 209.

20. *Jury*—*effect of verdict*.—Where a third party claims attached property, the court may order a jury to try the fact, and a verdict in favor of the claimant must be regarded as finding that the claimant was the owner and entitled to the possession of the property when the attachment was levied, although the verdict does not specify that he was the owner at that time. *Ib.*

VI. *Trial of attachment. Practice.*

21. *Evidence on trial of attachment*.—Affidavits can not be used as evidence on the trial of attachments. The proof must be by deposition or by the oral examination of the witnesses in court. *Newton v. West*, 3 Met. 24.

22. *Garnishment of debt not due or ascertained*.—A garnishee answers that he owes the defendant, but that the debt is not yet due nor the amount thereof ascertained: *Held*—that the court could only retain control of the case until a final judgment could be rendered as the facts should thereafter

ATTACHMENT.
[TITLE

be developed and shown by further pleading and proof. *Roberts v. Drinkard*, 3 Met. 309.

23. *Affidavits constitute pleadings*—*no other effect*.—On the trial of an attachment the affidavits of plaintiff and defendant constitute the pleadings and have no other effect. They can not be used as evidence. *Duncan v. Wickliff*, 4 Met. 118.

24. *Failure to contest attachment*—*effect*.—If the defendant in an attachment appears and fails to contest it, the court must regard it as valid. *Spalding v. Simms*, 4 Met. 285.

25. *Must be warning order before attachment sustained*.—An order sustaining an attachment against a non-resident who has not been summoned or appeared, is erroneous unless there has been a warning order against him. *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met. 342.

## VII. Liens and priorities.

26. *Sale in contemplation of insolvency*—*no priority obtained by attachment*.—A sale made in contemplation of insolvency operates under the statute as an assignment by the vendor for the equal benefit of all his creditors, and neither of them can thereafter obtain priority over their co-creditors by suing out attachments. *Shouse v. Utterback*, 2 Met. 52.

27. *The same*.—The rule is the same although the attachments were sued out and levied before any action had been brought under the statute to have the sale declared an assignment, provided such action is brought at any time within the six months prescribed by the statute. *Ib*.

28. *Attachment of debt assigned by parol*.—Attaching creditors acquire only an equitable right to a debt in the hands of the garnishee; and this equity is subordinate to that of a surety of the defendant to whom the defendant had made a parol assignment of the debt before the suing out of the attachment, as an indemnity for his suretyship. *Newby & Co. v. Hill & Co.*, 2 Met. 530.

29. *Attachment of steamboat*—*lien for supplies*.—An attachment to enforce a lien upon a steamboat for supplies is subordinate to the lien of a prior mortgagor, and where a forthcoming bond has been given and the boat is not produced, the value of the equity of redemption should be ascertained by reference to a master. *Halbert v. McCulloch*, 3 Met. 456.

## VIII. Sale of attached property.

30. *Sale of property as perishable not complete till confirmed*.—The sale of attached property made by a sheriff under an order of the county court issued in vacation as authorized by section two hundred and thirty-nine, is not complete until reported to, and confirmed by, the court in which the attachment is pending. *Greer v. Powell*, 3 Met. 124.

And such sales, upon the report of the sheriff, may be excepted to and set aside upon proper grounds shown. *Ib*.

## IX. *Damage for wrongful attachment.*

31. *Cost and counsel fee recoverable.*—The only damage to which a defendant was subjected by the wrongful issue and levy of an attachment upon his land, was the employment of counsel to defend the attachment. In an action against the surety in the bond: *Held*—that he is responsible for the costs, and reasonable counsel fee in defending the attachment. *Trapnall v. McAffee*, 3 Met. 34.

32. *What damage recoverable.*—The only damage which can be recovered in such an action, is what results from the attachment itself; not what is occasioned by the action independent of the attachment. *Ib.*

33. *Only reasonable attorney's fee recoverable.*—The defendant in an attachment wrongfully sued out, can only recover his attorney's fees where he has paid, or contracted to pay them, and upon showing that the amount is reasonable. *Shultz v. Morrison*, 3 Met. 98.

34. *When action for damage may be brought.*—An action on an attachment bond for maliciously suing out an attachment, will not lie until the attachment has been discharged, and the plaintiff in such action must aver that it has been discharged. *Nolle v. Thompson*, 3 Met. 121.

35. *Counter-claim for damage—not sustained in attachment suit.*—Defendant in an action in which an attachment issued against his property, can not set up as a counter-claim in that action, a demand for damages for maliciously suing out the attachment, although the plaintiff is a non-resident and has no property in the state out of which the damage can be made. *Ibid.*

## X. *Attachments against steamboats.*

36. *Forthcoming bond does not preclude from contesting attachment.*—The claimant of a steamboat taken by attachment, by executing the bond prescribed by the Code, section two hundred and sixty-eight, is not precluded from asserting his right to the boat, or contesting the validity of the attachment. *Halbert v. McCulloch*, 3 Met. 456.

37. *Informal bond construed.*—The obligors in the bond prescribed by section two hundred and sixty-eight of the Code, undertaking that they will pay the plaintiffs such sum of money as may be adjudged in the action or have the boat forthcoming for the satisfaction of such judgment, whichever the court may order, are only liable for the forthcoming of the boat for the purpose of subjecting the defendant's interest therein to the satisfaction of the attachment lien. *Ib.*

## XI. *Attachments after a return of "no property."*

38. *Lien created by levy.*—The levy of an attachment sued out after the return of an execution "No property found," creates a lien upon the property of the debtor levied on, which the plaintiff can enforce against all subsequent purchasers. *Lewis v. Quinker*, 2 Met. 284.

# TITLE XLIX.
## EMANCIPATION.*

I. BY ONE OF TWO JOINT OWNERS OF A SLAVE. EFFECT OF.
II. EMANCIPATION UPON A CONDITION. PERFORMANCE OF CONDITION.
III. CHILDREN BORN BEFORE EMANCIPATION TAKES EFFECT.
IV. DEVISE OF A SLAVE TO A FREE NEGRO.

---

### I. By one of two joint owners of a slave. Effect of.

1. *Amounts to a conversion, and slave free.*—A person owning a three-fifths' interest in certain slaves devised their freedom to them: *Held*—that the slaves were thereby emancipated; that the act of the testator was a conversion of his co-tenants' interests to his own use, and changed their interest in the slaves into a money demand on his estate. *Oatfield v. Waring*, 14 Johnson's R. 188; *Davis v. Tingle*, 8 B. Mon. 544; *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 593.

### II. Emancipation upon a condition. Performance of the condition.

2. *Condition must be faithfully performed.*—A testator, in 1837, declared in his will, that any of his slaves at certain periods and ages who wished to be free, "should be delivered to the colonization society to be transported to Liberia. None of them are to be forced to go. Those of them who do not choose to go to Liberia, are to continue to serve, etc., until they are willing and do go:" *Held*—that to entitle the slaves to freedom, they must go to Liberia, in good faith to dwell there and become colonists; that to go with a design to return immediately to the United States, which design is immediately carried out, is not a performance of the condition upon which the slave was to be free. *Winn v. Sam Martin*, 4 Met. 231.

---

502 SLAVES, RUNAWAYS AND FREE NEGROES. [TITLE

2. *Reward for arrest of runaway*.—To entitle a person arresting a runaway in a border county, who has escaped from another county, to the reward prescribed by the act of 1856, he must deliver the slave to the owner, or person from whom he escaped, or into the jail of the county where the owner resides. *Nall v. Proctor*, 3 Met. 447.

3. *Delivery of runaway*—*reward*.—A party is not entitled to the reward provided in the above act who arrests the runaway and delivers him into the jail of the county where the arrest was made, although the jailer deliver him afterward to the owner. *Ib*.

4. *Owner's liability for medical services to slaves*.—It is only in cases requiring instant and indispensable assistance, and not admitting delay to obtain the master's assent, that the latter would be liable for medical services rendered his slaves without his consent. *Kellar v. Bate*, 3 Met. 130.

5. *Acquiescence is consent*.—But if a master knows that medical services are being rendered to his slaves, and permits it to be continued without objection, or shows a willingness or desire that the services should be rendered, he is presumed to consent. *Ib*.

6. *Loss of slave*—*criterion of damage*.—In an action by the owner of a life estate in a slave against a constable for negligently suffering the slave to escape while in his custody under a criminal charge whereby the slave is lost, the measure of damage is the value of such life estate. *Tudor v. Lewis*, 3 Met. 378.

7. *Selling dower slaves*.—A doweress who sells a dower slave to a southern trader, who removes the slave out of the state without the consent of the person owning the reversion, does thereby forfeit her life estate in such slave. *Williams v. McClanahan*, 3 Met. 420.

8. *The same*—*reversioner's right*.—In the case *supra*, if the doweress invest the money received upon such sale in other slaves, the owner in reversion has a right to take such slaves instead of the money for which the dower slave was sold. *Ib*.

9. *Suit for division*.—In general a suit for the division of slaves can not be maintained until an administrator is appointed and made party; but cases may arise where this will not apply. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

10. *Emancipation by one joint tenant*—*effect*.—If one of several tenants in common of slaves devise them their freedom, it is a conversion of the slaves to his own use. The slaves will be free, and the co-tenants will be entitled to be paid the value of their interest out of the testator's estate. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

### I. *Privileges of attorneys.*

1.  *Privileged communication.*—Attorneys can not testify concerning any communication made to them by their clients in that capacity, nor as to their advice to their clients thereon, without the client's consent. In all other cases attorneys are competent witnesses for or against their clients. *Hall v. Renfro*, 3 Met. 51.

2.  *Attorney responsible for wrong advice resulting in trespass.*—An attorney at law who advises a judicial officer to act beyond the limits of his authority whereby a party suffers wrong, is responsible for the damage, however correct or pure his motives may have been. *Revill v. Pettit*, 3 Met. 314.

### II. *Extent of attorney's authority.*

3.  *Attorney can not compromise client's claim.*—Without special authority from his client, an attorney has no power to compromise his client's suit, or surrender his claim to the defendant, or dismiss his action. *Smith v. Dixon*, 3 Met. 438.

4.  *Extent of power to bind client.*—The general authority of an attorney to bind his client, extends only to admissions made of record in court while

ATTORNEY.
TITLE

conducting and managing the client's case, and preparatory to the trial and decision of the case. *Ib.*

5. *Client may have unauthorized compromise vacated.*—Where an attorney has compromised his client's suit without his consent, the client may maintain an action to have the judgment on the compromise set aside; and in such action, the merits of the original case will not be inquired into. The judgment should be set aside, and the original case allowed to proceed as though no compromise had been made. *Ib.*

### III. Liability of, for partner's acts.

6. *An attorney responsible for funds converted by partner.*—Two attorneys were in partnership in the practice of law, and as a firm received and receipted for claims for collection "by suit or otherwise," and recovered judgments on them, when M., one of the attorneys died, and P., the surviving partner, collected the money on the judgment, failed to pay it over, and died insolvent. There being no assets of the firm of M. & P., the separate estate of M. is liable for the money collected by the surviving partner. *McGill v. McGill, 2 Met. 258.*

7. *Contract for professional services, personal—death terminates it.*—A contract with a law firm for professional services, is *personal* in its nature, and the client has the right to the services of all its members, and if one of them dies, the contract is at an end, unless what remains to be performed is not of a personal character, and can certainly be done as well and to the same purpose by another. *Ib.*

8. *Otherwise if what remains to be done is not personal.*—Where a law firm receipted for claims "for collection by suit or otherwise," and after the recovery of judgment thereon one of the partners died: *Held*—that what remained to be done in completion of the contract to *collect* the debt, was not of a personal nature, and that the surviving partner had the right to complete the collection, and the estate of the deceased partner was bound for the performance of the contract. *Ib*

### IV. Liability of, for client's cost.

9. *Praying and prosecuting appeal for non-resident.*—An attorney by praying and prosecuting an appeal for a non-resident defendant who has no property in this state, without causing bond to be executed for cost, does not thereby become liable for the appellee's cost. *Chrisman v. Russell, 2 Met. 112.*

10. *How attorney becomes responsible for cost*—An attorney can only be made liable for the costs of his client by directing process to issue in an action in the inferior court in which the client was required by law to give bond for cost, which he had failed to do, and the inferior court only has jurisdiction to enforce such liability. *Ib.*

XI.] ATTORNEY. 55

## V. *Striking name from rolls.*

11. *Must be in name of commonwealth.*—A proceeding against an attorney to strike his name from the roll of attorneys, for official misconduct, should be prosecuted in the name of the commonwealth. *Turner v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 619.

12. *Court of appeals has jurisdiction of appeal from order.*—The court of appeals has jurisdiction of an appeal from the order of a circuit court striking the name of an attorney from the roll of attorneys of such court, upon charges of official misconduct. *Ib.*

## VI. *Employment and compensation of.*

13. *Unless agreement to pay, can not recover for services.*—A defendant in an action employs B. as counsel for himself, and also for another defendant, of which the latter was apprised; but he had already employed counsel of his own. Although the services of B. may have been beneficial to the latter, and were accepted by him, yet he would not thereby incur any liability to pay for them, unless he had been apprised by B. that he looked to him for compensation, and afterward received the services without informing B. that he would not pay for them. *Savings' Bank v. Benton*, 2 Met. 240.

14. *In divorce suit, husband must pay wife's counsel-fee, unless, etc.*—In suits for divorce and alimony, the husband is bound to pay a reasonable compensation to the attorney of the wife, whether she succeeds or not, and even if she die pending the suit, unless the wife was in fault and has ample estate of her own to pay the cost. *Ballard v. Caperton*, 2 Met. 412.

15. *Court to fix allowance in such case.*—The court in which the action is brought, may, in such case, fix the allowance of the attorney as an item of the cost, even after the death of the wife, or the termination of the suit by judgment, and may enforce its payment by attachment for contempt. *Ib.*

358 OCCUPYING CLAIMANT—OFFICE AND OFFICER. [TITLE

# TITLE XCIV.
## OCCUPYING CLAIMANT.

1.  *Must deduce title from commonwealth.*—To entitle the occupant of land to pay for his improvement under the occupying claimant law, he must deduce a title from the commonwealth, but he need not show that it is a valid title. He must show that he believed himself to be the legal or equitable owner of the land by reason of a claim founded on a grant from the commonwealth, and must therefore connect himself with the grant. *Fairbain v. Means*, 4 Met. 323.

2.  *Belief of ownership gives no right.*—The occupying claimant law does not make the right of the claimant depend upon his belief concerning his title. *Ib.*

3.  *Claimant holding under void deed.*—When the occupant holds under a sheriff's deed made in pursuance of an unauthorized sale of the patentee's land for taxes, he can not bring himself within the occupying claimant law. *Ib.*

---

# TITLE XCV.
## OFFICE AND OFFICER.

1.  *Title to office, how and where tested.*—The Code prescribes the manner in which a usurper may be deprived of the office usurped; but this does not preclude an inquiry into the title of one claiming to be an officer, where in an action against him he relies upon the office to protect him from liability. *Patterson v. Miller*, 2 Met. 493.

2.  *Acts done after term expires.*—Where by a city charter, the councilmen are only chosen for one year from the day of election, they can not hold over beyond that period; and all their official acts done after the year expires, are void. *Louisville v. Higdon*, 2 Met. 526.

6. *Suspension and removal of officers.*—Wherever the constitution has created an office and fixed its terms, and declared on what grounds and in what manner the incumbent may be removed before his term expires, the legislature has no power to remove such officer or *suspend* him from office for any other reason or in any other *mode* than that prescribed in the constitution. *Lowe v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 237.

7. *The same—constitutional provision.*—The constitution prescribes two modes in which a civil officer may be removed from office before the expiration of his term: (1) by impeachment, which mode embraces every civil officer in the state: (2) by presentment or indictment, which mode applies only to judges of county courts, justices of the peace, sheriffs, coroners, surveyors, jailers, county assessors, county attorneys, and constables. *Ib.*

8. *The same—constitution authorizes indictment.*—Section thirty-six, article four, of the constitution, of itself and without other legislation, authorizes an indictment against any of the officers therein named for the offenses there mentioned and a conviction and judgment vacating the offender's office. *Ib.* 237.

9. *Section nine, article two, chapter ninety-one Revised Statutes, unconstitutional.*—The provision of chapter ninety-one of the Revised Statutes authorizing the suspension of a jailer by the county court, virtually authorizes his removal from office in a manner different from that prescribed in the constitution and is therefore void. *Ib.*

10. *Officers responsible for negligence.*—If property in the custody of an officer pending process and proceedings touching the same, is lost by the negligence or dishonest conduct of the officer, he is liable in damage therefor. *Tudor v. Lewis*, 3 Met. 378.

11. *The same—degree of diligence required.*—An officer having custody of property by virtue of his office, is bound to the diligence of a prudent man, and to an honest discharge of his duty, and such as is required of all persons who receive a compensation for their services. *Ib.*

12. *Constitution construed as to police courts and officers.*—Section forty-one of article four of the state constitution, relates exclusively to such police

LII.] EXAMINING COURT. 211

# TITLE LII.
## EXAMINING COURT.
### 1865.
*March 3.*

*[Certain officers allowed fees for holding.]*

AN ACT in relation to examining courts.

**SEC. I.** That mayors of cities, and other officers, empowered by law to hold examining courts, shall, for their services in such cases, be allowed the same fees that are now by law allowed to justices of the peace for similar services, and that they be paid therefor in the same manner that justices are paid: *Provided*, That the provisions of this bill shall not apply to the judge of the city court of Louisville, and he shall not be entitled to any compensation out of the treasury, as an examining court, in any case.

**SEC. II.** This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

1. *One justice can not sit as examining court on charge of felony.*—One justice of the peace can not act as an examining court and commit to jail, or hold to bail on a charge of felony. Another justice must be associated with him. *Revill v. Pettit*, 3 Met. 314.

2. *Otherwise as to misdemeanors.*—But a single justice may examine and hold to bail for a misdemeanor; he is only required to call in another justice when the prisoner is charged with felony. *Thorp v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 411.

3. *Examining court can not admit to bail during examination.*—Where the prisoner is charged with felony, an examining court has no right during the progress of the examination to admit to bail or release from custody. During the adjournment of the examining court the prisoner must be committed to jail or the custody of an officer. *Commonwealth v. Moore*, 3 Met. 477.

4. *Such a bail bond void.*—A bail bond taken for the appearance of the prisoner before the examining court during the progress of the examination is void. *Ib.*

WHO ARE SURETIES. WHEN BOUND. SIGNING UPON CONDITION. II. RELEASE AND DISCHARGE OF SURETIES. III. REMEDY OF SURETY AGAINST PRINCIPAL. ### I. Who are sureties. When bound. Signing on condition. *Where surety signs as principal.*—Where the obligors in an assignable instrument hold themselves out as principals, both the assignor and assignee of the instrument have a right to treat them as such, unless they know that some of the parties are sureties of the others. *Neel v. Harding*, 2 Met. 247. 2. *Signing on condition that others sign.*—Where sureties signed the note on condition that two other solvent persons should also sign it, of which condition and its breach the holder had notice before he took the note: *Held*—that he could not recover against the sureties. *Coffman v. Wilson*, 2 Met. 542; *Bivins v. Helsley*, 4 Met. 78. 3. *The same—notice.*—Where the surety signed upon condition that principal was to get another to sign, and that the note was not to be delivered unless the other did sign, and the principal delivered the note to the obligee without procuring it to be signed by the other, of which condition and violation the obligee had no notice when he took the obligation: *Held*—that the surety was bound. *Millett v. Parker*, 2 Met. 608. 4. *The same—notice.*—But if the obligee when he takes the note has notice that the surety had signed and was to be bound only on condition that another should also sign, which he had not done, the surety will not be bound to him. *Bivins v. Helsley*, 4 Met. 78. ### II. Release and discharge of sureties. 5. *Indulgence where surety signs as principal.*—A contract of indulgence between the assignee of a joint and several note and the principal debtor, without the assent of the surety, does not release the latter unless the assignee had knowledge that he was a surety at the time of granting the indulgence. *Neel v. Harding*, 2 Met. 247. 6. *Release of surety by replevying judgment.*—Where a judgment against principal and surety is replevied by the principal debtor with new sureties, the replevin bond merges the judgment and releases the original surety. *Havens v. Fondry*, 4 Met. 247. 33 Digitized by Google # INDEX.

[TITLE 7. *New surety—rights of as to indemnity held by released surety.*—In the case *supra*, where the original sureties who are released by the execution of the replevin bond, were indemnified by a mortgage against loss, the sureties in the replevin bond who pay the debt, have no equity under such mortgage, as against other lien holders. *Ib.* ### III. *Remedy of surety against principal.* 8. *Must pay debt before he has claim on principal.*—Until he pays the money for his principal, the surety has no available demand against him which amounts either to a setoff or an equitable discount. *Walker v. McKay, 2 Met. 294.* 9. *The same—mutual debts—insolvency—set off.*—The insolvency of the principal at the time he assigns a note held by him upon his surety, does not entitle the latter to an equity against the note in the hands of the assignee, he not having paid the debt for which he was surety until after he had notice of the assignment. *Ib.* 10. *Co-sureties—liability inter se.*—A surety who pays his principal's debt, must, in order to compel his co-surety to contribute his proportion of the debt so paid, show that the principal is insolvent. The Revised Statutes (chapter ninety-seven, section seven), have not changed this rule. *Lee v. Foreman, 3 Met. 114.* 11. *Payment by surety after he is released.*—Where a surety, who has by operation of law, been released from all obligation to pay the debt, pays the same, he can not recover it from his principal. *Kimble v. Cummings, 3 Met. 327.* 12. *The same—surety in replevin bond.*—Execution issued upon a replevin bond after twelve months from its maturity and was levied on property of the surety, who thereupon paid the debt: *Held*—in the absence of any assignment of the debt to the surety, or of any request by the principal to him to pay it, or of a subsequent promise of the principal to pay it, that he can not recover against the principal. *Ib.* 13. *Payment by surety entitles him to securities held by creditor.*—When a surety has paid the debt of his principal he is entitled to stand in the place of the creditor as to all liens and equities to which the creditor had a right to look as security for payment of the debt. *Havens v. Fondry, 4 Met. 247.* 14. *The same.*—In such a case it must be shown that at the time the surety paid the debt, the creditor had a valid and subsisting lien or equity enforceable in a court of equity, in order to entitle the surety to the benefits of it. *Ib.* --- See LIMITATION, Notes 13, 14, 15. BASTARDY.
[TITLE

1. *Bastardy cases not actions but special proceedings.*—A bastardy case is not an action but a special proceeding as defined by the Code. It is not for a penalty or forfeiture or to enforce a private right within the meaning of the Code. *Chandler v. Commonwealth*, 4 Met. 66.

2. *Bastard father liable though under age.*—The infancy of a bastard father does not excuse him from prosecution under the bastardy statutes for the support of his child. Infants are liable for the wrongs they commit. But in such a prosecution a guardian *ad litem* should be appointed to defend for him. *Ib*.

3. *Court may render judgment though bastard father fail to appear.*—Bastardy proceedings under *chapter six of the Revised Statutes*, are civil and not criminal proceedings, and the appearance of the defendant is not necessary to authorize a trial, but the court may proceed to judgment, and enforce its judgment by rule and attachment. *Ib*.

4. *Absence in army does not authorize continuance.*—The absence of a defendant in a bastardy case as a soldier in the armies of the state or United States, does not entitle him to a continuance under the acts of March 8 and 11, 1862. *Ib*.

5. *Evidence—what incompetent.*—A promise of marriage is not admissible evidence on a trial of a bastardy case. *Ib*.

6. *Appeals lie from judgment on forfeited recognizance.*—An appeal from a judgment of the county court on a forfeited recognizance for the appearance of defendant in a bastardy case, lies to the court of appeals and not to the circuit court. *Commonwealth v. Taphorn*, 4 Met. 71.

EXECUTION.
[TITLE

—For an act requiring the execution and return under which real estate is sold by the sheriff to be recorded, see CLERKS, 8.

See ELISOR. JAILER. SHERIFF.

I. TO WHOM TO BE DIRECTED.
II. LEVY AND SALE: VALIDITY OF.
III. SALE OF EQUITABLE INTERESTS UNDER EXECUTION.
IV. INDEMNITY TO OFFICER BEFORE LEVY OR SALE.
V. STAY OF EXECUTION BY INJUNCTION OR SUPERSEDEAS.
VI. PENALTY FOR FAILING TO RETURN.
VII. DEATH OF SHERIFF: COMPLETION OF EXECUTION.

I. *To whom to be directed.*

1. *When execution to be directed to jailer or coroner.*—Where the original process in a case is served by a jailer or coroner an execution issuing upon any judgment therein must be directed to such jailer or coroner. *Boaz v. Nail*, 2 Met. 245. (But see ante, p. 215, Act, February 16, 1864.)

II. *Levy and sale—validity of.*

2. *Levy and release thereof—subsequent levy.*—A sheriff and constable levied executions on the personal property of the defendant. At the instance of the defendant, the officers released the property to him upon his agreeing to sell it and apply the proceeds to the respective executions, according to the liens created by them: *Held*—that after the surrender of the property, the sheriff had a right to levy the executions on the lands of the defendant. *Jones v. Lusk*, 2 Met. 356.

3. *Execution on replevin bond taken for six months void.*—An execution issued upon a replevin bond taken for six instead of three months, is invalid, and a sale and conveyance of land thereunder, passes no title. *Vertrees v. Shean*, 2 Met. 291.

4. *To make valid levy officer must see property.*—The levy of an execution on personal property not seen by the officer at the time of the levy, is invalid and creates no lien upon the property. *Bradley & Cobb v. Phillips*, MSS. opinion, December 10, 1864.

5. *Sale of personality under fi. fa. void unless property seen by purchasers.* And the sale of the same property not seen by the purchasers at the time of the sale is void. *Ib.*

6. *Void execution—sale under.*—A debtor who surrenders in writing a tract of land to be sold under a void execution against him, is not estopped to controvert the title of the purchaser. The sale and sheriff's deed are void, and the surrender can impart no validity to them. *Geoghegan v. Ditto*, 2 Met. 433

### III. Sale of incumbered property and equitable interest under execution.

7. *Property held under title bond not subject to levy.*—The provisions of article five, chapter thirty-six, Revised Statutes, in relation to the sale of incumbered property under execution do not apply to lands of which the defendant has only the possession and a bond for the title, although the execution issued on a judgment for the purchase money. *Moore v. Simpson*, 3 Met. 349.

8. *The same.*—The equitable interest of a party in real estate of which he holds only the possession and a bond for a title, is not subject to levy and sale under execution. *Ib.*

9. *Otherwise if the holder of the bond surrender the land for levy.*—But where such equitable owner by a written indorsement on the execution surrenders his interest in the land for sale in satisfaction thereof, he will be estopped to deny the title of a purchaser thereof, and compelled, by the proper action, to surrender the possession to such purchaser. *Ib.*

10. *Incumbered property.*—The only interest acquired by the purchaser of a right to redeem incumbered property, at a sale thereof under execution, is a lien for the purchase money and ten per cent. interest per annum thereon from the day of sale. *Forrest v. Phillips*, 2 Met. 194.

11. *Incumbered property—effect of sale.*—The defendant surrendered to the sheriff, by writing, a tract of land to be levied on. The land had, in connection with other property, been previously mortgaged to secure another debt, and the mortgage duly recorded. The defendant neither gave notice of the incumbrance or represented it as unincumbered, and it was sold as unincumbered property. Afterward the mortgage was satisfied by the other property: *Held*—nevertheless that the purchaser only acquired a lien for his bid and ten per cent. interest. *Ib.*

### IV. Indemnity to officers before levy or sale.

12. *When officer may demand indemnifying bond.*—The officer has the right to demand an indemnifying bond before proceeding to levy or make sale of personal property, whenever he, in good faith, and upon the exercise of a sound discretion, doubts whether the property is subject to levy and sale. *Board v. Helm*, 2 Met. 500.

*The same.*—This right is not confined to any particular class of cases, and the existence of such doubt is the sole condition upon which the right to demand the bond depends. *Ib.*

14. *When sheriff justified in refusing to sell.*—Where the sheriff's return of an execution showed that no summons had been served upon the defendant in the execution before the rendition of the judgment on which it issued, and that he had levied the execution with the understanding that the plaintiff was to give an indemnifying bond before the sale which he had failed to do: *Held*—that the sheriff was justified in refusing to make the sale. *Ib.*

15. *Bond must not only be taken but returned.*—Before a sheriff can defend himself under an indemnifying bond, he must show a strict compliance

AN ACT to regulate the sale of spirituous liquors.

I.** That hereafter no person shall sell to any white person, under the age of twenty-one years, any spirituous or vinous liquors, or the mixture of either, unless by the written consent or request of the father of such minor, if living, or of the mother or guardian of such minor if the father be dead; and any person so offending shall be subject to a penalty of fifty dollars for each and every offense, recoverable in any court having jurisdiction of the amount, by warrant or indictment.

that the fact that the execution had been replevied, and then accidentally mislaid by the sheriff, so that he was unable to make the return within thirty days, was a good defense to the motion. *Shippen v. Curry*, 3 Met. 184.

25. *Sheriff must sustain defense by proof.*—Where a sheriff, upon a motion for the penalty for failing to return the execution, sets up in his response facts which constitute an excuse for the failure, he must, on the trial, prove them to be true or fail in his defense. *Mitcheson v. Foster*, 3 Met. 324.

26. *Execution from court of appeals—motion in that court.*—Where the execution issues from the court of appeals, the sheriff and his sureties are liable for the amount thereof with the penalty, if he fail to return it within thirty days after return day, and the motion may be prosecuted in the court of appeals. *Ib.*

27. *Sheriff responsible for money paid deputy.*—Money was paid to a deputy after the return day of the execution, it never having been levied. The deputy failing to pay it over: *Held*—that the sheriff is responsible. *James v. Yates*, 3 Met. 343.

28. *The same—deputy's sureties liable.*—Held further in the case *supra*, that the sheriff, having paid the money to the plaintiff in the execution, could recover it from the sureties of the deputy in the bond given by him to the sheriff to indemnify him against the deputy's misfeasance in office. *Ib.*

29. *The same—money collected by one deputy and handed to another.*—Held further in the case *supra*, that the fact that the deputy after the return day, and before the execution was levied, handed the execution to another deputy, who collected the money and paid it over to the first deputy, who failed to account, did not release the sureties of the latter from responsibility. *Ib.*

### VII. Death of sheriff—completion of execution.

30. After an execution came to the hands of a deputy, but before it was levied, the sheriff died, and the deputy was appointed deputy to his successor, and then levied the execution as deputy of the latter: *Held*—that it was his duty to complete the execution as deputy of the first sheriff, and that his successor is not responsible for an illegal levy thereof. *Hamilton v. Vail*, 2 Met. 511.

CXXIII.] TAVERNS AND TIPPLING HOUSES. 519

subject to penalties provided by law against selling by retail without a license, and to be recovered in the same manner.

1.  *Baggage*—*degree of care*.—Innkeepers are bound not merely to ordinary care but to uncommon care of the baggage of their guests. And it is not necessary that the baggage should be in his special keeping; but it is generally sufficient that it is in the inn under his implied care. *Packard v. Northcraft*, 2 Met. 439.

2.  *When innkeeper not responsible*.—The innkeeper will not be responsible if the guest has taken upon himself the exclusive custody of his goods, but even a request from the guest that his goods shall remain at some particular place in the inn, the place not being under the exclusive control of the guest, but under the supervision of the innkeeper, will not exonerate the latter from liability for their loss. *Ib*.

3.  *The same*.—Unless the innkeeper has notified the guest that he will not be responsible for goods left in the public room, his liability continues. The mere exercise of a choice of a room by the guest which is not objected to, will not discharge the innkeeper. *Ib*.

4.  *The same*.—An innkeeper remarked to his guest as he was about to go to bed, that he had better take his valise to his room, to which the latter replied that it was not necessary; that it would be safe in the bar-room, where it was accordingly left, but on the next morning could not be found: *Held*—that the innkeeper was liable for its value. *Ib*.

5.  *Breach of bond*—*penalty*.—A proceeding against a tavern keeper to recover the penalty prescribed in chapter ninety-nine, section seven, Revised Statutes, is not for a misdemeanor, and does not come within the provision of the Code requiring the record to be filed within sixty days after judgment. *Margoly v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 405.

6.  *Proceeding against surety*—*bar*.—Where a tavern keeper has been fined for a breach of his obligation as such, his surety not being a party to the proceeding nor notified of it, the latter is not in a subsequent motion to have judgment against him barred by the judgment against his principal, but may contest the breach of the obligation, and he is entitled to ten days' notice and a trial by jury. *Ib*.
