The fees upon each hogshead of tobacco to be collected by the proprietors of the different warehouses in the city of Louisville, shall be as follows; For receiving, storing, weighing, uncoopering and coopering up again, marking, twine, sample card, making out note, and delivering same to purchaser, selling, making out account sales, collecting and after deducting fees, paying over, or disbursing proceeds, three dollars and twenty-five cents; of this amount, the purchaser shall, upon presentation of his note, pay one dollar and fifty cents, and the planter or seller one dollar and seventy-five cents: *Provided, however*, Said proprietors shall enter into bond, with good security, to be approved by the mayor and general council of the city of Louisville, payable to the commonwealth of Kentucky, in the sum of ten thousand dollars, conditioned well and truly to pay over to the planter or seller the proceeds of all sales made by them for said planter or seller; and the injured party, if any, may sue thereon, and recover by civil suit, for his own benefit, as in other cases for any injury. Should said proprietors fail to execute said bond for ten thousand dollars, then they shall not be entitled to collect any fees, under a penalty of one hundred dollars for each offense, to be recovered in the name of the commonwealth, in any court of competent jurisdiction, at the instance of any one; and one half of said fine shall be paid over to said informant. The proprietors of the different warehouses shall not charge storage on tobacco sold or to be sold, for the space of six months; at the expiration of six months, they shall charge twenty cents per month on each hogshead,

in section eighty-five, Civil Code of Practice, or upon the owners of said boat.

SEC. II. This act shall take effect from its passage.

1. *Steamboat liable for damage done by charterer.*—By section two chapter seven, Revised Statutes, a steamboat and its owner are liable for any damage done *by the boat* to any other boat, vessel or property through the willful or negligent conduct of her officers or crew; also for any damage willfully or negligently committed by her officers or crew while acting for her as such: *Held*—that although at the time of the injury complained of the boat had been chartered by the owners to other persons, whose employees did the injury complained of, still the boat was liable therefor. *Sparks v. Steamer Kate French*, 3 Met. 533.

2. *Liability of owner in such case.*—Whether in case a boat is chartered, the owners would be responsible personally for damage done by the charterers, or whether the charterers would not be considered temporary owners, not decided. *Ib.*

3. *Lien for supplies and material.*—Under the first section of the act *supra*, does a lien attach to a boat for supplies and material furnished to persons who had chartered it from the owner? The case of *Parmele v. The Mary Stevens*, where it was held that such a lien did not attach, questioned.

VII. OTHER MATTERS.

### I. Granting administration.

1.  *Not granted on estate of non-resident*—*when*.—A grant of administration on the estate of a non-resident by the courts of this state is void unless the non-resident left *personal* property here to be administered. *Thumb v. Gresham*, 2 Met. 306.

2.  *Lapse of time*.—The lapse of twenty-eight years from the death of a person affords a presumption that there are no outstanding debts against his estate, and therefore no administrator is necessary to a division of slaves among his heirs after that time. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

3.  *Not granted after twenty years*.—By the Revised Statutes administration can not be granted after twenty years from the death of the decedent. *Ib*.

4.  *Husband's executors may administer on deceased wife's estate*—*when*.—Husband surviving his wife is entitled to her choses in action, and the equitable right thereto passes to his executors; but they can not maintain an ordinary action for them without administering on her estate. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

5.  *May recover without administering*.—In equity, cases may arise where the husband's executors will be entitled to the benefit of covenants to the wife without having administered on her estate. *Ib*.

### II. Demand on personal representative.

6.  *Where debtor dies pending suit*—Where the defendant in an action for money dies before judgment, the plaintiff must verify his claim and make demand upon the administrator before proceeding to judgment. *Matthews v. Jones*, 2 Met. 254.

7.  *Rule on plaintiff, to verify*.—Where an action is revived against a personal representative he may have a rule on the plaintiff to verify his demand, and if the latter refuse, the action will be dismissed without prejudice. *Ib*.

### III. Rights and duties of personal representatives. Power to sell.

8.  *Power to sell*—*extent of*.—Where the title of land is devised to the executors with power to sell, they may assign a part thereof to the widow for dower, in consideration of her releasing her dower in the residue, which, in

9. *Power to two, and one resigns.*—If a testator directs lands to be sold by his two executors, and one of them vacates his office, the power (under the statute), devolves on the other. But if both remain in office, both must concur in the sale. *Wills v. Lewis*, 4 Met. 269.

10. *Suit—in whose name brought.*—Where the sheriff is appointed administrator *de bonis non*, he may sue in his own name on notes executed to the former sheriff, as such, which have come to his hands. *Maraman v. Trunnell*, 3 Met. 156.

*Same.*—If the testator devises his lands to his executor with power to sell, giving the devisee only the proceeds when sold, an action to recover the land must be in the name of the executor, *Jennings v. Monks*, 4 Met. 103.

### IV. Marriage of administratrix.

11. *Vacates the office.*—When an administratrix marries, she ceases to be the personal representative of the intestate, and a suit will not then lie against her to establish a debt against the estate, or to test the question of assets to discharge the debts. *Young v. Duhme*, 4 Met. 239.

### V. Devastavit.

12. *What necessary before proceeding for devastavit.*—Before a creditor can maintain an action against the sureties in the administration bond for a *devastavit*, he must obtain a judgment against the personal representative establishing his debt, and must fix upon him assets sufficient to discharge the debt, or some part of it; but a return of no property on an execution issuing thereon is not necessary. *Young v. Duhme*, 4 Met. 240; *Lee v. Waller*, 3 Met. 61.

13. *The same.*—This is more especially the case under our statute, which requires the creditor to verify his debt in a particular way before it is sued on or paid. *Lee v. Waller*, 3 Met. 61.

*Exception.*—To the general rule *supra* there are exceptions, as where the administrator has received sufficient assets to pay all the debts of the intestate, and becomes insolvent, the creditor might attach the property of his surety, who had become non-resident, without having first got judgment against the administrator. *Ib.*

### VI. Foreign administrators.

14. *May be sued in this state, when.*—A foreign administrator who has received assets of the deceased and removed to this state and settled here, may be sued in the courts of this state by the persons entitled to the assets. The right to sue a foreign administrator here has never been extended further than this. *Baker v. Smith*, 3 Met. 264.

### VII. Other matters.

16. *No provision in will for debts*—*what property first liable*.—Where the will makes no provision for the payment of debts, they must be paid, first out of the perishable goods not specifically bequeathed; next out of the slaves, then out of the lands. *Broadwell v. Broadwell*, 4 Met. 290.

17. *Executors as garnishees*.—A judgment creditor after a return of "no property," may subject to his demand a debt due from the decedent to his debtor by a proceeding in equity, in which the executor must be made a defendant. *Lee v. Waller*, 3 Met. 61.

18. *Verifying demand*.—In such a proceeding the defendant in the execution may be compelled by the court to verify his demand against the estate of his deceased debtor, and to litigate it with the personal representative, and if he succeed in establishing it, the court will subject it to the plaintiff's debt. *Ib.*

19. *Liability of surety*.—Where in such a case it was shown that the personal representative had committed a *devastavit* and was insolvent, and his surety was a party to the suit, a joint judgment may be rendered against the executor and surety *de bonis propriis*, to be levied first of the estate of the executor, and then of that of the surety. *Ib.*

20. *Notes executed to deceased administrator, who entitled to*.—Notes executed to a deceased administrator as such, become assets in the hands of his own administrator or in the hands of the administrator *de bonis non*, according to the result of the inquiry, whether or not the first administrator had become beneficially entitled to them as a creditor of the intestate, or had charged himself with them in a settlement of the estate. *Maraman v. Trunnell*, 3 Met. 146.

21. *Refunding bond*.—A judgment, even by default, against an administrator and in favor of the distributees for the amount of the estate in his hands, is erroneous, unless it requires a refunding bond from such distributees. *Mountjoy v. Pearce*, 4 Met. 97.

See GUARDIAN, Note 7; SETOFF, Note 9; WILLS, Note 31.

380 REAL ESTATE. [TITLE

# TITLE CIV.
## REAL ESTATE.

1. *Estates tail*—*what words create*.—The words "heirs of the body," "heirs lawfully begotten of the body," and similar expressions are words of limitation, and create an estate tail, which by our statute is converted into a fee, unless there is some other expression in the deed or will showing that the words were used in a sense different from their legal import. *Johnson v. Johnson*, 2 Met. 331.

2. *All interests in land alienable*.—The effect of section six, chapter eighty, Revised Statutes, is to abolish the distinction between such estates as were alienable and such as were not, and to render every interest in, or claim to, real estate, whether present or future, vested or contingent, and however acquired, alienable by deed or will. *Nutter v. Russell*, 3 Met. 163.

3. *Contingent estate*—*what is*.—Estates are contingent which are to take effect upon an uncertain and doubtful event, or where the persons to whom they are limited are not ascertained. or not yet in being. *Augustus v. Seabolt*, 3 Met. 155.

4. *Land conveyed to one*—*consideration paid by another*—*no trust*.—Since the Revised Statutes were adopted, no resulting trust arises in favor of one who has paid for land, against one to whom it has been conveyed, unless the latter obtained the conveyance to himself, without the consent of the person paying the consideration, or where, in violation of some trust he shall have purchased the land deeded to him with the effects of another person. *Graves v. Graves*, 3 Met. 167.

5. *The same*—*pleading*.—In the case *supra* held that one claiming a resulting trust in land conveyed to another because of having furnished the consideration with which it was purchased, must aver that the grantee had taken the deed in his own name without his consent, or that in violation of some trust, he had purchased the land with the effects of the claimant. *Ib*.

6. *Trust estate liable to beneficiary's debts*.—Section twenty-three, of chapter eighty, Revised Statutes, which renders trust estates liable for the debts of beneficiaries is a re-enactment of the thirteenth section of the act of 1796, on the same subject, and is subject to the same construction. *Samuel v. Salter*, 3 Met. 259.

7. *Land purchased with wife's money*.—If a man in violation of a verbal agreement with his wife should purchase land in his own name with effects belonging to her, a trust would result to her according to section twenty-two, chapter eighty, of the Revised Statutes. But where the land was paid for

TREASURY WARRANT CLAIMS.
[TITLE

# TITLE CXXVII.

## TREASURY WARRANT CLAIMS.

1. *Misnomer in patent.*—A misnomer where the patentee is sufficiently described will not render the patent void, but the title vests in the patentee notwithstanding the mistake. *Russell v. Marks*, 3 Met. 37.

2. *The same.*—The warrant, entry, and survey were in the name of *Isaiah* Marks, but the patent issued to *Josiah* Marks: *Held*—in the absence of proof of an assignment, that the name Josiah was erroneously written for Isaiah, and that the patent passed the title to Isaiah. The mistake was corrected after a lapse of sixty-five years. *Ib.*

3. *Patent issued to a dead person.*—In 1787, a patent issued to one who was previously dead. The grant was void, and the title remained in the commonwealth. But by the act of 1792, the title vested in the devisees of the deceased patentee in the same manner as if he had been living at the time it issued. *Ib.*

4. *How patent avoided.*—A patent can not be avoided at law in a collateral proceeding by matters *dehors* the patent, unless it is declared void by statute or its nullity indicated by some explicit statutory denunciation. *Hartly v. Hartly*, 3 Met. 56.

5. *Prior statutes repealed.*—Chapter one hundred and two, Revised Statutes, repealed prior statutes upon the subject of the location and appropriation of land in possession of an actual settler thereon. *Ib*

See SURVEYS, Notes 1, 2, 3.

# TITLE LVI

## FEEBLE-MINDED CHILDREN.

### 1860.
*February 11.*

AN ACT to establish an institution for the education of idiots and feeble-minded children.

Whereas, There are over five hundred idiotic or feeble-minded persons supported by the State of Kentucky, at an annual expense of about thirty thousand dollars, which is yearly increasing in amount; and there are known to be many others in the state sustained by their friends: And whereas, Schools have been successfully established for the education and training of this imbecile and helpless class, both in Europe and America, by which it has been demonstrated that they are capable of comparatively very great improvement, and that their infirmity may, to a considerable degree, be relieved, and so far that, in many cases, they may become intelligent, useful, and self-sustaining; therefore,

**SEC. I.** That there be, and is hereby, established an institution for the education and training of the feeble-minded children of the commonwealth, under the corporate name and title of "The Commissioners of the Kentucky Institution for the Education and Training of Feeble-minded Children."

**SEC. II.** The commissioners of said institution, to be appointed as hereinafter directed, shall be, and they are hereby, constituted a body corporate, with all the usual powers of a corporation, necessary to carry out the objects of the establishment of said institution, with perpetual succession; with authority to purchase and hold, or to rent or hire, or to receive by gift or bequest, property, real or personal, for the objects of said institution; with capacity of contracting and of being contracted with, of suing and being sued, of pleading and being impleaded, and of using a common seal, and of altering the same at pleasure.

### 1861.
*December 23.*

*[Trespasses to growing crops, fruits, poultry, etc., punished.]*

AN ACT to punish certain trespasses in Jefferson, Scott, Mason, Kenton, Campbell, and Woodford counties.

Whereas, Great injury is oftentimes inflicted on divers citizens of Jefferson, Scott, Mason, Kenton, Campbell, and Woodford counties, by trespasses committed on their gardens, orchards, vineyards, and poultry yards, by persons injuring and carrying off the products of the same; and in order to prevent the evils and injuries arising therefrom,

**SEC. I.** That hereafter, if any person or persons shall unlawfully take, destroy, or carry away any corn, hay, shucks, provender, small grain, field or garden vegetables, or anything of value less than four dollars, which can or may hereafter be grown upon land, or any poultry, or domestic animal of any description whatever, of less value than four dollars, he or they shall be fined not less than five, nor more than fifty dollars for each offense, to be recovered by warrant, in the name of the commonwealth, before any justice of the peace, or county judge of the county in which such offense may be committed, or by indictment in the circuit court of the county.

**SEC. II.** That any person or persons who shall, without the permission of the owner, or tenant of land, pass through or over any growing crop, he or they shall be subject to a like fine, recoverable in like manner as in preceding section set forth.

AN ACT to prevent the careless or wanton or malicious use of deadly weapons.

**SEC. I.** That the widow and minor child or children (or either or any of them) of a person killed by the careless or wanton or malicious use of fire-arms, or other deadly weapons, not in self-defense, may have an action against the person or persons who committed the killing, and all others aiding or promoting the killing, or any one or more of them, for reparation of the injury; and in such action the jury may give vindictive damages.

# TITLE XX.

## CHAMPERTY.

1.  *Conveyances by tenants in common are within champerty act.*—Conveyances made by tenants in common and co-parceners of their interest in lands in the adverse possession of their co-tenants, are within the purview of the champerty act of 1824. *Wall v. Wayland*, 2 Met. 155; *Barret v. Coburn*, 3 Met. 510.

2.  *Adverse possession to be established by unequivocal proof.*—But in such case the adverse possession of the co-tenant at the date of the conveyance, must be established by proof of such specific and unequivocal acts of renunciation of allegiance, as amount to an ouster of the co-tenant. *Ib.*

3.  *Auditor's deed not champertous.*—The auditor's deed releasing to a widow all the right, title and interest of the state in a lot of which her husband died seized, without heirs capable of inheriting, made in pursuance of a special act of the legislature, passes the title of the state to the widow, although the land was adversely held at its date. *White v. White*, 2 Met. 185.

4.  *The same.*—And this, although the act of the legislature under which the deed was made, contained a proviso that nothing in it contained should affect any right which any other person might lawfully have in the premises. Such a proviso can not be invoked to protect the possession of one having no lawful right to the property. *Ib.*

5.  *Evidence of adverse holding in cases between tenants in common.*—Stronger evidence is required to show an adverse holding by a tenant in common, as against his co-tenant than by a stranger; but when the adverse holding is established, the same rule applies to co-tenants as to strangers. *Barret v. Coburn*, 3 Met. 510.

6.  *Character of adverse holding.*—A possession, adverse under the statute of limitation, may not be so under the champerty act. *Ib.*

7.  *Adverse possessor estopped to deny grantor's title.*—Where the party holding the adverse possession is estopped to deny the title of the grantor in the deed made during such adverse possession, such deed will not be champertous. *Ib.*

8.  *The same.*—The plaintiff and defendant each claimed the exclusive right to the whole of a tract of land in controversy. The plaintiff recovered an undivided moiety, and the sheriff, under the writ of possession, turned the defendant out and put the plaintiff in the exclusive possession, who claimed and held adversely to defendant: *Held*—that a conveyance by

# TITLE LVII.
## FERRIES.
### 1865.
*March 4.*

*[Penalty for taking illegal ferriage or failing to transport in reasonable time.]*

AN ACT to prescribe punishments for abuses at ferries.

**Penalty for over charge of ferriage**
SEC. I. That if the proprietor or keeper of any ferry, or his servant or agent, shall demand or take from any person a greater sum for ferriage than is allowed by the order of the court fixing the rates of toll at his ferry, he and the owner of such ferry shall forfeit and pay the sum of twenty-five dollars to each person so overcharged; and after the first forfeiture and conviction, they shall, for every like offense, forfeit and pay the sum of one hundred dollars to the person overcharged.

**Penalty for failing or refusing to run ferry.**
SEC. II. That if any keeper of a ferry shall, in person or by agents, fail or refuse to put any person immediately across at their ferry, without a reasonable excuse therefor, such owner or keeper shall forfeit and pay to each person aggrieved the sum of twenty-five dollars for each offense; and for every like offense committed after the first forfeiture and conviction, they shall forfeit and pay to the person aggrieved the sum of one hundred dollars: *Provided*, That nothing herein shall be construed as depriving the proprietor or keeper, or his agent, of the right to delay a reasonable time between trips.

**Penalties, how enforced.**
SEC. III. That the forfeitures denounced in the first and second sections of this act may be recovered by the person aggrieved by ordinary action, in any court having jurisdiction to render judgment for the sum claimed to be forfeited, which judgment may be enforced by writ of *fieri facias* or *capias ad satisfaciendum*, at the election of the plaintiff.

1. *Alien wife*.—Naturalization being a personal privilege, the alien wife
does not become a citizen by the naturalization of her husband. *White v.
White, 2 Met. 185.*

2. *Alien can not inherit except under statute*.—By the common law, aliens
can not inherit. This law is in force here, except as modified by statute.
*White v. White, 2 Met. 185.*

3. *Statute of 1800—two years' residence necessary*.—To inherit land under
the act of 1800, the alien must have resided in the state two years, and be a
resident thereof at the death of the ancestor. *Ib.*

4. *Preparatory oath, alien can not inherit by taking*.—Taking the pre-
paratory oath does not render an alien a citizen of the United States, or en-
able him to take by decent, nor will his subsequent naturalization vest him
with his ancestor's title which had in the meantime passed to the common-
wealth. *Ib.*

5. *Alien wife can not inherit or take dower*.—The law in force before the
adoption of the Revised Statutes, did not permit the alien wife to inherit
from her husband, or to take dower in his lands. *Ib.*

6. *How alien may receive and hold real estate*.—An alien friend, who has
resided in this state two years, may receive, hold, and pass any right to land
within the commonwealth during the continuance of his residence after that
period. *Yeaker v. Yeaker, 4 Met. 33.*

# TITLE LIX.

## FORCIBLE ENTRY AND DETAINER.

---

1. *Right of entry*.—The statute relating to forcible entries and detainers does not affect the common law right of entry when exercised in a peaceable manner, except so far as the statute itself furnishes a remedy to regain the possession. A person having a right to enter, who makes entry in pursuance of that right, can only be removed by a writ of forcible entry and detainer; not by an action in which *the right of possession* is in issue. *Tucker v. Phillips*, 2 Met. 416.

2. *Writ: who may issue.* A writ of forcible detainer can only be issued by a justice of the peace. Such a writ issued by a judge of the county court, together with all proceedings under it, are void. *Johnson v. Erwin*, 3 Met. 251. (But see title COUNTY JUDGE, 2.)

3. *Law of, a substantial re-enactment of old law.*—The provisions of the Civil Code regulating proceedings of forcible entry and detainer, are a substantial re-enactment of the previous act of 1810. *Belcher v. Barret*, 4 Met. 307.

4. *No judgment upon verdict.*—That the magistrate failed to render a judgment upon a verdict of the jury in the country is no ground for dismissing the traverse in the circuit court. Such judgment not necessary to enable party to maintain a traverse. *Ib.*

5. *What to be tried on traverse.*—Upon a traverse, the truth of the inquisition in the country is the only matter involved in the issue to be tried by the jury in the circuit court. *Ib.*

6. *Sufficiency of verdict.*—Upon a trial in the country the verdict was: "the defendant not guilty:" *Held*—sufficient, and that a traverse thereof could not be maintained. *Ib.*

7. *The same.* Strict technical precision and regularity is not required in proceedings in the country for forcible entries and detainers. *Ib.*

1. *What constitutes under act of 1856.*—To bring an assignment, sale or transfer within the operation of the act of 1856, two things are essential: (1) the act must be done in contemplation of insolvency: (2) and with intent to prefer one or more creditors to the exclusion in whole or part of the others. *Hampton v. Morris*, 2 Met. 336; *Millett v. Pattinger*, 4 Met. 213.

2. *Providing pro rata payment not within.*—Two partners convey all their property to trustees, which the latter are to apply to the payment of their individual and firm debts, and if not sufficient to pay all, then the money to be divided *pro rata* among all their creditors: *Held*—not within the statute of 1856. *Hampton v. Morris*, 2 Met. 336.

3. *No advantage to be acquired by creditors over each other.*—By an act which amounts to an assignment under the law of 1856, the creditors become invested with equal rights in the property of the debtor, and neither can acquire any advantage over the other by attachments of the property. *Shouse v. Utterback*, 2 Met. 52; *Given v. Gordon*, 3 Met. 538.

4. *Assignment of note to secure sureties.*—A person aware of his insolvency, assigned a note to his sureties as an indemnity to them for a pre-existing liability, reciting that it was "to pay in part a note to M. B., who holds a note on me and they [*i. e.* the assignees of the note, my] security:" *Held*—to be within the act of 1856. *Corn v. Simms*, 3 Met. 391.

5. *Same.*—That the assignment *supra* was made in pursuance of a previous promise to the sureties to indemnify them in their suretyship, unless it was of a character to invest the sureties with an equitable right to the fund, would make no difference. *Ib*.

6. *Pre-existing liens.*—Where a transaction amounts to an assignment for the benefit of creditors under the act of 1856, the creditors take the property subject to all valid equities and incumbrances existing at the time the transfer takes place. *Ib*.

7. *Sales contemplated by statute, not void.*—The act of 1856 does not declare the sales, conveyances, etc., mentioned therein to be void, but declares that they shall inure to the benefit of all the creditors of the person making such sale. *Given & Co. v. Gordon*, 3 Met. 538.

8. *Authorizing agent to sell debtor's property.*—Where a person owing more than he was able to pay, executed a power of attorney to one of his creditors whom he owed largely, authorizing him to sell his property of every description, collect debts due him, and pay debts owing by him, and transact

9. *Pre-existing laws not repealed*.—The act of 1856 does not repeal the pre-existing acts against fraudulent conveyances. They embrace distinct classes of cases and provide appropriate remedies for each. *Millett v. Patteringer*, 4 Met. 213.

10. *Same*.—The act of 1856 does not prohibit or afford a remedy for, conveyances that are *merely* fraudulent, although it prohibits the preferences allowed by the pre-existing law. *Ib*.

11. *A case within the act of 1856*.—A debtor owed $12,000, and had property worth $6,000. He conveyed part of his property to one of his creditors in payment of $1,000 of his debt. The residue he conveyed to his sureties in consideration of their agreeing to pay $5,020 for which they were bound: *Held*—that it was within the act of 1856. *Story v. Graham*, 4 Met. 319.

12. *Must sue within six months*.—To cause a sale, mortgage or assignment to inure to the benefit of all the creditors, a petition in equity must be filed for that purpose within six months after the transfer is recorded. The benefit of the act can not be obtained by any proceeding under execution or upon motions upon bonds given to suspend sale under execution. *Wintersmith v. Painter*, 2 Met. 457; *Given & Co. v. Gordon*, 3 Met. 538.

13. *Voluntary conveyance—how far void*.—A voluntary conveyance is void as to the debts of the grantor existing before the conveyance; but not on that account alone as to debts contracted by him afterward. *Todd v. Hartley*, 2 Met. 206.

14. *Part of debt created before and part after conveyance*.—Where a part of the demand was created before and a part after the voluntary conveyance, the conveyance, if not intended to be fraudulent, will be set aside only as to so much of the debt as was created before the conveyance. *Ib*.

I.** That the presidents of the various local boards in the state shall furnish a full and complete statement of the condition of the roads under their respective charges in which the state is a stockholder—their expenditures, receipts, and dividends—semi-annually, as now required by law to be made to the auditor of the state, which report shall be forwarded to the chairman of the state board at Frankfort; the report shall be made out according to a form which shall be furnished by the board of internal improvement for the state; and upon their failure to comply with the provisions of this act, the president of the local boards thus failing shall be subject to be removed from said office by the chairman of the board of internal improvement for the state.

Form of report.

—For an act taxing turnpike companies in aid of the sinking fund, see SINKING FUND, 10.

1. *Sale of road and franchise.*—The legislature have the constitutional power to authorize the sale of "the road, right of way, franchises, and all property real and personal" of a turnpike company, "upon the application of any creditor of the company whose debt may be due and unsatisfied," and to direct the proceeds to be divided *pro rata* among all the creditors. *Lou., etc., T. Co. v. Ballard, 2 Met. 165.*

2. *The same: parties to suit.*—In an action to subject the road and franchises of a turnpike company to sale if the stockholders are necessary parties, and are numerous, one or more may defend for all. *Ib.*

3. *Land over which road passes: who owns.*—The condemnation of land for a turnpike road only vests in the company the right to use the land for the purposes of their road. The company own the road, but not the ground over which it passes. *Kelly v. Donahoe, 2 Met. 482.*

4. *Proprietor's rights in such land.*—The proprietor retains his exclusive right to the land, and to all mines, quarries, springs of water, etc., and the right to use the same for every purpose not inconsistent with the right of way and the rights and franchises of the corporation. *Ib.*

# TITLE LXI.
## FRAUDS—STATUTE OF.

I. CONTRACTS RELATING TO LAND OR INTEREST THEREIN.
II. SALE OF CROPS AND TREES BEFORE SEVERANCE.
III. AGREEMENT TO PAY DEBT OF ANOTHER.
IV. DEBTS CONTRACTED DURING INFANCY. RATIFICATION.
V. TRUSTS AFFECTED BY THE STATUTE.
VI. HOW THE STATUTE RELIED ON.

### I. Contracts relating to land or interests therein.

1. *Verbal grant of right of way.*—As a right of way is an interest in land, a verbal contract that it shall be granted, can not be enforced. *Hall v. McLeod*, 2 Met. 98.
2. *Agreements to buy land.*—Though the statute speaks only of contracts to sell land, a contract by which one agrees to buy land for another is equally within its operation. *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.
3. *Contracts within, valid for some purposes.*—The statute declares that no action shall be brought on a verbal contract for the sale of land, not that the contract is void. Such contracts are valid for some purposes, as for the defense of a possession acquired in good faith under them. *Harrow v. Johnson*, 3 Met. 583.

### II. Sales of the product of realty before severance.

4. *Standing trees.*—A sale of standing trees, in contemplation of their immediate severance from the soil, is a constructive severance, and they pass as chattels, and the contract, though verbal, is not within the statute. *Byassee v. Reese*, 4 Met. 372.
5. *Same.*—The expression "in contemplation of immediate severance from the soil," is used to distinguish a sale of growing crops or standing trees, which pass no interest in the soil beyond a license to enter and take them, from a contract passing the exclusive right to the land for a period for the purpose of enabling a party to make a profit out of the growth upon it. *Ib.*
6. *Sale of the land before severance.*—If land be sold to a *bona fide* purchaser for a valuable consideration paid before notice of a sale of the trees growing thereon, and before they are severed, the trees would pass with the land to the party purchasing it. *Ib.*

## USURY.

—For an act limiting the time to sue for usury paid, to one year, see LIMITATION, 2.

1. *Party may recover though he failed to set it up when sued.*—Where a party is sued on a debt containing usury, and the defense is known to him but he fails to set it up and judgment goes against him which he pays, he may afterward bring a district action and recover the usury from the party receiving it. *Chinn v. Mitchell*, 2 Met. 92.

2. *Principal not responsible for usury paid by agent.*—If the obligor in a note authorize the obligee to raise money to pay the same, and the latter do so, paying usurious interest for the sum raised, he can not recover the usury so paid from the obligor, although the obligor authorized it to be paid or sanctioned its payment after it had been done. *Totten v. Cooke*, 2 Met. 275.

3. *Recovery after judgment by default.*—A defendant who has suffered judgment by default for a debt embracing usury, and has paid the same, may nevertheless maintain an action for the recovery of the usury although he knew that the usury was embraced in the judgment at the time it was rendered. *Ross v. Ross*, 3 Met. 274.

### III. Agreement to pay the debt of another.

7. *Upon the creditor's releasing his attachment.*—Where a creditor had secured his debt by an attachment, and a third party verbally agreed with him, that if he would release the attachment and take fifty cents in the dollar for his debt, he (the third party) would execute his note therefor, including the cost of the attachment, and the plaintiff assented thereto, and dismissed the proceeding and released the property, it was *held*—that the agreement was against the statute, and not enforcible. *Leiber v. Levy*, 3 Met. 292.

### IV. Debts contracted during infancy. Ratification.

8. *To what cases statute applies.*—The statute which provides that no action shall be brought to charge any person upon a promise to pay a debt contracted during infancy, or a ratification of a contract or promise made during infancy, unless the promise or ratification, or some memorandum or note thereof be in writing, applies to all cases in which the plaintiff relies upon a promise or ratification such as the statute refers to, in support of his action, whether he sues upon the promise, or proves it in avoidance of the plea of infancy. *Stern v. Freeman*, 4 Met. 309.

9. *The same*—*applies only to promises to pay debts.*—The statute applies to every express promise to pay a debt contracted during infancy, but not to promises to perform any other contract made during infancy, unless such promise is embraced in the word "ratification." *Argu. Ib.*

10. *Ratification*—*what amounts to.*—A writing showing that the defendant has performed an act of ratification is as effectual as one containing an express ratification. *Ib.*

11. *The same.*—A writing addressed to another than the plaintiff, although it contains no promise to pay, may be used as evidence of a ratification previously made by the defendant, and is entitled to the same weight as if it had been addressed to the plaintiff. *Ib.*

12. *The same.*—An infant bought out his partner's interest in a mercantile firm. After he attained his majority, he retained possession of the property and dealt with it as his own, and wrote a letter to one of the debtors of the firm, stating that he "had the collecting of the debts due the firm, and that he was going on in the same business in his own name." In an action on a note given by the infant in part payment of his partner's interest: *Held*—that the letter showed a sufficient ratification in writing, and that he must pay the note. *Ib.*

FRAUDS—STATUTE OF.
[TITLE

the use, loan, or reservation be declared by will or deed in writing proved and recorded. *Kaye v. Tydings*, 3 Met. 527.

14. *Trusts created by foreign will*.—The act *supra* applies to property acquired and held under foreign wills and decrees for the use of another than the possessor. *Ib*.

15. *Trust property brought here from another state*.—Where by a foreign will and decree, slaves were vested in one for the use of another, and the trustee removed them to this state, and retained them in his possession more than five years, without causing the will and decree creating the trust to be recorded here: *Held*—that the slaves were subject to the debts of the trustee. *Ib*.

### VI. *How the statute relied on*.

16. *Statute not pleaded*.—Although the statute be not pleaded, yet if the answer deny the making of the contract, it has the same effect. *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.

17. *No defense made*.—A judgment can not be given on a contract embraced by the statute of frauds, even if no defense be made. *Ib*.

18. *Agreement presumed verbal, when*.—An allegation in a petition that the parties made "an agreement," without saying it was in writing, and without exhibiting any writing, will be construed to mean a verbal agreement. *Ib*.

19. *Plea of statute—sufficiency of*.—The defendant's answer stated that at the time of the execution of the note sued on, he was an infant under twenty-one years of age: *Held*—sufficient; and that it was not necessary to aver that the note was voidable. *Stern v. Freeman*, 4 Met. 309.

20. *Ratification may be proved without pleading it*.—In an action on a promissory note, the defendant relied upon his infancy in avoidance. The plaintiff may prove a ratification without averring it in his pleadings. *Ib*.

1865.

*[Duty of tax payer to pay to sheriff at county seat.]*

AN ACT to amend the revenue laws of this commonwealth.

**SEC. I.** That from and after the passage of this act, it shall be the duty of the tax payers of this commonwealth to pay their taxes to the sheriff at the county seat of their respective counties, or such other place as he may designate by notice given, between the first day of June and the fifteenth of December of each year.

**SEC. II.** The sheriff shall keep an office at or near the court house of his county, and shall, by himself or deputy, attend at said office every day, from the first of June to the first of October to receive the taxes: *Providing*, That in the county of Kenton he shall keep an office, by himself or deputy, at Independence and in the city of Covington; and in the county of Campbell he shall keep an office, by himself or deputy, at Alexandria and in the city of Newport.

**SEC. III.** That if any tax payer shall fail or refuse to pay his taxes, as above provided, the sheriff is hereby authorized to collect from such delinquent ten per cent. on the amount of his tax due the state, which shall be retained by the sheriff as additional compensation.

**SEC. IV.** The sheriff shall post up not less than three printed public notices in each election precinct in his county, at the most public places in said precinct, notifying the tax payers at least thirty days before said tax is due, and where they are required to pay the same; and, in case he fails to post up said notice as aforesaid, he shall not be allowed the ten per cent. on the tax due by the tax payer.

**SEC. V.** This act shall remain in force for two years from its passage.

is my wish that all the notes and accounts found among my papers (vs.) my brothers should be destroyed or handed over to them. Should I never return, I also desire that each of them should have $200.00 in addition, and the remainder of my property divided equally between the heirs of T. and J. M., at their respective ages of eighteen, should it be deemed judicious to do so:" *Held*—that the words "should I never return," do not apply to the whole will, but to the clause in reference to the notes; that the acts and declarations of the testator after his return, recognizing the paper as his will, are admissible, and that the paper must be established as his will. *Massie v. Griffin*, 2 Met. 364.

2. *Wills may be made dependent on contingency.*—A will or codicil may be entirely dependent on a contingency so as to have no effect as a will or codicil, unless the contingency happen. *Maxwell v. Maxwell*, 3 Met. 101.

3. *Case of contingent will.*—M., while absent from Kentucky, wrote to his wife, "If I never get back home, I leave you every thing I have in the world. The property I got from my first wife I wish you to return every thing to her father." He returned home and died: *Held*—that as the contingency did not happen, the paper was not his will. *Ib.*

4. *Same—parol declarations.*—The parol declarations of the testator after his return, that he had written such a paper, that he still had it, and it was his will, and he intended that his property should go as therein directed, are not sufficient to give effect to the paper as a will. *Ib.*

5. *Case of contingent will.*—"As I intend starting in a few days for the State of Missouri, and should any thing happen that I should not return alive, my wish is that all my lands," etc. [devising his estate]. The writer of the paper made the trip and returned to Kentucky, and died: *Held*—that the paper is contingent and inoperative as a will. *Dougherty v. Dougherty*, 4 Met. 25.

6. *The same—re-publication.*—At the date of the paper *supra* the writer addressed two notes to J. M. L., asserting that the paper was his last will, and that if he did not return home he wished J. M. L. to have the paper recorded, but did not deliver the notes or paper to J. M. L. at that time. After his return from Missouri, he handed the three papers to J. M. L., declaring the one in question to be his will, and that he desired it to be recorded as such: *Held*—not sufficient to establish a re-execution or re-publication of the will. *Ib.*

7. *Unfinished paper.*—The presumption is always against a paper which bears self-evident marks of being unfinished, and it behooves those who propound it, as a will, to show by unequivocal proof that the testator subsequently adopted it as his will in its unfinished state. *Jones v. Jones*, 3 Met. 266.

8. *The same.*—A paper offered as the will of one who died in 1854, was wholly in his handwriting, with his name written in the beginning of it, but it was not dated, and contained intrinsic evidence of its unfinished state, and the name of the deceased was not subscribed at its close: *Held*—that the propounders must prove, not only that it was written before the Revised Statutes took effect, but that the deceased, previous to that time, intended it in its actual condition to operate as his will. *Ib.*

—For an act providing that all forfeitures and penalties for gaming heretofore given to informers are to go to the school fund, see SCHOOLS AND SEMINARIES, 2.

1. *Note for money loaned to bet.*—Where a part of the consideration for which a note was executed was money loaned for the purpose of gaming, the whole contract is void and the note not obligatory. *Collins v. Merrill*, 2 Met. 163.
2. *Same—loan in another state.*—Though the money be loaned in another state for the purpose of gaming there, the note, executed in this state is void. *Ib.*
3. *Suit for lost stake.*—The loser and his creditors have the *exclusive* right to sue for and recover the lost stake for six months. After that any person (including the loser), may sue and recover within five years, the one first suing to have the preference. *Barnes v. Turner*, 4 Met. 114.
4. *Amount recoverable.*—The loser and his creditors can recover only the amount lost, to be for their sole benefit. But if another sues he recovers treble the amount lost, one half to go to the commonwealth. *Ib.*
5. *Compromise of lost stake.*—The loser may adjust the loss with the winner if done in good faith and before any other person has commenced suit for the penalty. *Ib.*

## II. *Signing, witnessing, and publishing will.*

9. *"Signing in presence of testator."*—A literal adherence to the words of the statute requiring that the witnesses "shall subscribe the will with their names in the presence of the testator," is never exacted, and a substantial conformity with the spirit of the statute is all that reason and sound policy require. *Montgomery v. Perkins*, 2 Met. 448.

10. *The same—marksmen.*—Accordingly where the attesting witnesses were unable to write, and their names were written by another, each of them holding the pen as the draftsman wrote their names: *Held*—sufficient. *Ib.*

11. *Re-execution of revoked will.*—A re-execution of a revoked will can only be effected by such acts of the maker as are equivalent to an original execution. Parol acts and declarations are not sufficient. *Maxwell v. Maxwell*, 3 Met. 101; *Dougherty v. Dougherty*, 4 Met. 25.

12. *How subscribed by testator.*—Since the 1st of July, 1852, when the Revised Statutes took effect, an instrument can have no effect as a will unless the testator subscribe his name at the close of the writing. A will written before that period depends upon the law then in force for its validity. *Jones v. Jones*, 3 Met. 266.

13. *The same—testator marksmen.*—After testator's name had been subscribed to the writing, he acknowledged it to be his will in the presence of two witnesses, who subscribed as such in his presence. The testator then made his mark to the will between his Christian and sir-name: *Held*—a sufficient publication of the instrument as a will, and that placing the mark to it was unnecessary. *Sechrist v. Edwards*, 4 Met. 163.

14. *Testator may subscribe after witnesses.*—It is immaterial whether the names of the attesting witnesses or that of the testator be first subscribed to the will, if the witnesses were present when the testator wrote his name, or acknowledged it to be his signature, and being called on for that purpose, actually witnessed or attested that fact. *Ib.*

15. *Revised Statutes same as act of 1797.*—The provision in regard to the attestation of wills in the statute of 1797, is substantially the same as that of section five, chapter one hundred and six, Revised Statutes. *Ib.*

## III. *Probating and contesting wills.*

16. *Proof of identity of will.*—Two subscribing witnesses who were unable to read or write, and who had signed with a mark, expressed the opinion, upon hearing the paper in contest read, that one of its provisions differed from the provisions of the paper they heard read and attested. The draftsman testified that he wrote the paper in contest, and that it was the identical paper which was executed by the testator and attested by the two witnesses aforesaid: *Held*—to be sufficient proof of the identity of the instrument. *Montgomery v. Perkins*, 2 Met. 448.

17. *Wills proved before Revised Statutes, how contested.*—Proceedings to contest wills admitted to probate before the Revised Statutes took effect, should be in accordance with the eleventh section of the act of 1797, and

### 1861.

AN ACT to compel the attendance of absent members of the general assembly.

**SEC. I.** That if, at any time, when the general assembly is in session, there should be less than a quorum in either or both branches of the legislature, the speakers of the two houses, if present, and if either or both speakers are not present, two members of the senate and three of the house, shall have power to adjourn from day to day, and shall be authorized to compel the attendance of absent members.

**SEC. II.** The speakers of the two houses shall have power, by proclamation, to warn in the absent members by a named day, and upon the failure of any absent member to attend on the day designated, to issue their warrants to the sergeant-at-arms of either or both houses, and to such sheriffs, constables or jailers as they shall see fit, to arrest and bring to the capitol the absent members; in case the speaker of the house is not present, three members may sign the warrants in his stead; and in case the speaker of the senate is not present, two members of the senate may sign the warrants in his stead; and in case both speakers are not present, the warrants shall be signed by three members of the house and two of the senate.

**SEC. III.** It shall be the duty of the sergeant-at-arms, all sheriffs, constables, and jailers, to execute said warrants; and if they fail or refuse to do so, they shall be fined in a sum not less than one hundred dollars nor more than one thousand dollars; and the officer executing such warrants shall receive as his fee and for mileage the same sum that sheriffs are entitled to for like services.

18. *Testator presumed to know contents of will.*—The execution of a paper as a will by the testator, with the requisite solemnities, is presumptive evidence that he knew its contents, and that it conformed to his intentions, and it is incumbent on those who seek to avoid it on the ground that it is not in conformity to his intention, to establish the fact by proof, *aliunde*. *Secrest v. Edwards*, 4 Met. 163.

19. *Same.*—A testator, who was illiterate and could not read, furnished the draftsman with a previous will which he had made, and directed him to write his will like that one, except that he should omit the lands, as they had been deeded. The draftsman did so, and as he wrote each clause, read it aloud to the testator who approved what was written. In this way the whole instrument was read to, and approved by him: *Held*—sufficient to show the testator knew the contents of the will. *Ib*.

20. *Proof of sound mind.*—Though subscribing witnesses to a will testify that at the time the instrument was published the testator was not of sound mind, still his capacity may be established by other sufficient evidence. *Ib*.

21. *Court of appeals tries law and fact.*—In cases of contested wills the court of appeals is made the trier of the facts certified from the circuit court, without reference to, and wholly independent of the finding of the jury, and by applying the law to the facts of which the court is made the sole trier, it determines whether the testamentary paper should be admitted to probate or rejected. *Ib*.

22. *Mandate to circuit court.*—The circuit court after a trial in the court of appeals can only enter the mandate from the latter court, with directions to the county court to make such orders as are proper to carry out the judgment of the court of appeals. *Ib*.

23. *Trustee under will competent to prove its execution.*—A person created a trustee by a will, but having no beneficial interest, is competent to prove facts necessary to establish the will. *Montgomery v. Perkins*, 2 Met. 448.

## IV. Construction and effect of will.

24. *Mistakes in will.*—Courts of equity have jurisdiction to correct mistakes in wills when they are apparent on the face of the will. But the mistake must be apparent on the face of the will, or susceptible of being made out by a proper construction of its terms, otherwise there can be no relief. Parol evidence, though admissible to remove a latent ambiguity, is not admissible to vary or control the terms of the will. *Jackson v. Payne*, 2 Met. 567.

25. *Parol evidence to explain.*—Parol evidence is, in general, inadmissible for the purpose of explaining, contradicting or adding to the contents of a will. Its language must be interpreted according to its proper signification, or with as near an approach thereto as the body of the instrument and the state of the circumstances at the time of its execution will admit. *Ib*.

**SEC. IV.** The members so arrested shall pay all the cost of his arrest, to be retained out of his per diem, if it shall be so much; and if not, to be recovered as other debts due to the commonwealth; and the members so arrested and brought to the capitol shall pay such other sums above the cost of his arrest as the house of which he is a member shall impose as a fine: *Provided, however*, That either house shall, for good cause shown, relieve their members so arrested from the penalties imposed by this section.

**SEC. V.** This act to take effect from its passage.

1. *Power to enact laws.*—The legislature may enact any law not forbidden by the national or state constitution, either in express terms or by necessary implication. *Johnson v. Higgins*, 3 Met. 566.
2. *On what day law takes effect.*—An act which declares that it shall take effect from its passage, is in force during the whole day on which it is approved by the governor. *Malory v. Hiles*, 4 Met. 53.
3. *Is in force the whole day.*—The act of 1861 suspending the courts was approved on the 24th day of May, and on that day the circuit court rendered a judgment. It did not appear at what hour the act was signed or the judgment rendered: *Held*—that the judgment was improper, the law being in force the whole of the day. *Ib.*
4. *Members not exempt from process.*—A member of the legislature in attendance on its sessions, is not privileged against service of process against him in a civil action. *Johnson v. Offutt*, 4 Met. 19.
5. *Extending session—effect.*—When the general assembly by a vote of two thirds of all the members elected to each house extends the session beyond sixty days, the constitutional restriction as to the length of the session is removed, and the assembly may thenceforward protract the session from time to time by the concurrence of a majority simply. *Speed & Worthington v. Crawford*, 3 Met. 207.

There being nothing in the will to indicate the existence of a mistake, or an intention to make any other provision to remedy an inequality alleged by one of the legatees to exist, and which it was alleged the testator intended to remedy: *Held*—that the express terms of the will can not be contradicted or explained by parol testimony, and that a court of equity has no power to modify it. *Ib.*

27. *Future estate*—*no disposition of intermediate term*—*heirs take it.*—A testator devised certain lands to his wife for her life, but provided that if she married, her estate in the lands should cease. He then gives the land "herein devised to his beloved wife *during her natural life, after her death*, to be equally divided" among certain persons, "or such of them as may be living at the time of her death." There was no residuary clause in the will. The widow married: *Held*—that the heirs at law are entitled to the land by descent until her death, when it vests in the devisees. *Augustus v. Seabolt*, 3 Met. 155.

28. *General rules for construing wills.*—The intention of the testator is to be gathered from the will itself whenever it is possible to do so. Every word is to have its effect, provided an effect can be given to it not inconsistent with the general intent of the whole will taken together, and no word is to be rejected unless there can not be a rational construction of the will with the word as it is to be found. It is not necessary to take all the words in the order in which they are; the court may transpose them to comply with the intention, but in no case where the words are plain and sensible, is a transposition to be made in order to create a different meaning, much less to let in different devisees and legatees; nor where a former clause is express and particular, shall a subsequent one enlarge it. *Ib.*

29. *Omissions in wills.*—Where a testator overlooks a particular event in disposing of his property, which, had it occurred to him, he would probably have guarded against, the omission will not be supplied by inserting the necessary clause. And though the inference of intention be more or less strong, yet, if not necessary or indubitable, the court will not aid the supposed intention by adding or supplying words. *Ib.*

30. *Vested estate.*—A testator declared in his will: "I also devise to the children of my son, T. J. G., the following slaves (naming them). These slaves and their increase I direct my executor to hire out until the youngest of said children attains the age of twenty-one years, then I direct my executor to sell all the slaves and divide the proceeds equally among said children." One of the legatees (a female) married after the death of the testator, and died before the youngest child attained twenty-one years: *Held*—that the legatees took a vested interest in the proceeds of said slaves, which, in equity must be regarded as a right to money from the death of the testator, and that the husband of the deceased devisee is entitled in her right to her share of the money. The division to take place at the time designated in the will, and the legatees to be entitled to the hire of the slaves. *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.

*[Gipsies to be treated as vagrants, and fined and imprisoned.]*

AN ACT to relieve the state of gipsies.

SEC. I. That for the purpose of ridding the state hereafter of the evil influences resulting from incursions of hordes of gipsies, roaming through the commonwealth without any fixed habitation, or honest and lawful mode of obtaining a livelihood, it shall be the duty of any county judge, justice of the peace, police judge or mayor of any town, city or corporation in the commonwealth upon complaint made by any citizen within the respective jurisdiction of such officers, to issue a warrant of arrest against such gipsies, directed to any ministerial officer of the commonwealth, who shall execute the same, and take the party or parties before the officer issuing such warrant, for trial.

How gipsies may be arrested, and dealt with.

SEC. II. That upon a fair hearing of the case, if it appear to the satisfaction of the officer trying it, that the person or persons apprehended are guilty of vagrancy, and unable to show that they are engaged in some lawful and proper pursuit for the maintenance of themselves and dependents, he or they shall be convicted of an offense against the peace and dignity of this commonwealth, and fined, each, not exceeding fifty dollars, and give good and sufficient surety for behavior in the future; and in default of payment of such fine and giving such surety, he or they shall be confined in the county jail for a period long enough to pay the fine, at the rate of two dollars per day during such confinement.

SEC. III. This act to take effect from and after its passage.

# 1863.
*March 1.*

AN ACT authorizing sheriffs and other collecting officers to attach for the payment of state revenue and county levy.

**SEC. I.** That it shall and may be lawful for the sheriff, or other collecting officer of the public revenue or county levy, in the several counties of this commonwealth, whenever the tax payer fails or refuses to pay the revenue tax or county levy due by him, or is insolvent, to attach any choses in action or debts due to said tax payer within the county, for the payment of said taxes.

**SEC. II.** The sheriff or collecting officer shall give to the person in whose hands said taxes may be attached, written notice of the attachment; the notice shall also warn such person to appear before the presiding judge of the county, at the office of said judge, on a day to be fixed by the sheriff or collecting officer, not less than ten days after the service of the notice, to answer as garnishee; and it shall be the duty of the said judge, in a summary way, without written pleadings, to hear and determine the attachment, as in cases of attachment upon a return of no property found upon an execution; and if he sustains the attachment, to give the sheriff or collecting officer a written order to collect said taxes out of the estate of the person in whose hands the same were attached; and by virtue of which distress may be made as in other cases for the collection of taxes.

**SEC. III.** The service of the notice, as provided for in the second section of this act, shall operate as an attachment and restraining order, and have the same force and effect as other attachments granted under the laws in force upon that subject.

**SEC. IV.** The sheriff or collecting officer, for serving the notice mentioned in the second section of this act, shall be allowed a fee of twenty-five cents, to be paid by the tax payer.

**SEC. V.** This act to take effect from and after its passage.

32. *No provision for debts—how paid.*—In the case *supra* the testator made no provision in his will for the payment of his debts, and the widow as administratrix sought to subject the after-acquired real estate to the debts, and to exonerate the personal property and slaves which were ample for their payment: *Held*—in the absence of any provision for payment of the debts, that the personality and slaves must be first subjected to the debts, in accordance with the statute declaring such estate first liable. *Broadwell v. Broadwell*, 4 Met. 290.

33. *Survivorship—to what period words refer.*—Where a devisee is made to several persons by name, or as a class, with words of survivorship annexed, if the gift is to take effect immediately after testator's death, the uniform rule is to refer the words of survivorship to that event, and to regard them as intended to provide against the death of the devisee in the life time of the testator. *Wren v. Haynes*, 2 Met. 129.

34. *Same.*—Where the word survivor is used in connection with a distribution of the subject of the gift, to be made at some period subsequent to the time of the death of the testator, the word seems naturally and properly to refer to the period of distribution, and the most obvious meaning of the term when thus used is, that the devisees *then* surviving are the persons intended to be embraced by it. *Ib.*

35. *Same—a will construed.*—A will contained this clause: "It is my will and desire that when my youngest child shall arrive at the age of twenty-one years, the $12,000 set apart in bank stock, shall be equally divided among all my *surviving* children *or their heirs*. The will also directs the dividends on the bank stock to be paid to the widow of the testator until the period of distribution: *Held*—that the survivorship refers to the period when the youngest child arrived at twenty-one years of age and not to the death of the testator: *Held also*—that the words "or their heirs" are words of purchase, and that a daughter of the testator, who survived him, having died before the period of distribution, her heirs, and not her husband, took her share. *Ib.*

36. *Vested remainder.*—In 1835 a testatrix devised lands, slaves, and personality to her two nephews in trust for her daughter during life, remainder in fee to her three grand daughters "and their heirs lawfully begotten of their bodies." One of the grand-daughters died in 1853, leaving children. The daughter died in 1856: *Held*—that the grand-daughter took a vested remainder in fee under the will, which having vested prior to the act of 1846, went to her husband as her survivor. *Johnson v. Johnson*, 2 Met. 331.

37. *Devise to wife for a certain purpose.*—A testator devised all his estate to his wife "for her use and benefit during her natural life, that she may be able to raise and educate our little children:" *Held*—that she was not thereby vested with the absolute control, free from charge or restraint, over the profits of the whole estate during her life, but that her life estate has a charge upon it for the benefit of the children, who were to be raised and educated at the death of the testator. *Henderson v. Hayne*, 2 Met. 342.

38. "Children"—*when it means grand-children*.—The word "children" used in a will, will never be enlarged to include grand-children, unless it is indispensably necessary to effectuate the obvious intent of the testator. *Churchill v. Churchill*, 2 Met. 466.

39. *Same*.—Such a construction is admissible only in two cases: *First*, from necessity when the will would otherwise be inoperative: *Secondly*, where the testator has shown by other words that he did not use the word "children," in its ordinary sense, but in a more extended sense. *Ib*.

40. *Defeasible fee*.—A will gave to a devisee a defeasible fee in land and in a slave, subject to divestiture if she should die without issue, upon which event the fee was to vest in her co-devisees or their children, then living. This was an executory devise, and neither the conveyance of the property by the person holding the defeasible fee, nor the possession of the purchaser, however long continued, before the happening of the contingency upon which the executory devise was to take effect, could defeat the right of the executory devisee. *Nunnally v. I. COMPETENCY OF WITNESS.
II. DISCREDITING WITNESSES.

I. *Competency of witness.*

1. *Owner of lost baggage.*—In an action to recover the value of a valise and its contents, which had been placed in the custody of the defendant at his tavern by the plaintiff as his guest, the latter is not a competent witness to prove the contents of the valise or their value. *Packard v. Northcraft*, 2 Met. 439. [But such owner is now competent. See *Ante*, 205.

2. *Trustee under will competent to establish it.*—One who is constituted by a will a mere trustee for one of the devisees, is a competent witness to prove facts necessary to establish the instrument. *Montgomery v. Perkins*, 2 Met. 448.

3. *Interest—parties to suit and to issue.*—Persons are not incompetent merely because they are parties to the action; but if they are parties to the issue then they are incompetent to testify either in their own behalf or in favor of those united with them in the issue; or if they are not parties to the issue, yet if they are interested in it, whether parties to the action or not,

1. *No authority to receive money on replevin bond.*—Circuit court clerks have no authority to receive money on replevin bonds in their office and receipt therefor on the bond, and such a payment does not relieve the obligor in the bond from its payment where the clerk failed to pay the money to the obligee. *Durant v. Gabby*, 2 Met. 91.

2. *Nor upon judgments or executions in their courts.*—Clerks of circuit courts are not authorized by law to receive money upon judgments, executions, or replevin bonds, of which the law makes them the custodians, and a payment to them, unless authorized by the party entitled to the money, or directed by the court having control of the fund, furnishes no protection to the party making it. *Chinn v. Mitchell*, 2 Met. 92.

3. *Nor money paid into court.*—It is no part of the legal or official duty of a circuit court clerk to receive money deposited or paid into court, and the sureties on the clerk's official bond are not liable for the failure of the clerk to pay over money so received. *Hardin v. Carrico*, 3 Met. 289.

4. *Party to issue.*—That a person is a party to an issue of itself renders him incompetent to testify either for himself or on behalf of those united with him in the issue. *Ib.*

5. *Who are parties to the issue.*—Defendants who rely upon the same matters of defense, although by separate answers, are parties to the issue, and one of them can not testify in behalf of the other, even when not incompetent on the score of interest. *Ib.*

6. *Surety competent for principal.*—In an action upon a covenant the surety is competent for the principal, both of whom are sued, to sustain the defense set up by the principal that he was to be bound only on condition that another would execute it, which had not been done. *Millet v. Parker*, 2 Met. 608.

7. *Remote, uncertain, and contingent interest.*—A remote, uncertain, and contingent interest does not render a witness incompetent, but only goes to his credibility. *Ib.*

8. *Parties to bills and notes.*—The payee and indorser of a bill of exchange for whose accommodation the bill was accepted, is not a competent witness for the accommodation acceptor in a suit against such acceptor by the holder of the bill. His liability for the cost to which the latter may be subjected in case he fails in his defense, is a disqualifying interest. *Finnell v. Cox*, 3 Met. 245.

9. *Interest in the issue.*—Section six hundred and seventy of the Code excludes all persons, whether parties to the action or not, who have an interest in the issue, from testifying in favor of that interest, without regard to the amount, except in some cases specially provided for in some of the succeeding sections. *Ib.*

10. *Persons jointly indicted, incompetent for each other.*—Where several persons are jointly indicted, one is not a competent witness for the other, even where they are tried separately. *Cornelius v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 481.

11. *Wife of one jointly indicted with person calling her.*—But this rule, *supra*, does not universally exclude the wife of one defendant as a witness for another where they are tried separately. Accordingly where two were indicted for larceny and embezzlement, the wife of one is a competent witness for the other when they are tried separately. *Ib.*

12. *When wife incompetent.*—The wife is incompetent for one jointly indicted with her husband in three states of the case, to-wit: *First*, Where her testimony, as in case of conspiracy, would tend directly to the acquittal of her husband; *second*, Where, as in case of an assault, the interests of all the defendants are *inseparable*; *third*, Where the *rights* of the husband, though not a party to the prosecution, would be concluded by a verdict therein. *Ib.*

13. *Husband on behalf of wife.*—A husband is incompetent to testify on behalf of his wife. *Kaye v. Tydings*, 3 Met. 527.

14. *Liability for cost disqualifies.*—That a witness is liable to a judg-

## II. Discrediting witness.

15. *Mode of attacking reputation.*—The regular mode of inquiring into the reputation of a witness is to ask those introduced for that purpose, whether they know the general reputation of the person in question among his neighbors, and what that reputation is. The witness must be able to state what is generally said of the person by those among whom he dwells or is conversant. But it is not essential to the admissibility of such statements that they should come from those who are both neighbors and personally acquainted with the party impeached. They are admissible if made either by those who are acquainted with him, or those who dwell in his immediate neighborhood. *Henderson v. Hayne*, 2 Met. 342.

16. *Same: what jury may consider.*—An instruction which confines the jury, in considering the evidence relating to the character of the impeached witness to such statements respecting his general moral character and principles as have been made by those "among whom he dwells, and to whom he is known," is misleading. It is not necessary that it should come from those who are both neighbors and personally acquainted with the party impeached. *Ib.*

17. *When party may contradict his own witness.*—The right of a party to contradict his own witness by showing that "he has made statements different from his present testimony," does not depend upon the ability of the party to prove in addition, that the testimony of the witness is untrue. But he may contradict him: *first*, by other evidence; *secondly*, by showing that he has made statements different from his present testimony. Either or both modes may be adopted. *Champ v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 17.

18. *The same.*—A party introduces a witness to prove certain facts, but the witness deposes that they did not transpire. He can not, by way of discrediting the witness, prove by others that the witness had on other occasions, in their presence, stated the facts in question—in effect to show that the witness had stated out of court facts which he failed to prove in court. *Ib.*

19. *The same.*—Where a witness does not state any fact prejudicial to the party calling him, but only fails to prove facts supposed to be beneficial to the party, it is not a case within the reason or policy of the rule which allows a witness to be contradicted by evidence that he had previously stated the same facts to others. *Ib.*

20. *Testifying falsely.*—An instruction "That it is the province of the jury to decide upon the credibility of each witness introduced in the cause, and if a witness swears *falsely* in relation to one particular fact, in the case, they have a right to disregard every other fact proven by said witness:" *Held*—on the whole, not to be misleading, though the witness must have knowingly testified falsely to a fact material to the case. *Rutherford v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 387.

21. *Interrogatories that tend to disgrace witness.*—A witness is not bound to answer interrogatories which, if affirmatively answered, would subject

2. *Penal statutes strictly construed.*—The statutes providing remedies against constables for failing to return executions are highly penal, and parties who seek to enforce them, must comply strictly with their requirements. *Ib.*

He had filed no inventory of their estates within the time prescribed by law, and had set up title in right of his marriage with their mother, to a portion of the estate claimed by them: *Held*—that the guardian should be removed. *Windsor v. McAtee*, 2 Met. 430.

3. *Suits by guardian*.—A guardian can not sue in his own name for personal property of his ward, unlawfully detained by another. The suit must be in the name of the ward by his guardian or next friend. *Anderson v. Watson*, 3 Met. 509.

4. *Suit by non-resident guardian*.—A foreign guardian desiring to sue for the sale of his ward's land in this state, must first procure an order of the county court of the county where the land lies, authorizing him to act as guardian in this state. *Bell v. Clark*, 2 Met. 573.

5. *Hiring out ward*.—If the hiring out of a ward by his guardian be a breach of his trust or a violation of the rights of his ward, a court of equity would, upon a proper case shown, grant adequate relief. *Clinkinbeard v. Clinkinbeard*, 3 Met. 330.

6. *Who may take ward's earnings*.—A guardian hired out his wards. Their mother, a free colored woman, sued the guardian for their wages: *Held*—that she could not recover. *Ib*.

7. *Ward first paid out of deceased guardian's estate*.—The estate of a ward remaining in the hands of his insolvent guardian at the time of his decease, must be paid before a *pro rata* distribution is made among the general creditors of the deceased. *White v. Carrico*, 2 Met. 232.

8. *Creditor of ward*.—In equity the creditor of a ward may be substituted to his right to sue the estate of the deceased guardian, where the claim of the creditor is such a one as the guardian would be legally bound to pay. *Ib*.

9. *Settlement*—*undue influence over ward*.—A receipt as between parties not standing in fiduciary relations, may be explained by parol. Less effect still will be given to a receipt given by a ward to his guardian upon final settlement, where the minority of the ward has recently ceased, and the dominion of the guardian is still presumed to exist to some extent over his mind. *Clay v. Clay*, 3 Met. 548.

10. *Receipt in full by ward to guardian*.—A receipt for a sum of money in full of all claims upon final settlement between guardian and ward within a month after the latter attained age, and where the ward was nephew to the guardian and had been so situated as not to be acquainted with the management of the estate, will not conclusively bar a re-examination of the guardian's account. *Ib*.

11. *Mingling ward's funds with his own by guardian*.—The equitable doctrine applicable to all trustees, and especially to guardians, is that if they use the trust fund or mingle it with their own, they are liable for interest. *Ib*.

12. *Loaning ward's money without security*.—If the guardian loan his ward's money, taking no security therefor, he can not escape personal responsibility for its loss by showing that the person to whom the loan was

### I. Powers of the legislature—limit of.

1. *Powers unlimited except by constitutions.*—The legislature may enact any law not forbidden by the fundamental law of the land. If the federal or state constitution does not, in express terms, or by necessary implication, forbid the exercise of such power, the enactment must be adjudged valid and enforcible by law—courts will not interpose to arrest its operation, except upon clear and satisfactory grounds. The judiciary can not inquire into the motives and necessities which have superinduced the enactment of the law. *Johnson v. Higgins*, 3 Met. 566.

2. *The same—power to punish offenses.*—There is no provision in the constitution of Kentucky which indicates an intention to deprive the legislature of the power which it possessed, without constitutional grant, to punish a person for challenging, in this state, any one whether a citizen or an alien. *Moody v. Commonwealth*, 4 Met. 1.

### II. Laws in conflict with treaties.

3. *Must give way to treaty.*—By the constitution of the United States, where a treaty made under the authority of the United States conflicts with a law of the state, the law must give way to the extent of its conflict with such treaty. *Yeaker v. Yeaker*, 4 Met. 33.
